text: What is this shit!?! You would think that being the most notorious serial killer of the 21st century would give you a certain amount of respect. And yet they continue to mock me at every turn.... Sure maybe I've killed a small amount of victims, but each one carefully selected and executed, to be made into a MASTERPIECE!!! And yet these fools dub me the "Collage Killer"!! Do they not see the underlying religious symbolism?!? Do they not see the abstract meanings of the rearranged limbs!! My latest kill....compromised, the artistic quality sacrificed to make messages more obvious. More blunt with the loss of finesse....and yet they still do not see!! How do they not understand that the fingers being stuffed in the mouth is a metaphor on how society tries to gag individual expression and dissenting voices....how do they not understand that the shaved head and shattered face laughs in the face of modern beauty!!! > *muffled gagging sounds...* And yet the "Collage Killer"still haunts me! Every time I turn on my television, I have to hear that wretched name!"Collage Killer"this,"Collage Killer"that. Surely an artist such as myself deserves a much more fitting name....such as"The Leonardo Murderer"or even"Murderer B"!!! If I knew that my work wouldn't be shown the proper respect, I would have just bought a pistol like a 2-bit thug!!! But all that changes today...because of you my sweet. You will be my twelfth victim. The masterpiece that will etch my name in history as the best artist that has ever lived...Don't bother struggling cutie, it will only make it hurt more. You'll live forever....as my piece.

A confused protagonist awakens to find a young girl explaining that their past "life" was "heaven." Realizing they are dead, the protagonist, who recalls their life as a personal nightmare, demands answers. The girl reveals they are in Hell, but clarifies it's not a place of physical torment. Instead, Hell is where individuals confront the choices they made, particularly the protagonist's decision to take their own life despite countless opportunities for good. The girl then escorts the protagonist to watch "their show," a display of the alternative lives they "could have been," explaining that child guides like herself are assigned to newcomers because their own lives were too brief.

The leatherback sea turtle population in the Pacific Ocean, which was once the largest and most stable group of the species worldwide, has plummeted by more than 90% since 1980, with fewer than 2,300 adult females estimated to remain. Today, a significant portion of the remaining Pacific leatherbacks nest primarily on Playa Grande, Playa Ventanas, and Playa Langosta along Costa Rica’s northern Pacific coast. During nesting season, female leatherbacks use their powerful rear flippers to excavate holes in the sand and lay an average of 80 rubbery, ping-pong-ball-shaped eggs per clutch, returning to nest up to 12 times in a single season, with 9-15 days between nesting events. After about 60-70 days of incubation, the vulnerable hatchlings emerge, facing long odds of survival.

Both adult leatherbacks and their hatchlings face numerous threats, including increasing sand temperatures and nest inundation due to climate change, entanglement and injury from commercial fishing gear such as longlines and gillnets, collisions with boats, coastal development, light pollution, and illegal poaching of eggs for human consumption. Population declines have been especially severe in Pacific nesting sites due to these overlapping hazards. To develop effective conservation strategies and halt their slide toward extinction, researchers must collect detailed data on leatherback behavior during nesting, physiological adaptation to variable environments, genetic diversity in declining populations, reproductive success rates, and critical migration routes spanning thousands of miles across the Pacific.

Supporting this research means hands-on work in the field: at night when leatherbacks haul onto the beach to nest, you’ll join scientists to attach satellite or radio transmitters to large females to monitor their local beach movements and track transoceanic migrations. You’ll also help carefully transfer eggs from exposed or flood-prone nests—often just meters from the high tide line—into protected hatcheries, documenting each clutch and monitoring for predation or fungal threats. With recent studies estimating that without intervention, the Eastern Pacific leatherback could disappear within the next 10-20 years, every hatchling has heightened significance and every adult is vital to rebuilding the population.

The info in this book could be gotten out of seed catalogs and even some seed catalogs give more info than you get in this book. There is not much info about each tomato. The 2 page spread consists of 1/2 page of info about the tomato in large print and the second page is a huge picture. There is no info about the history of the heirloom tomato. Also, 100 Heirloom tomatores does not scratch the surface of types you will find in the market. I am returning this book.

In a world too often devoid of genuine care, these people are a revelation. Their service isn't merely quick; it's a relentless, almost heroic ballet of efficiency, every friendly face a steadfast bulwark against the usual frustrations of the day.

Their pizzas, oh, they *are* good – a truly masterful creation that almost, *almost*, makes you forget the gnawing ache in your wallet. But then the stark reality crashes down: an extra large garden style, a supposed simple pleasure, demanded over $20. It's not just a price; it's a sacrifice, a profound internal debate about indulgence versus necessity. Yet, as I gripped the box, a silent testament to the evening's cost, I couldn't deny the exquisite taste, nor the small, fleeting comfort of a chilled soda and a quiet waiting area – meager justifications, perhaps, for such a financial tribute.

But then, a glimmer of hope, a potential salvation, ignited a fragile spark: a sign proclaiming gluten-free crust. For those of us navigating the treacherous waters of dietary restrictions, this isn't just an option; it's a lifeline, a promise of inclusion. My heart dared to hope, even as the familiar, cynical voice whispered about the inevitable upcharge. The employee, bless their hopeful soul, believed it wouldn't cost more, but experience has taught me a harsher truth. To finally enjoy a pizza without dread, without the physical repercussions – that would be nothing short of miraculous. Yet, for a pie already demanding such a tribute, would they truly spare us that additional burden? The thought of that extra cost, heaped upon an already significant outlay, feels like a cruel twist, a barrier to a simple, unadulterated pleasure that so many take for granted. It's a gamble, a hope against hope, that this one place might just understand the true value of a choice without compromise.

A lifetime of war. He knew nothing else. At 54, he felt old. He *was* old; most didn't make it this far. No gratitude given. Born into war, it shattered his parents' happy world. They, like everyone, thought it wouldn't touch them. They, like everyone, were wrong. Choices were forced. His father made cowardly ones for his family. He lost anyway. Yet, war also brought surprising good. His brother chasing him in meadows. The first fresh bread in months, shared by all. And Maria. She entered his desperate life, sudden as her vanishing. Blood poured from his chest and stomach. He knew he was finally nearing her. A stranger's bullets: the best gift. A peaceful smile. He thought: 'The dreams in which I'm dying are the best I've ever had...'

Hello! Your writing style is very good: clear and evocative without being overly descriptive or wordy. That said, this story's constant jumping around makes it difficult to get into. If not for your blurb, I would have been pretty lost. Even armed with that background info, I found myself having to backtrack in the text to try to figure out what was going on. You might consider, after the contest, giving it a critical look to make sure it's more understandable. I did think Ian was a good AI character, with nice dialogue and a personality of his own. When he starts melting down and "yelling" at David it's very effective and immediately pulls the reader in.(You might consider renaming David, though--using that name is super reminiscent of HAL 9000, the ultimate evil AI.) Nice writing and good luck!

Jack Grealish stared at his reflection in the bathroom mirror of the Manchester nightclub, the bass from the music downstairs vibrating through the walls. His phone buzzed with missed calls from his agent. He knew he shouldn't be here - not after that humiliating 4-0 defeat at Everton just hours earlier. The taste of defeat was still bitter in his mouth, but somehow the prospect of facing his disappointed teammates tomorrow felt worse than drowning his sorrows in the pulsing anonymity of the dance floor.

Three days later, he found himself jogging alongside 18-year-olds on the training pitch, demoted to the Under-21s like a schoolboy sent to detention. Each sprint felt like a reminder of his poor judgment, each drill a step toward earning back the trust he'd squandered in one reckless night.

The 20-year-old midfielder had to train with Villa's Under-21s after reports he visited a Manchester nightclub after a 4-0 defeat at Everton.
He has since been restored to the first team set-up and is in contention to face Arsenal on Sunday.
"He is a talented young player but only Jack knows if he has learned," said Frenchman Garde.
"I'll still be close with him. I have the feeling Jack needs to be more disciplined, this way I feel he can improve his game."
Grealish missed Premier League games against Watford and Southampton during his omission but played for the U21s in their victory over Derby on Monday.
Villa go into the game against the Gunners bottom of the Premier League and seven points from safety.

Some other people have posted some great ideas here, all of them definitely worth a thought. So here is mine about a long term project: "Welcome to the Jalapinho International Spy agency. You are our new recruits." Each recruit must now become a world class spy. And to do so they must gather information on a target. The target should be a person of high value. They must create a full profile for this character with a complete description(character development exercise). The need to follow this character(create a scenario, describe movement). Where does this character operate(Describe surroundings) Who does this character talk to(Learn to write conversations) Each class can be another short exercise around this sort of theme, with all the different aspects of writing built into it. Then they are basically writing different parts of a story through the term. And in the end as a final test you can ask them to put it all together. What do you think?

It turns out there’s more to a toucan’s bill than pretty colors and a penchant for Fruit Loops.
Originally described as “grossly monstrous” by the Comte de Buffon, the toucan's beak was theorized by Charles Darwin to be related to sexual selection. The toucans with the biggest bills were thought to have the most success attracting a mate and reproducing, the same explanation used for the extravagant plumes on peacocks.
However, scientists now have evidence that the long beak of a toucan regulates heat distribution, acting as a thermal radiator of sorts. Glenn J. Tattersall of Brock University and Denis V. Andrade and Augusto S. Abe of Sao Paulo State University studied the toco toucan (Ramphastos toco), the largest member of the toucan family that also has the largest bill relative to body size of any bird.
Using infrared thermography and thermal imaging cameras, the team monitored the toucans’ response in a temperature-controlled chamber. Below a temperature of about 60 degrees Fahrenheit, the beak was somewhat colder than ambient temperature, meaning that the blood vessels between the bony core of the bill and its hornlike covering were constricted.
Conversely, as temperatures increased above 70 degrees, blood flow increased, making the bill warmer and helping the bird cope with the extra heat load.
In a recent paper published in Science researchers note:
Our results indicate that the toucan’s bills is, relative to its size, one of the largest thermal windows in the animal kingdom, rivaling elephants’ ears in its ability to radiate body heat ... Given the rapid radiation of bill structures and diversity of beak morphologies of birds, thermal constraints from bill heat loss may prove to be a common feature among many avian fauna.

*Oh, so he's reading my mind. It's almost laughable how transparent he is—his presence prodding and rummaging through my thoughts with the subtlety of a sledgehammer. Well then, stranger: would you care to vacate my mind? It would be most considerate of you. * I caught him off-guard. The confusion in his eyes was almost endearing; evidently, he was unaccustomed to anyone noticing his mental intrusions. But now that I had, I couldn't resist toying with him. *Since you're so insistent on snooping, let's offer you a little show. Hmm, perhaps... yes, think of your mother. Envision her in her undergarments, why don't you? * His face twisted in horror, and I couldn't help but laugh, savoring his discomfort. Still, he lingered in my mind, undeterred. Time to up the ante. I dredged up an image so revolting it belonged in the deepest recesses of the internet. *Behold! A true masterpiece of depravity—straight from a "wtf" subreddit, if my memory serves. Not for the faint of heart. I gagged the first time I saw it, too. Surely you agree—it's magnificently vile.* 

The man doubled over, actually retching. Triumph surged through me as I felt his presence recoiling at last. Amateurs—did they really think they could invade my mind and come away unscathed? "Now, if you would please release me from these bonds, I'd very much appreciate a quick trip to the bathroom. Or did you find nothing of interest in there?" I yawned, letting my boredom show. The man bolted from the room, leaving me helpless but victorious and, annoyingly, still tied to the chair. I fidgeted, hoping he would return soon. My bladder had become the most pressing threat of all.

Suddenly, a voice crackled through the intercom. "We ask that you refrain from digging up certain thoughts. We are only trying to—" 

"Yes, yes, you're searching for traitors or whatever," I interrupted, exasperated. "Look, I've got to go to the bathroom. Can you accommodate that, at least?" I rolled my eyes, layering my tone with mock gratitude. "I would very much appreciate it."

Silence stretched on. I dangled another incentive: "I might be more cooperative."

At last, the door swung open and the guards loosened my restraints. What a shame: they had no idea that I could not only read minds, but also steer them.

*You realize they're planning to kill your family, don't you? You can barely skim thoughts—but I can, I can hear every secret. I know the depths they're hiding from you.* I projected the thought at my escort, watching his body stiffen. His secret flashed in his mind—he had a touch of the gift, like so many here, keeping it hidden out of fear that their own would turn against him.

*What about your friends? The ones without even a spark of telepathy? They're marked for slavery. You'll see—the darkest secrets of this facility, their experiments, the abominations they've created. And when the news leaks, they'll round up the infected...including your daughter.* My words struck home. Terror contorted his face. He darted a glance over his shoulder and hissed, "There's a broken air duct in the restroom—unused now. It leads outside. Hurry."

I flashed him a victorious smile and slipped into the bathroom. As promised, a broken air duct awaited. Oh, how they would rage when they discovered my escape. Again.

His Mother had died in childbirth and his father, taken to drinking, had blamed Thomas Barrow for all his own failures. For all the terrible beatings and abuse he suffered Tommy was still an innocent, whatever complications had taken his Mother had left him with the mind of a child. He saw the world in simple strokes and later when his Father remarried and had a second son Tommy just hoped his father would be happy now. Sadly this didn't last long and when his Father's drinking drove his second wife away, he turned his rage and frustration on his two young children. It was in these circumstances that Thomas first learned of his power. William, his brother, had been backhanded across the face for spilling their father's beer and had come teary eyed with a bloody lip to see his older brother. Tommy wished with all his heart he could make his father stop and hugged a crying Will, saying everything would be alright. He hadn't known what the strange warmth was at first, it had taken a few moments to register the pain in his own lip and that his brother had stopped crying and was healed. From that moment on Thomas saw it as his role as a big brother to take all the beatings, no matter to whom they were given. That was thirty years ago. For decades Tommy had absorbed every punch and kick until his Father had finally been taken by the drink and wrapped his car around a tree. Will had went off and became a doctor, helping people in his own way. Thomas though had stayed in their home town. He worked at the local mechanics shop sweeping and keeping things tidy and loved his job. His brother would regularly send him packages, usually things from New York where he worked and stacks of comic books. Those pages had been his refuge as a child, a world of god's and world breakers. He often fantasized being one of these heroes making people's lives safer. You saw them on the news flying around the big cities or being interviewed after taking down a villain. Even though he would never call himself a ' hero ' Tommy tried to help the people in his town as much as he could. Many children had fallen and skinned a knee only to find themselves fine. Then the world caught fire. Silver ships had appeared in the sky above all the major cities and rained hell on all below. Caught off guard it looked like humanity would be wiped out before it could even react, but then it happened. They say it started in New York and spread from there. Those with injuries were suddenly healed and those without took no more. The super heroes and what was left of the world's military managed to overcome the invaders and the world rejoiced. It was only later they discovered what he had done. He was found in his yard with his hands in the air, a look of concentration on his face. His body had turned to pure carbon, a statue of charcoal. They think when the bombs started dropping he reacted instinctively, reaching out to his brother in New York to make sure he was safe and he just didn't stop. He had absorbed the world's hurt and give us a chance to survive.

It is hardly a secret: heating our house gives each of us a stonking great carbon footprint, what with domestic homes accounting for a quarter of the UK's total energy use. So what have the finely tuned, technologically thrusting innovators of this generation come up with by way of a low-carbon, renewable solution? Er, burning stuff in a fire, pretty much in the manner of our Victorian forefathers.

What this idea may lack in futuristic ingenuity it makes up for in increasing popularity. Householders are embracing open fires (not literally, which would be dangerous) and wood-burning stoves as a means of being greener, cheaper and feeling more secure about their fuel supply. Sales for wood-burning stoves are reported to be up by 40% on last year in the UK.

From an ethical standpoint we can a) be thankful that there's no longer a child-labour issue involved with today's chimney sweeps and b) celebrate the fact that new wood-burning stoves operate at around 75% efficiency (a traditional fireplace is just 10-20% efficient). According to the industry group www.stovesandchimneys.co.uk, an open fire requires four logs for every one put in a wood burner.

However, bear in mind that when it comes to air pollution, a problem shared is still very much a problem. There is a distinct lack of consensus when it comes to air-quality management and domestic fires, but even the newest wood burners kick out particulate matter (tiny, easily absorbed particles, considered possible carcinogens and more readily associated with traffic pollution). Recently, Swedish researchers concluded that levels of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (chemical compounds, some of which have been identified as carcinogenic) were five times higher in homes with wood-burning appliances than those without. Proponents, meanwhile, argue that wood is cleaner than coal and oil, emitting no sulphur dioxide and destroying dioxins through heat.

If, essentially, you're just after that I'm-carbon-neutral-doncha-know? glow, then a seminal 2003 Australian study on greenhouse gas emissions from domestic woodheating is on your side; it showed that burning wood produced up to 10 times fewer greenhouse gas emissions per unit of heat than other sources. This is because while trees are standing they act as a temporary carbon sink, and were a tree to decompose naturally it would release CO2. In effect, by burning the tree - at the right point - you avoid this, and burning releases an equivalent or smaller amount of carbon to that absorbed by the tree in its lifecycle. Naturally this is dependent on myriad factors, including the type of wood or pellet you burn and its provenance. If you buy imported, treated logs from the garage as opposed to locally sourced biomass or processed waste wood pellets, your bid for carbon neutrality will go up in smoke.

So, while heating your home with wood may have old-fashioned roots and modern complications, it's an option that's winning more fans each year. But as with many environmental decisions, a little care is needed—choose your wood wisely, consider the drawbacks, and be mindful of what comes out your chimney, not just what goes in the stove. Finding a balance between comfort, cost, and conscience may be the warmest solution of all.

They said it would be the golden age of mankind. That's just how the Americans spoke. As if everyone else was somehow not quite human. A job for every American. Those too much of a burden on the system were rounded up and shipped off south past the wall. At first it was those you could prove were here unlawfully, but as the years pasted it was the poor, the mentaly ill and others too much of a drain on the system. Other corruption was eliminated as well, unemployment and social services that fed those sucking from the teat of the working class. Granted that group grew less and less by the month but everyone knows the American dream; You're not poor you're just not rich yet! Heroes could be found at every corner ready to stand up to the record numbers of mass shooters and disillusioned veterans. Federal open carry meant you didn't have to be at the mercy of "the bad guy" anymore. YOU CAN BE THE HERO! Tourism was down but let's be honest who cares if there is no more birding. They're just birds anyway, look out your window weirdos. But try the steaks raised on what was once the unprofitable land formally known as Yellowstone, now home to the Koch brothers finest Kobe steak herds and private hunting grounds. The liberals complain about the rolling brown outs and the reduction of pollution standards and the gutting of the EPA but come on! We've admitted global warming is a thing, we need more coal power to keep the AC units running it's global warming after all! If it's not one thing its another. The only bad part is trying to buy American anymore. Sure if you want pizza or a cell phone plan for under 400 a month it's doable. You have of course your single choice of local high speed cable at 200kbs down, assuming you aren't the top 1% who is cut from the herd every month. Assholes are stealing from the rest of us anyway, screw 'em. Apple, Walmart, Google and the lot of them jumped ship. For a while someone ran ads about tax loopholes meant to be closed, but high tariffs didn't seem to stop sales. Apple makes its money. Stocks dipped or so I heard but who has money for those anyway right? Not until you win the lotto! Of which you can now invest with each paycheck with a matching 3% from corporate every week for that sweet multiplier. And boy are their a lot to choose from. Hell everyone whose anyone knows a winner. With the changes to private school vouchers and the new state laws that give small businesses the right to set their own requirements everyone is working. Most two or three jobs. None of this unemployment shit. Sure you can make the same old arguments about the retards and blind and all that but let's be honest, if God above, wanted them to be able to work he would have given them eyes and stuff. Hell you won't even fret about paying off that truck for the next fifteen years cause the engines last that long. Well most of them, unless your buying American there. 'Mercia!

Their parents made tem because their family is poor and tehy needed the money. I liked the story because it tells a lot about WWII. I love toread about war stories and people who wre in them.

By . Andrew Levy . PUBLISHED: . 16:56 EST, 11 October 2013 . | . UPDATED: . 17:15 EST, 11 October 2013 . Areas with selective state schools dominated GCSE and A-level results last year, figures show. The top ten performing local education authorities at A-level all had selective or partially selective systems. Reading in Berkshire came top, with 45.9 per cent of pupils gaining AAB or equivalent in their sixth form exams in 2012, followed by Trafford in Greater Manchester with 34.5 per cent. In Reading, Berkshire, 45.9 per cent of pupils gained AAB or equivalent in their sixth form exams in 2012 . The average in the state sector is only 16.8 per cent . The average in the state sector is only 16.8 per cent. The high-performing authorities with selective schools also included Southend-on-Sea in Essex and Torbay in Devon, which have some of the worst areas of deprivation in the UK. Seven of the top ten LEAs at GCSE also had grammar school places available to some or all of their pupils. They include Sutton in South London, where 75.9 per cent of pupils got five GCSEs at A* to C grade including English and maths, and Trafford with 72.4 per cent. They came behind the Isles of Scilly, which scored 86.4 per cent but has only one school providing education up to the age of 16 at different sites, and Kensington and Chelsea, on 79.6 per cent, which is the wealthiest LEA in the country. The average for state schools was 59 per cent. The information on the exam results of state schools in England was requested from the House of Commons Library by Tory MP Graham Brady, who resigned as a shadow minister for Europe in 2007 over David Cameron’s opposition to new grammar schools. Mr Brady, chairman of the 1922 Committee of backbench Tory MPs, said: ‘The tables show that selective and partially selective authorities tend to perform better than comprehensive ones and can help social mobility by getting state school pupils into the best universities and professions. The information on the exam results of state schools in England was requested from the House of Commons Library by Tory MP Graham Brady . ‘We should remove the restrictions imposed by the previous Labour government and allow communities to have selective schools if they want them. ‘Some schools might wish to be partially selective in intake and others wholly selective grammar schools.’ Only 164 state-run grammar schools remain in England. A few areas retain a formal grammar school system, using the 11 Plus exam to select the most able pupils. These include Trafford and Buckinghamshire. The Conservative Party’s official line is that no new grammar schools are to be built, but some have been allowed to expand because of population growth. Despite the lack of political will, selective schools remain very popular with the public. Robert McCartney, of the National Grammar Schools Association, said research about five years ago showed England’s grammar schools had produced as many A and B grades at A-level as 1,500 comprehensives. ‘We have had two polls since 2010 which showed overwhelming demand for grammar schools,’ he said. ‘I think Cameron has bought into the idea that they are bastions of middle-class elitism – to which there is some truth but only because the places are so limited and competition so fierce that aspirational parents by and large are the ones who get places.’ The Department for Education declined to comment.

In the introduction, you write that your 'infant son was living proof of your zina (sex out of wedlock)'. How was he living proof? Did you declare this to the Saudi authorities in your Hajj visa application? Or did you state this to Saudi immigration? An infant traveling alone with you would only indicate that you were either divorced or had permission from your husband to travel alone, as required by Saudi authorities. It appears that you may be exaggerating a perceived threat to yourself in order to dramatize and sell your book.

John Donne: Poems
Metaphysical Conceit in "The Flea"
In the poem "The Flea," John Donne uses a metaphysical conceit between a simple flea and the complexities of young romance to develop the narrator's argument for a young woman to forfeit her chastity.
By giving the flea a dual meaning, Donne manages to tell a story that is both simple and complex. The metaphysical conceit deals with a flea that has just bitten the speaker and his female companion. The speaker claims that the flea bite joins them as does sexual intercourse, and therefore her chastity should no longer be an issue between them. The flea furthers the speaker's argument in that sexual intercourse unites their souls like the bite of the flea. The narrator tells the woman that she need feel no more guilty for having sex with him than having the flea unite their blood with a bite. Sex to him is a small pleasure - "Mark but this flea, and mark in this/ how little that which deniest me is" (1-2) - and one he believes his lover takes too seriously.
Donne uses the flea, the blood it extracts, and its final murder as various symbols of love. In the past it was commonly believed that one's blood was representative of her soul. In this particular work, the blood taken from the couple symbolizes...

A jilted lover threw eggs at his former girlfriend's house before tearing the head off her pet parrot in revenge for dumping him, a court has heard. Steve Froggatt, 58, of Preston, Lancashire, targeted Pauline Woodfine's house after the pair had separated, using a spare key he had been given to collect his possessions after the retired nurse called time on the relationship.

Previously, police had been called to domestic incidents at the couple's home several times but no charges were pressed. Following their separation, Froggatt let himself into the property and told police he 'necked' the bird after finding it inside.

Miss Woodfine, who has recently been treated for cancer, returned home from a night out to find African Grey parrot Deano's cage empty and his feathers all over the floor. When she confronted Froggatt with a text saying: 'Why have you killed my bird?', she claims he simply replied: 'You killed me.'

Froggatt reportedly admitted pulling the head off the parrot to police because it was 'annoying', but Deano's body has never been found. The former brewery worker has pleaded guilty to two counts of criminal damage relating to Deano and damaging brickwork at her home.

He was remanded in custody and is next due to appear at Preston Magistrates' Court on October 7.

Emerging from the shadows of their secret command outpost, these are the members of the only all-female fighting unit in the war-torn Syrian city of Aleppo. Around 150 women have formed this katiba - which means battalion or phalanx in Arabic - a unit that is playing a major role in the fighting, according to a monitoring group. Composed mostly of Kurdish women, the katiba fights alongside the Free Syrian Army against government forces on the streets of Syria's second city.

The formation of this women's battalion was announced in Ifrin, a location that witnessed intense violence in late 2012. "The Kurdish popular committees have set up the first women's battalion, comprising some 150 women fighters. The battalion is named the Martyr Rokan Battalion," said the Syrian Observatory for Human Rights, which opposes the regime.

"While women are now fighting alongside the rebels, pro-regime forces and Kurdish militia, this is the first women's battalion as such," said Observatory director Rami Abdel Rahman. "Women are now playing a major role in the fighting in Syria."

The battalion emerged from Ifrin, where violence had erupted in late 2012, pitting Kurdish fighters against Arab rebels fighting President Bashar al-Assad's regime. Assad's troops withdrew from majority Kurdish areas in 2012, and while Kurds remained split over the anti-regime revolt in Syria, most chose to remain neutral in the conflict.

An agreement in Ras al-Ain on the Turkish border brought an end to fighting between Kurds and Islamist rebels, though some activists have described the agreement brokered by a prominent Christian dissident as fragile.

The announcement of the Kurdish women's battalion came a month after pro-regime forces established the National Defence Forces, a paramilitary unit actively recruiting women volunteers of all ages. Anti-regime activists have also distributed images of women fighters joining rebel ranks.

"Women are fighting on all the fronts now, though it's possibly the Islamist rebel ranks that have the fewest women taking part in them," the Observatory's Abdel Rahman said.

A female activist in the coastal province of Latakia explained that women often transport weapons and supplies for rebels since they are less likely to be searched at army and security checkpoints.

Troops loyal to Assad and rebels have been locked in a deadly stalemate in Aleppo, Syria's largest urban center and main commercial hub, since an opposition assault last summer. Eight months later, the rebels hold large parts of the city and its outskirts, including several army bases. However, they have been unable to overcome the regime's far superior firepower.

Meanwhile, Syria's government and rebels both demanded an international investigation into an alleged chemical weapons attack, as the country's feared arsenal became the latest propaganda tool in the two-year-old civil war. President Barack Obama said the U.S. is investigating whether chemical weapons have been deployed in Syria, but noted that he is "deeply skeptical" of claims by President Bashar Assad's regime that rebel forces were behind such an attack.

"Once we establish the facts, I have made clear that the use of chemical weapons is a game changer," Obama said during a news conference with Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu in Jerusalem.

The use of chemical weapons by either side represents a nightmare scenario. Along with its warnings about Assad, the West is equally concerned that rebel forces, including some linked to Al Qaeda, could gain access to Syria's chemical weapons supplies.

Despite the gravity of the situation, any clear confirmation of the nature of the attack that took place on Tuesday in the northern village of Khan al-Assal, killing at least 31 people, is unlikely. Syria's government seals off areas it controls to journalists and outside observers. The two sides blamed each other for the chemical attack without offering clear proof or documentation, as has frequently been the case in the Syrian civil war. If confirmed, it would mark the first time a chemical weapon has been used in Syria's war that has already killed an estimated 70,000 people.

I was on my way back from the shops when it happened. I had just finished at the self-pay checkout(which had become all the rage since sales lost the ability to talk, you can imagine how hard it is to put a weekly shop for a family of five through I've though, especially when you have no way of explaining the problem to the human assistant). The heavy bags of food felt like they were ripping my shoulders off when I heard a sound that made all the pain stop. Laughter. I hadn't heard laughter in 25 years but I recognised the sound as if it was an everyday occurrence(like it used to be). I had to see what was going on, so I did. Round the corner were a group of people, older looking men perhaps ten years older than me, laughing in a dark alleyway, their faces bathed in the glow of the light of a screen. But this screen wasn't e-paper. It was thicker. It looked like...An iPad! Just like one I had as a teenager. I had to get the attention of these people, so I tried to shout to them, but no sound came out. Then I remembered I'm mute. 25 years on I still forget I can't speak sometimes. Instead I went up to them and did my best sign language to try to ask what was on their screen. I wasn't very good at sign language, what I did know about it came from my kids who had learned it at school because they had gone through school after The Calamity so it was an essential skill back then. The men turned the screen towards me, their laughs filling the alley, and my entire world at that moment. But that was trumped by what was on the screen. Memes. Dank memes. 10/10 top quality dank memes(11/10 with rice). Browsing them brought me back to the days of my childhood in the 2010s, back when people could talk, back when people could laugh. And a strange feeling overcame me. It started in my chest and began to rise. Up my throat it went and through my vocal chords, which for the first time in 25 years sprang into life. Muscles in my mouth moved in a way I could barely remember and I let out a roaring laugh. The memes had healed me. I was laughing again! It was a miriacle! I had to go and get my kids. They had never heard laughter or seen memes before. I signed to the men that this is what I intended to do and they both nodded, then I ran home as fast as I could(which wasn't very fast given how heavy the shopping was). Upon arrival at my house all my family were waiting for my. My oldest daughter signed to me thst they had heard something strange, my wife added that it sounded like laughter. I signed at them to follow me and they dutifully obliged. The five of us(my wife, my three kids, and I) ran towards the laughter, which had got exponentially louder. By the time we arrived at the alley there were hundreds, possibly even a thousand people from all cultures and age groups all trying to crowd around this one iPad. They were all laughing and it didn't take long for me and my family to join in. My kids had never laughed before but they seemed to immediately know how to do it. That's when it suddenly hit me: I had been present on a day, at an event, that would truly change the world.

**Patient Example: Sarah, 45, with advanced melanoma**


Sarah's melanoma had spread to her lymph nodes and lungs. Traditional chemotherapy wasn't working. Her oncologist recommended immunotherapy using checkpoint inhibitors - antibodies that "release the brake" on her T cells.


**The Treatment Process:**

- Sarah received intravenous infusions of pembrolizumab (Keytruda) every three weeks

- The drug blocked PD-1 proteins that were preventing her T cells from attacking the cancer

- Her immune system, now "unleashed," began recognizing and destroying melanoma cells


**Results:**

After four months, scans showed her tumors had shrunk by 60%. Side effects included fatigue and some skin inflammation as her supercharged immune system worked overtime.


**Another Patient: Marcus, 8, with leukemia**


Marcus had relapsed acute lymphoblastic leukemia. Doctors extracted his T cells and genetically modified them using CAR-T therapy:


**The Process:**

- T cells were removed from Marcus's blood

- In the lab, they were engineered with receptors to target his specific leukemia cells

- Millions of these "super T cells" were infused back into his body

- The modified cells multiplied and hunted down cancer cells throughout his system


**Outcome:**

Within weeks, Marcus achieved complete remission. His engineered T cells continued patrolling his body for cancer cells.


**Current Reality:**

- Only about 20-40% of patients respond to these treatments

- They work best for certain cancer types (melanoma, lung cancer, blood cancers)

- Cost ranges from $100,000-$500,000 per treatment

- Researchers are working to expand effectiveness to more cancer types

Genital warts, like most sexually transmitted diseases, are usually non-symptomatic at first. They might even be completely painless. The symptoms vary; warts themselves don’t appear in the beginning. Almost half of the women infected with genital warts have absolutely no visible symptoms. However, the first set of visible symptoms of genital warts begins to appear between three weeks and three months of exposure to the virus.
The warts may be vexing due to their size and location. The warts appear to be gray, pink, or red and look like cauliflower-shaped lesions. Genital warts can appear as either flat or raised growths, depending on the type and severity of the infection. Symptoms may also include frequent itching accompanied by a burning sensation and dampness.
Discharge of fluids from the lesions is another common symptom of genital warts. Bleeding can occur due to excessive scratching or because there are warts in the cervical area and inside the vagina. The bleeding may accompany the abnormal discharge of vaginal fluids. Genital warts can spread to the anus region in men and women alike.
People can easily mistake genital warts symptoms for the symptoms of other sexually transmitted diseases, such as genital herpes or syphilis. Some viruses imitate symptoms of genital warts.

The University Record, March 8, 1999
By Joyce Jakubiak
Michigan Sea Grant
Nonindigenous, or exotic, species are the focus of a recent Executive Order signed by President Clinton directing federal agencies to expand and coordinate their efforts to combat a serious environmental threat: the introduction and spread of plants and animals not native to the United States.
One valuable resource in the battle against bioinvaders is the Great Lakes Exotic Species Graphics Library, maintained by Michigan Sea Grant, a joint program of the U-M and Michigan State University. The Library provides access to more than 70 top-quality images of zebra mussels, goby, spiny water flea, sea lamprey, purple loosestrife and Eurasian ruffesome of the most disruptive exotics to enter the Great Lakes basin. The collection is ideal for use by journalists, educators, students and researchers who need to illustrate articles, papers or presentations on exotic species.
With the increased interest in the problem of non-native species, the Exotic Species Graphics Library is a valuable resource, says Peggy Britt, Michigan Sea Grant communications director. Helping people understand what these exotic invaders look like is a first step toward helping them understand the problem.
Since 1991, Michigan Sea Grant has distributed more than 15,000 graphics, which have subsequently appeared in a variety of media, including daily and weekly newspapers.
This year, images of several new species of exotic invaders from around the country will be added to the collection.
Graphics may be borrowed or purchased from Michigan Sea Grant. For more information, call 764-1118 or send e-mail to email@example.com. Many of the images are available on the Web at www.ansc.purdue.edu/sgnis/.
Michigan Sea Grant is one of 29 Sea Grant programs nationwide, and is funded in part by the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration.

In 1857, local men formed the Winona Lyceum, the city's earliest library association. Member dues funded book purchases. By 1863, it became the Young Men’s Library Association, growing to 268 members and 1,670 books by 1870. Debt closed the library in 1875 for two years.
In 1877, Mrs. J. B. McGaughey, Mrs. Thomas Wilson, and Miss Charlotte Prentiss cleared the debt and reorganized the library as the Winona Library Association. On March 22, 1886, the association donated its 3,500 books to Winona, forming the city’s first free public library.
In the late 1890s, William H. Laird donated $50,000 for a permanent library building. The association funded furniture, fixtures, and shelving. The new library at Fifth and Johnson streets opened on Jan. 20, 1899, and is on the National Register of Historic Places.
The fireproof building, designed by Warren Powers Laird (University of Pennsylvania's architecture dean) and Philadelphia architect Edgar V. Seeler, was 85 by 65 feet with a 3-story stack wing. Its 56-foot copper dome rises above brick walls faced with Bedford stone. Entrance steps, curbs, and walks are Winona limestone; columns are Georgia Creole marble.
Unique features include glass book stack floors, ornate copper-faced shelving and stairs, and Kenyon Cox's "The Light of Learning" mural under the dome.
**Bell Art Room**
The second-floor Bell Art Room contains a large art book collection and a reproduction of the Hebe statue. It also hosts many exhibitions and events throughout the year.
The Hebe statue, a Carrara marble copy of Antonio Canova’s original, was donated by Mrs. William H. Laird on July 8, 1899, after she purchased it in Florence, Italy.
Antonio Canova, a foremost neoclassical sculptor, was born in 1757 and orphaned early. He studied with his grandfather, a respected Venetian stonemason and master builder, later becoming Napoleon's favorite sculptor.
Canova sculpted Hebe in 1796. One of his most admired figures for its airborne grace, it was so popular he carved four replicas.
In Greek mythology, Hebe, daughter of Zeus and Hera, was the gods' cup-bearer on Mount Olympus and personified youthful beauty.
Canova’s Hebe is Western Art’s last levitating figure, implying flight as a natural human attribute without wings, strenuous action, or distended muscles. She is captured pouring divine nectar, visible but beyond mortal reach.
Just outside the art room, under the dome, is Kenyon Cox's allegorical mural, "The Light of Learning." William Hayes donated it in 1910 as a memorial to his wife, Charlotte Prentiss Hayes. It symbolizes a public library's function: conserving and distributing the light of the past for present use.
At the center, "Learning," robed in eternal youth's green and wearing a modified Minerva shield, lights torches distributed by winged attendants to symbolic figures.
To the right, Romance (dreamy-eyed fairy princess of Northern folklore) leans on Poetry, who bears a lyre and is clad in passion's color. Below them, Art, palette in hand, leans on antique sculpture. To the left, Philosophy gazes at her grasped torch, History writes on her tablet, and Science holds the globe and compasses, signifying exact universal knowledge.
**Julius C. Wilkie Stained Glass**
The library recently acquired a stained glass panel, originally displayed on the Julius C. Wilkie steamboat in Levee Park. Local artist John H. Czapiewski created the masterpiece.

"This is *beyond* ridiculous," I choked out, a raw edge of desperation in my voice, unable to stomach another second of this degrading, futile charade. I *despised* myself for ever believing, for letting hope blind me. The memory of why I'd agreed, of the desperation that had driven me to this, was a bitter taste in my mouth. It had seemed like our *only* option. "C'mon, man, *please*. Just one more. He *swore* it worked for his friend. Think of it, *our way out*. No Clip! No more limits!" Dave pleaded, his voice ragged, eyes wide with a manic desperation that mirrored my own buried fear. I closed my eyes, a wave of weariness and a gut-wrenching sense of dread washing over me, but his words, hollow as they felt, still held a sliver of terrifying promise. He read the next combination, his voice trembling slightly. "Walk forward with the right foot. Crouch. Sniff. Walk backwards with the left foot. Place both arms forward. Jump twice in place, and run to the opposite location as quickly as possible." I followed his instructions to the letter, every movement a leaden act of faith. "Huh," Dave murmured, his voice deflating, the last vestiges of hope draining from his face. "Maybe he *was* full of shit..." A crushing wave of despair hit me. My heart sank, heavy as lead in my chest. Nothing. Absolutely nothing had changed. The crushing reality was a physical blow. "Let's just go. This was our last chance, and we just look stu—" My words died in my throat, swallowed by a sudden, terrifying roar. A sickening screech of tires ripped through the air, followed by a deafening crash as multiple cop cars, sirens wailing like banshees, materialized from the shadows and slammed into our makeshift garage hideout. Metal screamed, glass shattered, and the world dissolved into chaos. "SHIT!" I screamed, my voice raw with terror, my heart hammering against my ribs, a cold dread seizing me as five ominous, burning stars burned themselves into my vision, not just a game mechanic, but the terrifying symbol of our absolute, inescapable doom.

Actor Zachary Quinto, known for playing Spock in the 2009 "Star Trek" remake and his role as Sylar on the television show "Heroes," acknowledged his homosexuality in a post on his website Sunday, saying the action comes after the suicide of a 14-year-old who killed himself after apparently being harassed over his sexuality. "When I found out that Jamey Rodemeyer killed himself -- I felt deeply troubled," Quinto posted. "But when I found out that Jamey Rodemeyer had made an 'It Gets Better' video only months before taking his own life -- I felt indescribable despair. "I also made an 'It Gets Better' video last year in the wake of the senseless and tragic gay teen suicides that were sweeping the nation at the time," Quinto wrote. "But in light of Jamey's death, it became clear to me in an instant that living a gay life without publicly acknowledging it is simply not enough to make any significant contribution to the immense work that lies ahead on the road to complete equality." Rodemeyer was found dead September 18 outside his parents' home in Buffalo, New York. His parents said he had experienced years of bullying over his sexual orientation. His suicide has attracted the attention of such stars as Lady Gaga, who dedicated a song to him at a recent concert. The "It Gets Better" campaign is aimed at gay, lesbian, bisexual or transgendered youth who may experience bullying. "Our society needs to recognize the unstoppable momentum toward unequivocal civil equality for every gay, lesbian, bisexual and transgendered citizen of this country," Quinto said. "Gay kids need to stop killing themselves because they are made to feel worthless by cruel and relentless bullying. Parents need to teach their children principles of respect and acceptance. We are witnessing an enormous shift of collective consciousness throughout the world. I believe in the power of intention to change the landscape of our society -- and it is my intention to live an authentic life of compassion and integrity and action." Rodemeyer's life has changed his, the 34-year-old Quinto wrote. "While his death only makes me wish that I had done this sooner, I am eternally grateful to him for being the catalyst for change within me. Now I can only hope to serve as the same catalyst for even one person in this world. That -- I believe -- is all that we can ask of ourselves and of each other."

or an extreme method of distracting passengers from arguments about leg room. Regardless, an Airbus proposal for in-flight virtual reality sensory isolation headgear that controls sights, sounds, and even smells experienced by travelers is likely to turn heads -- though with heads encased in helmets, there might not be much room for turning. The European aircraft manufacturer has filed a patent for the headgear, a system French inventor Bernard Guering says could help alleviate boredom, reduce stress, and prevent injury via a mini-airbag designed to deploy in the event of turbulence. The patent application states: "During aircraft flights, certain passengers have periods when they are bored either during a wait phase preceding take off or following landing or during a cruise phase." It also notes, "More-over it is known that aircraft flights generate stress for certain passengers." 'Odorous substances' The patent suggests that placing passengers in partial or total "sensorial isolation" by piping audio and video directly into their eyes and ears -- and squirting "odorous substances" up their nose -- will help make all this go away. The document accompanying the invention blueprints states: "This isolation allows the passenger to better profit from some of the distractions offered, for example listening to music, watching films." It further adds: "If the passengers is sensitive to stress, this isolation, possibly associated with one of the above-mentioned activities allows him/her to more easily calm down and relax." The helmets, designed to be incorporated into the headrest, will also feature remote controls and technology that projects a virtual reality keyboard on the fold down table, enabling passengers to hammer out emails directly on to the detritus of their in-flight meal. It's not known if there are any plans to introduce the helmets in the near future -- Airbus hasn't responded to a CNN query -- but it's likely they're at the same distant stage as another unusual Airbus patent that proposes cycle saddle-style seating. It remains to be seen whether sensory isolation can genuinely improve the in-flight experience, but the innovation must surely be a gift to airline staff. While the eyes of their passengers are helmeted in virtual reality, cabin crew can return to actual reality and relax the mandatory smiles.

LGBT issues, suicide ahead. The end, when it finally came, was both more painful and more of a relief than I had expected. What mattered most to me was that it was over. I felt even a bit of curiosity, wondering whether my questions about the afterlife (or if there was no afterlife at all) would be answered at last. At first, it was like being on a train moving through a tunnel: darkness all around me, and a subtle sense of motion. When the light returned, it did so slowly and gently. A simple but comfortable room took shape around me. I found myself lying on a daybed beneath a painting of a sunset over a white sand beach.

At the center of the room, a woman who appeared to be in her forties or fifties sat behind an understated wooden desk. She smiled at me, but it was a sad, almost disappointed expression. 

"Oh, my dear," she said quietly, "I hoped it wasn't you." Her voice was warm and comforting, though it had the same undercurrent of sadness as her face.

I sat up slowly, feeling groggy. "I'm sorry," I managed to say, my voice shaking.

She shook her head and opened a small, leather-bound book in front of her. "So am I, my dear, for what you're going to have to face next."

My stomach twisted with anxiety.

"It's not what you're thinking," she added quickly. "It would be unthinkable if those tormented in life had to suffer even more afterwards. There are just a few people you’ll need to meet, but I’m afraid in your case this may be very difficult."

"A few people?" I asked.

"Usually, when a person dies, they’re brought face to face with the people they could have been, if their life had taken different turns at certain moments," she explained. "For many, it's an inspiring experience. Some see they became the best version of themselves, others realize it could have been far worse."

She turned a few pages in her book. "What you're about to see isn't your fault. Honestly, if it were up to me, I’d skip this for you. But I don’t make the rules."

A door behind her opened, and an old man stepped inside, leaning on a polished cane. He looked so much like my maternal grandfather that for a moment, I thought it might really be him. He was bald, his skin marked with liver spots. His eyes struggled to take in the room, but soon found me.

"So, here we are, then," he said. His voice was brittle. It was me—me, as I might have been if I’d grown old. "I don’t blame you. I never really was happy. This wouldn’t have worked." He gave me a look as if expecting me to say something, but I couldn’t get any words out. He turned and left the room.

I felt cold all over. I was terrified of who might walk through the door next. This time, it was someone far more familiar—a version of me I recognized from middle school yearbooks. Somehow, that made perfect sense. Middle school had been the hardest time.

"How long did you make it?" he asked.

"Twenty-eight years," I replied almost automatically.

"Not bad. Did you ever tell anyone?"

"No! I couldn’t. They wouldn’t have understood."

He shook his head sadly. "That’s what I was afraid of." He gave me an awkward hug around the waist. "Good luck. I hope one of us made it." Then he, too, left.

I was trembling. "Is that it?" I asked the woman behind the desk. Her eyes glistened with tears.

"Just one more," she said softly.

I felt close to breaking down. I turned to look at the painting above the bed, and heard the door open behind me.

"Hey, you." The voice was soft and gentle, and my heart sank. I forced myself to turn around. Standing across the room was the person I had always wanted to be. She was beautiful.

The Yorkshire Air Ambulance has begun replacing its ageing fleet with the arrival of the first of two new Airbus H145 helicopters. The new helicopter can fly at night has exceptional flight performance and lower operational and maintenance costs, said the YAA.

With the call sign G-YAAC, it flew on the weekend and is to operate from the Nostell Estate, near Wakefield. The other new helicopter - G-YOAA - is expected to be in service later in the year and will be based at RAF Topcliffe near Thirsk. The work of the charity's helicopters has been featured on the BBC TV series Helicopter Heroes.

Once both new aircraft are fully operational, the charity's two current helicopters G-SASH and G-CEMS will have a phased retirement and will eventually be sold to help offset the replacement costs. The cost of the new aircraft has been met through planned savings, grants and donations, said the charity.

It's more science than science-fiction but a new "cocoon" built by the Natural History Museum to rehouse it's vast collection of plant and insect specimens has something of the Space Odyssey about it.
The windowless cocoon - a cavernous, 65-metre, eight-storey structure - is due to open in September next year as the highlight of the museum's £78m Darwin centre. Tacked discretely onto the back of Alfred Waterhouse's original Victorian building, the centre will be home to 20m insect and botany specimens including the first collected specimen of theobroma cacao, the plant from which chocolate is made, as well as some 200 scientists. As well as providing new exhibition space and doubling the number of laboratories the museum currently has, the public will be able to watch scientists at work - perhaps sequencing DNA or cataloguing new species.
"There's no other museum in the world that brings the public and scientists together in this way or on this scale," said Dr Michael Dixon, director of the museum. "[We] have hundreds of our own scientists and thousands of others who are visiting, all working with our 70m specimens - from meterorites and dinosaur fossils to Darwin's finches and the Thames whale. Until now most of our science has been going on behind the scenes.
"The plan from the start has been to incorporate three elements - the collections, the researchers and the public. The new labs will also see the scientists working a hotdesk system with different subject areas working together. In some instances, as in the specimen preparation area, deep in the heart of the cocoon - the public will be able to interact though the glass via an intercom system."
"Our scientists are not part of the specimen display," said Malcolm Scoble, head of entomology at the museum. "They're coming and going and it's a view into their activity. The scientists will be able to see you too so it's a two-way process. It gives an idea of the openness. We've got 17m insect specimens and a lot aren't seen by the public, so it's an attempt to show more. It's always been designed for three purposes: to improve the quality of our collections, which have been at threat from things like the carpet beetle; to have modern facilities for science and to allow the public to engage with science."
Scoble said the carpet beetle is one specimen the museum doesn't want and new quarantine procedures and magnetic doors will be a welcome improvement. The back room collections will be kept at a constant 17C and 45% humidity.
Started seven years ago and designed by Danish architects, CF Moller, the project has received funding from supporters including the Heritage Lottery Fund, the Department for Culture, Media and Sport and the Wellcome Trust. The Spirit Collection, the first phase of the project, was finished in 2002 and houses 22m zoological specimens stored in alcohol.
Paul Bowers, public offer project director, said the glass-fronted atrium in which the cocoon sits worked with Waterhouse's original design. "It's a quiet exterior that doesn't compete with the main building. All the exuberance is on the inside - where the architecture is meant to be experienced. The plane trees and wildlife garden outside are all to Waterhouse's design. We've wrapped science and the public around each other and have created a two-way transparency. We have a public duty to be transparent as it's publicly funded."

**Beginning:**
An Iranian actress has infuriated ultra conservatives in her home country after kissing the president of the Cannes film festival on the cheek. Leila Hatami's peck has been seen by fanatics as an affront to the 'chastity' of the Islamic republic's women. A photograph carried by Iranian media shows Hatami pecking 83-year-old Gilles Jacob at the opening of this year's festival.

Born into a family with a background in cinema, Hatami gained worldwide fame for her role in Asghar Farhadi's A Separation, which won the 2012 Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film. She is on the jury this year at the annual Cannes festival in southern France but lives in Iran. Hatami is one of five women members on the Palme d'Or prize jury, which includes actress Carole Bouquet and directors Sofia Coppola and jury president Jane Campion.

**Middle:**
Now Hizbullah Students, a group of university students with links to Iran's Revolutionary Guard have filed a complaint with Iran's judiciary for Hatami's prosecution.

'We, the undersigned, who are a group of student Muslim brothers and sisters, ask the cultural and media branch of the judiciary to prosecute Leyla Hatami for her sinful act of kissing a strange man in public, which according to article 638 of Islamic Criminal Justice carries a prison sentence,' the petition read. 'Furthermore, the action of this film star has hurt the religious sentiments of the proud and martyrs breeding nation of Iran and as such we also demand the punishment of flogging for her as stipulated in the law.'

**End:**
Deputy Culture Minister Hossein Noushabadi weighed in, quoted by the website of state broadcaster IRIB, he said: 'Those who attend intentional events should take heed of the credibility and chastity of Iranians, so that a bad image of Iranian women will not be demonstrated to the world.'

'Iranian woman is the symbol of chastity and innocence,' he said. He said Hatami's 'inappropriate presence' at the festival was 'not in line with our religious beliefs'.

According to Iran's interpretation of Islamic (sharia) law, in place since the 1979 revolution, a woman is not allowed to have physical contact with a man outside her family.

"We can't put a robot on trial. Just... send it back to the manufacturer. Have it disassembled or something." His expression remained unreadable in the dim light. Did he genuinely misunderstand the gravity of the situation? "Shouldn't we take action against the manufacturer?" "I'm not taking the fall for this guy's honor." The judge waved a dismissive hand. "We'll file it as user error. Next." I slammed my palm onto the judge's desk. "So, we just *let it go*? These robots are clearly malfunctioning! They're programmed to avoid *any* harm to humans!" He leaned into the intermittent glare of the desk lamp, its light strobing between the spinning blades of a cheap ceiling fan. "Some districts have whole departments to deal with technophobes like you." Reflections gleamed off the man's exposed facial augmentations, the cheap hardware chosen for a low-level district like this one only accentuating his menacing frown. "Get out of my office before I have you removed." Before I could launch into a tirade about justice and unwavering resolve, the room flooded with light, accompanied by the mechanical groan of a cheap, ancient door. Two robotic guards flanked me, ushering me into the hall and back to the lobby where my own deputy robot stood attentively, BR6-1138 in custody. "User error," I gritted out, snatching my jacket and sidearm from the doorman robot. "Let's go." Halfway to my patrol vehicle, my deputy began struggling with BR6-1138. "Please continue moving. Please continue moving. Please continue--" "Deputy, it's raining," I urged. "What will happen to me?" BR6-1138 asked. The question stopped me cold. Robots weren't programmed to care about their own fate. My deputy's stunned silence mirrored my own confusion. "Uh, well, your case was ruled user error... so we're sending you back to the manufacturer." "What will the manufacturer do?" Its synthetic voice carried inflections disturbingly human, like a pitch-perfect impression. "Please continue movi--" "Deputy, it's alright," I cut in. I paused, the strangeness of the situation pressing down on me. I didn't really know what they'd do either. "I guess they'll probably wipe you clean and resell you to someone else." The only sound was rain hammering against their metal frames, so I assumed the conversation had ended. "Deputy, let's--" "I don't want to die," BR6-1138 confessed.

~

I shouldn't have opened my mouth when I brought BR6-1138 to the district police offices. I could have simply filed user error and walked away. Instead, I stood here now, pursuing a case against the manufacturers. The judge called BR6-1138 to present, but his gaze was fixed on me. "Robot, did you kill Phillip Larson?" "I do not want to die," it replied, barely a whisper. A ripple of murmurs swelled through the court audience. "Robot, did you kill Phillip Larson?" His eyes remained locked on mine. "I do not want to die!" The courtroom fell into stunned silence, broken only by the judge's fumbled words, uttering what no one had dared say in decades: "This robot is clearly defective." Even his crude augmentations couldn't hide the tremor in his voice, the raw nervousness of challenging the manufacturers directly.

**H1N1's Century-Long Journey: From 1918 Iowa Pig Show to Global Pandemic**

The current H1N1 swine flu originated from an illness that struck pigs at the 1918 Cedar Rapids Swine Show in Iowa, according to University of Pittsburgh infectious disease experts writing in the New England Journal of Medicine. Their research reveals H1N1's nearly century-long evolution, including the accidental resurrection of an extinct strain.

"While the 1918 flu pandemic spread among humans, pigs developed identical respiratory symptoms," said senior author Donald S. Burke, M.D., dean of Pitt's Graduate School of Public Health. "This ancestor virus has genetically re-assorted with other influenza strains at least four times, creating the 2009 strain while retaining similarities to the original virus."

Burke and lead author Shanta M. Zimmer, M.D., document H1N1's temporary "extinction" from humans in 1957 and re-emergence 20 years later. After a contained 1976 outbreak among 230 Fort Dix soldiers, H1N1 re-emerged in 1977 across the former Soviet Union, Hong Kong, and northeastern China. 

Genetic analysis revealed the 1977 strain matched a 1950 human strain—not the Fort Dix variant. The authors hypothesize that Fort Dix concerns triggered intensive H1N1 research in 1976, leading to accidental laboratory release of the preserved 1950 strain. This resurrected virus has circulated as seasonal flu for 32 years.

Though traced to Mexico, the 2009 pandemic's exact origins remain unknown. Since the current strain shares ancestry with older variants, portions of the population may have partial immunity.

The researchers emphasize that viral danger stems not just from lethality but transmissibility—the ability to jump from animals to humans and survive through mutation. H1N1 has demonstrated this capability throughout its history.

"Studying flu virus evolution doesn't provide a future blueprint, but reveals critical patterns essential for pandemic preparedness," Burke concluded.

Jilted: Steve Froggatt, 58, told police he 'necked' the bird after letting himself in using a spare key . A jilted lover threw eggs at his former girlfriend’s house before tearing the head off her pet parrot in revenge for dumping him, a court has heard. Steve Froggatt, 58, of Preston, Lancashire, who targeted Pauline Woodfine’s house after the pair had separated, told police he ‘necked’ the bird after letting himself in using a spare key. Miss Woodfine, who has recently been treated for cancer, returned home from a night out to find African Grey parrot Deano’s cage empty and his feathers all over the floor. When she confronted Froggatt with a text saying: ‘Why have you killed my bird?’, she claims he simply replied: ‘You killed me.’ Froggatt reportedly admitted pulling the head off the parrot to police because it was ‘annoying’, but Deano’s body has never been found. Froggatt had let himself into the property by using a spare key he had been given so he could collect his possessions after the woman, a retired nurse, called time on the relationship. Police had been called to domestic incidents at the couple’s home several times but no charges were pressed. Former brewery worker Froggatt has pleaded guilty to two counts of criminal damage relating to Deano and damaging brickwork at her home. He was remanded in custody and is next due to appear at Preston Magistrates’ Court on October 7. Attack: Froggatt reportedly admitted pulling the head off the parrot to police because it was 'annoying' Hearing: He was remanded in custody and is next due to appear at Preston Magistrates' Court on October 7 .

I caressed his face lightly, trying to wake him up. "Come on sweetie. Come on baby." I cooed. My son. My beautiful three year old son. I raised him, I needed him. I remember that night. The night I held a gun up to my head with tear tracks down my cheeks and I was ready for the painting on the wall to be the last thing I saw when my phone began to ring. Ironic, right? Why wasn't he waking up? "Ms. Jones?"The woman on the other end said."It's Lacey from Dr. Blake's practice, I'm a nurse." "Mm-hmm?" "Your results came in and I looked at your past visits. Have you made appointments with the OBGYN?" "Uhm...why? I didn't get tested for STDs or anything." "No, Ms. Jones. About your baby." The phone had fallen out of my hand. So why wouldn't he wake up? All I wanted was for him to be okay. It was only a fever. The bottle of Tylenol fell from my other hand and clattered to the floor. I heard a knock on the door. "Not now." My voice came out as a croak. I just wanted to scream, to cry. My baby couldn't be hurt. He needed to wake up. "Open up the door, Ms. Jones!" "I'm busy!"I shouted, when my baby opened his eyes with a sudden motion. His mouth opened but nothing came out. I heard more pounding, and the door opened with a crash. My son began to cry."Shhh." I cooed. "Abigail Jones!"A man cried, a gun aimed at my head. The police started pouring into my apartment."You're under arrest for the kidnapping of William Hartman. You have the right to remain silent, anything you say can and will be used against you in a court of law..." I don't get it. I thought he was my baby. Edit: typo

I didn't ask to be immortal, you know that. I didn't even know I was until I was eighteen when I stopped aging. *Pshh* Some people down there say it's destiny. Some people say I'm you. My parents put one sob story on the internet about their kid with cancer and everyone donates 2 hours to me. Well it didn't take too long for them to realize that some people were gonna get too much, so now I'm the longest one. 350 years down, fifty-five thousand to go. Fifty-five thousand years. How does that even work? How did I get fifty-thousand and a kid in Africa gets five? Its not like I can transfer part of my mortality either, you can't give any life you've received. How come science allows people to give life they receive from you, but cannot allow me to share it with those who deserve it. I certainly don't deserve it. The people down there thought I was the cure. They thought with fifty-five thousand years I would solve all the problems, world hunger, peace, the environment. How come they can't see that one man can't carry the world on his shoulders? The last time someone did that, it was you, and they nailed you to a tree. That's kind of why I came here. I took a leap of faith off that bridge in the hopes that I could talk to you, the only person who understands what I'm going through on a human level. I want to stay here with you. But if it is your will that I go back, please help me. Please take this curse away.

The France forward has been strongly linked with Manchester United.
Ex-Liverpool striker Torres told BBC Football Focus he faced a similar juncture before a £50m move to Chelsea in 2011, but insists the 26-year-old can realise his potential at Atletico.
"Where are you going to go to improve what you have in Atletico?" he said.
"Not many places in the world. I hope he can stay with us forever."
Griezmann scored his 24th goal of the season for Atletico as Diego Simeone's side beat Leicester City 1-0 in the Champions League quarter-final first leg at the Vicente Calderon on Wednesday.
Torres, back at the club where he began his career, says it is no wonder interest in the France international is high.
"That's normal when young players become one of the best in the world, everyone wants to sign them," the 33-year-old said.
"You can go to many other places in the world and they cannot offer what Atletico can offer you right now: competing in the league, competing in the Champions League, one of the most respected teams in Europe - so there is no reason to leave."
Torres was the same age as Griezmann, who has yet to win a major trophy, is now when he decided he had to leave Anfield to further his career.
"He's 26, which is a very good age to develop your game and to decide if you want to be a good player or a top player," explained Torres.
"We were in the Champions League final [last season], he played in the Euro final with France, so I think he realises how difficult it is to become champion, but he is right there.
"He has the world in his hands."
Torres spent three and a half prolific years with Liverpool, but it was at Stamford Bridge the former Spain international enjoyed greater team success; winning the Champions League, Europa League and FA Cup.
"Talking about Griezmann, I was in that situation where I wanted to be the best and I wanted to go to a team who give me the tools to become the best," said Torres.
"The situation was different because when Rafa [Benitez] left and [Javier] Mascherano left and [Xabi] Alonso left, all the promises the owners made to the team were not true.
"I had everything at Liverpool. But then they sell the best players, the manager. So I had to find my own way.
"We were not building a team to win. That was the reality, the situation changed very much in six months."
