It is claimed Peter Barnett, 43, boarded trains at Haddenham and Thame Parkway without a ticket and used an Oyster card to "tap out" at Marylebone.
Chiltern Railways claims it lost £23,000. Barnett, who admits fraud, said it was £10,000 once leave was taken into consideration.
The case at Westminster Magistrates' Court was adjourned until 27 July.
Chiltern Railways has been urged to calculate the exact loss to the company before the case can proceed.
The court was told Barnett, from Oxford, was stopped by a ticket inspector at Marylebone station and asked where he travelled from.
When he claimed to have travelled from Wembley, instead of Haddenham and Thame Parkway in Oxford, the inspector became suspicious and Barnett ran off.
He handed himself in later that day.
Barnett admits six counts of fraud by false representation between April 2012 and November last year.
He has been released on unconditional bail.

I stroked his cheek, desperate to wake him. "Come on, sweetie. Come on, baby," I whispered. My son. My beautiful three-year-old son. I raised him. I needed him. I remembered that night—the gun pressed to my temple, cheeks stained with tears, ready for the wall's blank stare to be my last. Then the phone rang. Bitter irony.

Why wouldn't he wake up?

"Ms. Jones?" a woman's voice interrupted. "It's Lacey, Dr. Blake's nurse."  
"Mm-hmm?"  
"Your results came in. I checked your past visits. Have you seen your OBGYN?"  
"Why? I wasn't tested for STDs."  
"No, Ms. Jones. About your baby."  

The phone slipped from my hand.

Why wouldn't he wake up? He only had a fever. The Tylenol bottle clattered to the floor. A knock at the door. "Not now," I rasped, choking back tears. He needed to wake up. He had to be okay.

"Open up, Ms. Jones!"  
"I'm busy!" I shouted.

Suddenly, his eyes fluttered open. His mouth moved, silent. The pounding at the door grew louder—a crash, and police burst in.

"Abigail Jones!" a man shouted, gun pointed at me. Tears streaming, I cradled my wailing son.

"You're under arrest for the kidnapping of William Hartman. You have the right to remain silent..."

I don't understand. I thought he was my baby.

We didn't purchase this product to keep the squirrels out; that was never our intention. For the most part, squirrels ignore it; we offer them more flavorful alternatives. Our true aim was to thwart the large flocks of blackbirds that swarm our feeders in winter and spring. And it worked beautifully! We're thrilled that only one blackbird at a time can feed, and then only if it's painstakingly slow and careful to pick out each seed. No longer do our lovely goldfinches find a feeder picked clean. The only limitation, however, is that it exclusively holds sunflower seeds. While I do like the feeder and would buy it again, it doesn't accommodate thistle as we'd hoped. So, if you're seeking a thistle feeder, this isn't your solution. That's its single drawback.

I visited the Western Navajo Nation office in Tuba City to ask about my nalí’s grazing permit. If your family has a grazing permit, please make sure to get your livestock counted yearly 🐂🐏🐐🐎🐮🐑. With the ongoing drought in the Western agency, this annual livestock count is important—you can use it to apply for assistance through the USDA office in Flagstaff. Just like the census for people is done every 10 years, livestock in open range grazing areas must also be counted regularly.

PLEASING TO THE EAR, BUT NO EXCITEMENT I FEAR!

This is a pleasant album with a good variety of songs, some new to me. Drew Minter's voice is technically perfect: excellent tone, accurate pitch, and clear diction. But it utterly lacks excitement or emotion. He sings "O Solitude" and "Crown the Altar" identically, despite their vastly different meanings. His best renditions are "Lord, What is Man" and "Be Welcome then, Great Sir." Drama is limited to volume changes. These songs gain meaning when a performer grasps their message. The accompanying instruments are excellent, and the album is worth buying if you're a Purcell enthusiast. However, for exciting renditions of these wonderful songs, try Michael Chance or David Daniels instead.

I enjoyed the setting and the acting in this mystery. I'd like to know, though, why the man who is the bookstore owner's assistant works in a bookstore when he is so highly qualified that he can call up the Canadian PM for information. It would have been good to have backstory, unless one of the other Mystery Women movies offers this. I've watched the Snapshot mystery, which I enjoyed, and there was no backstory there either.

Imagine, for instance, if he'd once been a brilliant young analyst for Canada’s intelligence service—someone with a passion for rare books, who became disillusioned with the endless bureaucracy after a case went tragically wrong. Seeking peace, he quietly left his high-profile world and settled into the comforting scent of old paper and the gentle hum of turning pages. Still, now and then, his old life calls to him, as when an urgent question from his new employer prompts him to dig deep into his contacts for answers that can change the course of a mystery. Adding just a hint of this kind of past would make him even more intriguing and the story richer.

The Changing Face of Malnutrition
Almost every country in the world, low-, middle- or high-income, faces some form of malnutrition including undernutrition, overweight/obesity, or a combination of these conditions. This overlap of different types of malnutrition — undernutrition (e.g., stunting, vitamin and mineral deficiencies) co-existing with increasing rates of overweight and obesity — is known as the double burden of malnutrition.
Stunting (being too short for age) in young children is the result of undernutrition in the womb and early in life. In young children, stunting also is associated with poor brain development and educational performance, which leads to lower adult wages and lost productivity. When accompanied by excessive weight gain later in childhood, stunting is associated with increased risk of nutrition-related chronic diseases, such as diabetes.
Malnutrition affects school performance and studies have shown it often leads to lower earning potential as an adult.
About the Double Burden of Malnutrition
While stunting and vitamin and mineral deficiencies remain high in many low- and middle-income countries, rising income, urbanization, decreasing levels of communicable diseases, changes in diet and reductions in physical activity also are driving a transition to the co-existence of undernutrition and overweight/obesity. The double burden presents enormous health, social and economic challenges to countries and action is needed now to address this changing face of malnutrition.
When accompanied by excessive weight gain later in childhood, stunting is associated with increased risk of nutrition-related chronic diseases, such as diabetes

Michael Ruddy scored a penalty kick in the 37th minute after Ballinamallard's goalkeeper Richard Brush had fouled Guillaume Keke.
It was French player Keke who scored the second with a skillful shot aimed at the near post in the 64th minute.
Ballinamallard could have moved above Ards in the league standings if they had won, but it was Colin Nixon's team who won the match and have now gone three games without a loss.
Before that, the team from County Down had lost five Premiership matches consecutively.
Ards are level on points with Glentoran, who are currently in eighth place, and are playing an away game against Coleraine on Saturday.
Ards manager Colin Nixon: "I thought it was a well-deserved win.
"We put them under pressure a bit and had a few chances and scored a well-taken penalty.
"In the second half I think we fully deserved the win.
"Ballinamallard had caught up to us when we lost several consecutive games, but we are four points ahead of them now and on a good run of form now."

The Reds have been heavily linked with the 18-year-old midfielder, who has hugely impressed Rodgers.
"I read we had put in a bid or something had been agreed but there has been nothing," said the Liverpool boss.
"I think he is a wonderful young talent who just needs to play football and for me he is probably at the best place he could be."
He added: "I don't normally comment on speculation but [I will] because I have the interest of the player at heart."
Hughes, who came through the ranks at Derby, scored in England U21's 9-0 win over San Marino in midweek.
"This is a very talented young player that either a person or a group of people aren't doing him any favours whatsoever by constantly linking him with coming to Liverpool," said Rodgers.
"This is a young kid learning the game - he is at an outstanding club at Derby, he has a great manager there and we sent one of our young players [Andre Wisdom] there to develop and learn."
Hughes, who joined the Rams from local rivals Nottingham Forest at the age of 12, made his first team debut in November 2011 as a 16-year-old.
So far this season he has made 18 appearances for the Championship side, scoring four goals.

"So you're searching for adventure?" "Yes we're noble champions..." said the dark-skinned one. "Of Bottomless pouch!" shouted the little fellow with pointy ears. "...of the Kingdom of Ekalbar, pursuing justice and triumph of good." The first shot his companion a hard glance. Four of them total. The black man led the party—a warrior, strong and quiet. The little one was less impressive but talked constantly. A silent woman in white robes, pretty. The last had impressive girth and walked like a whale swam, slow and elegant, with an array of wands at his side. "I see. Several weeks ago we had bandit problems, but they vanished or are hiding somewhere...no one knows what became of them so everyone grows more frightened daily. Nobody came to drive them away, so what are they doing now?" "Strange bandits. Anything else?" said the leader. "Weird noises from a cavern near the river, and strange smells, like...dead things?" "Probably someone nearby going mad. Any disappearances in the region?" "Nothing comes to mind, save those bandits." "Anything else?" "There's a crumbling temple in the forest, something nasty, ancient and malignant...Rumor says it was once beautiful, full of riches, gold trees, crystal fountains and mighty artifacts now buried under rubble." "What caused this devastation?" "Legend tells of tragic romance, death and rebirth...but not pleasant." "...Like undead? Powerful maybe?" "Maybe...you're more knowledgeable than me." "Sure. Thanks. Pack us some food? Tomorrow we're going to that forest." "But that's the most dangerous one!" "So? It's the most malevolent! We're cleaning it out!" "And taking the gold!" said the little one. "As you wish, customer is king." They departed at dawn. They would meet their doom in the temple. *Undead?* Seriously? It was a dragon that claimed those ruins! Soon I would trade some ale for the corpses—they'd fit perfectly in my growing army of zombies.

COMAC is the main organization that is facilitating the building of large passenger aircraft in China. The company is mandated with the overall planning and development of trunk liner and regional jet programs, as well as the industrialization of civil aircraft. It is building the new C919 jet, which will use aluminum-lithium alloys and composites in its airframe structures.
COMAC also recently signed a long-term collaboration agreement with Bombardier. Initially, the companies will collaborate on four projects that recognize the similarities between the C919 and Bombardier's CSeries family of commercial airliners. One of the projects is developing standards and specifications for the use of aluminum-lithium in aircraft.
Several different companies and organizations jointly own COMAC, including the Aluminum Corporation of China, also known as China Aluminum Corp. (CHINALCO or CHINCO). CHINALCO has reportedly announced the third generation of its 540mm aluminum-lithium alloy for use in large aircraft.
The company has spent several years in R&D working on aluminum alloys for China's aerospace industry. The new alloy represents a breakthrough for China, and for its aerospace industry, since the production of aluminum-lithium has long been dominated by suppliers in other countries, the company's senior managers said.

Schools, boards of education, and even whole countries (such as Iceland) can sign teachers up to take in-depth courses in various aspects of English teaching, such Learning Technologies for the Classroom and/or Primary Essentials, to name only two examples.
In Learning Technologies for the Classroom, teachers receive training in all sorts of technologies that can be used to make learning more effective. Examples include teaching students to use wikis to write collaborative projects, and creating videos on their school that the kids post on YouTube for the whole world to enjoy. (Included is also lots of useful information in being web-savvy in this sometimes nasty world.)
Primary essentials has been designed for teachers who find themselves teaching young learners English, yet have no training in this extremely demanding job.
These courses cost about Ikr. 12.000 per participant (assuming that we could get 16 participants to sign up). In this context, FEKÍ will make the case to education authorities in Iceland that such courses deserve central funding.

The first encampment appeared on Station Street on Christmas Eve, but its occupants were forced to leave earlier on Wednesday after a judge, at the council's urging, ordered their removal. Just days later, about 20 people, still seeking shelter, arrived at the Cliff Road site on 2 January—demonstrating the ongoing, unaddressed need for housing.

Yet Nottingham City Council continues to claim that most of these vulnerable individuals are neither homeless nor local, dismissing their struggles rather than addressing them. Instead of offering solutions, the council has denied calls to open up empty buildings to those in need, arguing—without evidence—that such steps are unnecessary. In their statement, the council expressed satisfaction that the Station Street tents were cleared "without the need for enforcement action," ignoring the hardship this created for those evicted.

Now, the occupants—supported by FightBack, a Nottingham-based group dedicated to social justice—face yet another deadline: they have until 08:00 GMT on Thursday to leave the Cliff Road site. If the council's wishes are granted, these people will once again be displaced, their needs ignored so that "the site can return to public use and the residents can be left in peace," as a spokesman put it.

But real peace will not come from hiding homelessness and pushing people out of sight. As Joe Public, who spoke in court on behalf of the Station Street occupants, said: "I'm very disappointed because it shows the council are pushing these people from pillar to post without addressing the problem of homelessness." He added: "The council needs to stop trying to remove and hide these people from the public eye." Rather than shuffling the problem from one place to another, it is time for Nottingham City Council to acknowledge its responsibility and take meaningful action to provide shelter and support for every member of its community.

Stanford University scientists cautioned against using the devices for dietary decisions. The study recommended companies release data on their device measurement methods.

Accuracy of seven wrist devices was tested on 60 volunteers walking, running, and cycling. Six of seven fitness devices accurately estimated heart rate, with an error rate under 5%. The Apple Watch, Fitbit Surge, Basis Peak, Microsoft Band, PulseOn, and MIP Alpha 2 performed well; the Samsung Gear S2 had the highest error rate at 6.8%.

But for tracking energy expenditure, the five devices offering this feature were highly inaccurate. None had an error rate below 20%; some, like the PulseOn, showed significantly higher errors, the US research team found.

Stanford University's Dr. Euan Ashley, a study co-author, urged public awareness of fitness devices' strengths and limitations. "People need to know that energy expenditure readings are rough estimates," Ashley stated. "If you go to the gym, a perceived 400-calorie loss might lead to an unwarranted 400-calorie intake." This is problematic if dietary choices rely on perceived calorie burn.

Heart rate measurement technology advanced rapidly in the past five or six years, but energy expenditure "isn't quite there yet," he added. Companies may not use heart rates in their calculations. Furthermore, calorie burn varies widely between individuals. For example, 10,000 steps can range from 400 to 800 kilocalories, based solely on height and weight, the study noted.

Lancaster University's Dr. David Ellis called calorie calculation a tricky business, relying on many factors: height, weight, body fat percentage, heart rate, and more. "However, since manufacturers don't share their constantly updated algorithms for calorie determination, the exact error source remains nearly impossible to pinpoint at this stage," he said.

Fitness trackers can positively impact activity levels, so absolute accuracy may not be vital if devices encourage more exercise. But recent trials suggest wearing them doesn't always lead to positive outcomes or improved weight loss.

The researchers urged companies to be more transparent, publishing their own accuracy test results. This would ensure the public and clinicians know the devices' limitations. The Stanford team has launched a website to share such data.

Earl Hooker was the undisputed king of the electric slide guitar, surpassing even legends like Robert Nighthawk, Muddy Waters, and Elmore James. Renowned for his extraordinary versatility, Hooker's playing consistently demonstrated both technical mastery and soulful expression. The two extended improvisations featured on this disc are truly outstanding, showcasing his improvisational genius. Although my all-time favorite Hooker track, "Tanya," and the classic "End of The Blues" are absent from this collection, the album compensates with a selection of rare and compelling Hooker recordings. "Hooker 'N' Steve" is especially enjoyable, bursting with energy and creativity. It’s a real shame that more people—especially guitarists—aren’t familiar with Earl Hooker’s immense talent and influence. While he does appear on one of the American Folk Blues Festival discs, that performance is unfortunately lackluster and fails to capture his abilities. I sincerely wish there were more footage documenting Hooker in his prime.

The hum of the ship is my constant companion. There are a million different sounds: the tinny drip of condensation in the vents, the soft whir of the recycling pumps, the distant grind of the hull adjusting to some new thermal stress. But by now they're all lullabies, blending into the background until the place sounds like my own heartbeat. The ship is trying to trick me into falling asleep, lure me into a state of safety and then vent me out the airlock. Just like I did to Bhagat and Kelly. Shoooooof. Pop. Pop. I imagine the faint red stains still blooming along the seams in the bulkhead, the ghost-shadows slicked across the silver floor.

I've considered disabling the auto grav, letting myself drift through the corridor where a discarded food packet still spins lazily, its contents smeared along the walls. Let my muscles wither, my bones hollow out. The thought of me flopping down on the ground like some jello-filled balloon makes me laugh, the sound echoing weirdly against the metal panels. I force myself to laugh harder, until the laughter cracks into a scream—raw and scraping, bouncing around the empty galley and slicing through the recycled air until my throat itches and begins to bleed.

"You're doing serious damage to your vocal chords." O'Brien says, spinning slowly in his chair. His uniform is still tucked neatly at the collar, but his skin has gone grey and papery, and dark fluid stains the floor beneath him. The smell is thick, clinging, overripe—a stench that floats just beneath the tang of disinfectant and plastic. I hate you, O'Brien. Hate that you are (were) commander. Hate how you got the fame for those first, careful steps on Europa. I hate the way your skin has started to liquify, how your eyes slide half-shut, how you've voided yourself all over the floor I scrubbed yesterday. 

"I did some serious damage to your head," I rasp-mumble at the corpse.

"Computer, show me Earth." The screen in front of O'Brien's body stirs, blinking once, twice, before swallowing itself into darkness. The soft blue light from the monitors simmers over O'Brien's lifeless features. Beyond him, the porthole is smeared with condensation, and stars make feeble attempts to shine in the black. I can't see Earth. Can't see home. The tears come before I can stop them—hot, thin lines against my cheeks as O'Brien's jaw jolts and I swear I hear him laugh.

The battle for the Mediterranean was excellent. They gave a good account by all sides with the missteps by the Italians, Germans and British. The thoughts andprocesses and how they came up with the battle plans made for an interesting program. El Alamein is a different story. There was so much repetition of subject matter that one could walk away for 20 minutes and not miss a thing. The Battles for Tunisia also had some repetition in it but not as bad as El Alamein. I say save your money and buy the single disc for The Battle for the Mediterranean.

That dumb dog...chasing me out here- Oh? What's that? Glancing at the stupid dog, who was being pulled inside the...whatever humans call it. A nest, or a den? You get the idea, I hopped off the porch. Instantly, my fur was soaked by mud. Ouch...maybe I shouldn't have jumped off the porch... Sitting down, I turned to inspect the bandages covering my back left leg; thanks to them, I'd been stuck there for my entire short life. Yawning involuntarily, I flopped down on the ground. This green thing...grass, is it? Is annoying. It makes my paws and nose itch, but this spot in particular was nice and warm. Oh...here comes that stupid mutt. It's old and not too large, but it's still twice my size. The dumb beast grabbed me by the annoyingly tight strap around my neck, making the bell jingle as it dragged me inside. I don't want to go inside yet...

Bruce Dickinson delivers another masterpiece after last year's excellent "Accident of Birth." Heavy yet melodic, its themes draw from poet and artist William Blake. Featuring former Iron Maiden guitarist Adrian Smith, Roy Z, Eddie Casillias, and David Ingraham, Bruce has found a fitting team, delivering outstanding musicianship and songwriting. Bruce's voice has never sounded better. This record should make Iron Maiden jealous! Favorites: "King In Crimson", "The Tower", and "Book of Thel". Even the CD art is gorgeous. Turn it up; it won't leave your CD player for a long time.

The epicenter of the Fukushima Daiichi nuclear accident still remains fairly traumatized. Japan derived 33 percent of its electric power from nuclear energy and was one of the most advanced countries in developing the technology. But it may be a long time before it embraces nuclear again. All but one of Tokyo Electric Power Company's (TEPCO) 17 reactors are now shut down and at least 8 are unlikely to reopen. The resulting shortage of electricity has hurt manufacturing output by 15-20% and led to the nation's first trade deficit in 31 years, reaching $32 billion in 2011.

So far there have been no deaths or illnesses directly attributed to radiation exposure, although two workers in their 60s died of heat stroke during the cleanup efforts in May 2011. Of the 31,000 people who have been evacuated from the 20-kilometer exclusion zone around Fukushima Daiichi, many have suffered from depression and at least 56 have committed suicide according to government statistics. Radiation levels in the most affected areas of Fukushima Prefecture are now about twice normal background levels (0.2-0.4 microsieverts per hour). People in various parts of the world, such as parts of Iran and Brazil, live with background radiation 1,000 times as high, but Japan's extremely strict standards of 1 millisievert per year prevent Fukushima evacuees from returning to their homes.

Strong anti-nuclear movements have become politically powerful, with groups like the Metropolitan Coalition Against Nukes organizing protests of over 100,000 people. Several leading newspapers including Asahi Shimbun and Mainichi Shimbun are keeping up a constant drumbeat of alarm. It is unlikely that Japan will be building any more reactors in the near future, with plans for 14 new reactors effectively canceled. Government officials have indicated, however, that Japanese industries will continue to sell their nuclear products abroad. Westinghouse (owned by Toshiba), Hitachi-GE Nuclear Energy, and Mitsubishi Heavy Industries are all world-leading manufacturers with active international projects.

"Ensure the acquisition of Mentos; these can be employed in the creation of rudimentary explosive devices." As the designated elementary schooler, my responsibility lay in the meticulous collection of all remaining confectionery within the toy store. This expedition marked the inaugural foraging mission I had been permitted to oversee by Tommy. Tommy, an individual of approximately twenty years, claimed to have returned 'from beyond the veil,' his purpose being our salvation subsequent to the demise of the adult population. A significant portion of the adolescent population, especially the female students, had also succumbed. "The board game section has been thoroughly appropriated, sir; they possessed the latest expansion pack for 'Settlers of Catan'," another elementary schooler dutifully reported. "Excellent. Tommy holds a particular fondness for those games; his contentment will be profound." I then proffered the child a Snickers bar as a form of recompense. "It is imperative that morale remains maximally elevated tonight. Our objective is to engage in a nocturnal prank involving the strategic application of toilet paper to the girls' encampment; a previous attempt resulted in the loss of three operatives to their projectile-launching devices." This recollection prompted a new directive. "Timothy!" I called out, addressing the boy who was clandestinely acquiring theatrical attire within the toy section. "Ensure the acquisition of the maximum possible quantity of protective gear, and expedite this process; we must avoid apprehension by the entities known as 'the wizards'." I had never personally encountered these 'wizards,' yet Tommy consistently affirmed their existence. They were described as unaffected males, clad in lengthy robes, who habitually abducted and consumed young boys exhibiting undue delay in the procurement of provisions. A distant rumbling sound was carried upon the wind. "The wizards are approaching!" I exclaimed. "All personnel are to immediately cease current activities and commence immediate evasion!" Items clattered to the floor as the juvenile scavengers hastened toward the exit. The youth designated for confectionery collection erroneously endeavored to abscond with his illicitly acquired provisions. The rumbling intensified significantly as they approached the exit, and the aforementioned youth commenced to fall behind the group. The remaining individuals failed to observe his predicament until they had traversed several city blocks. By that juncture, remedial action was no longer feasible. "Observe, Brother Matt," Brother Stephen inquired of me, "it appears to be another contingent of unaccompanied juveniles. Perchance we should ascertain their requirements?" "No, Brother Stephen," I responded, "they appear disinclined to engage in discourse with us. Upon further reflection, however, the trailing individual appears to have solely appropriated confectionery from the establishment. It is highly probable he possesses numerous dental caries of considerable severity. It would be prudent to escort him to the abbey for proper dental assessment and care."

By . Ken Ferris, Reuters . Six-times Olympic champion Usain Bolt broke 10 seconds for the first time this season when he won a 100 metres race under a closed roof at the National Stadium in Warsaw on Saturday. The Jamaican clocked 9.98 seconds into a slight breeze at the Kamila Skolimowska Memorial in front of a 20,000 crowd on a new track laid down the side of the infield. Bolt's first Polish outing was his third race this season as he builds up to Thursday's Diamond League meeting in Zurich and saw him set a new best time for the infrequently run 100 indoors. Leading the way: Jamaican sprinter Usain Bolt finished in first spot - ahead of his compatriot Sheldon Mitchell . Competition: Six-times Olympic champion Bolt knew he was in for a tough test against Mitchell . Winner: 100m sprinter Bolt picked up yet more silverware in Poland's Kamila Skolimowska Memorial . High five: Bolt touches hands with a mascot after competing in the men's 100m race in Warsaw . The world record holder beat the previous unofficial indoor best mark of 10.05 set by Namibian Frankie Fredericks in 1996. Bolt anchored Jamaica to 4x100m gold at the Commonwealth Games in Glasgow last month and last weekend won an exhibition race on Brazil's Copacabana beach in a time of 10.06. His nearest rival in Warsaw was fellow Jamaican Sheldon Mitchell, who ran 10.33, but in Zurich will face three sub-10 sprinters plus Britain's European 200m champion Adam Gemili. Added rivalry: British athlete Adam Gemili will come up against Bolt at Thursday's Diamond League meeting .

"You know, ever since causality broke down," he went on, "I realize computers are useless now, and it’s impossible to get reliable news, but mate, the world’s basically ended! Random things keep reversing through time unpredictably. Planes are falling out of the sky, there’s all sorts of strange implosions and explosions, objects appearing out of thin air. Just today, I woke up, my alarm clock bounced off my hand, and then it started ringing. Every twenty seconds there’s something bizarre happening. It’s like Y2K—except it’s actually happening." 

Shocked, I snapped my head around to check my time machine. It didn’t look like anything was reversing right then. Still, the ransacked homes, odd craters, and the generally wrecked scenery all suggested that something major had just happened. I turned back to my unusual companion, eyes wide, jaw hanging open. 

He laughed. "Haha, gotcha! No, seriously, I’m Steve. You sent me here from your own future—which technically is your past. Here," he said, pushing what looked like a shiny gold SD card into my palm, "just hop back into your time machine and hit the recall button. That’ll take you right back to your own time. The card has all the directions you’ll need. We actually built an island paradise opposite where Pangaea used to be—about 232 million years ago—after your first jump, and we invited everyone to become Panthalassians. The distance in information keeps any paradoxes at bay. You’ll have to start working on your own, at first, but we’ll be able to help more as things progress. Anyway, we’ve got endless time to catch up, and you’ve got a ton to do, so you’d better get a move on. Odds are, whatever you need will show up when you need it." 

"Wait... what about all this damage? Who… what… huh?" I stammered. 

He waved his hand. "Don’t worry. Everybody left, took what we needed, and mostly left the world alone to recover from pre-time-travel human contamination. Just go home, pour yourself a scotch, and get started."

Nano-engineered composites promise a boost in efficiency in next-generation solar cells
[2014-03-18] Carbon nanotubes are becoming increasingly attractive for photovoltaic solar cells as a replacement to silicon. Researchers at Umeå University in Sweden have discovered that controlled placement of the carbon nanotubes into nano-structures produces a huge boost in electronic performance. Their groundbreaking results are published in the prestigious journal Advanced Materials.
Carbon nanotubes, CNTs, are one dimensional nanoscale cylinders made of carbon atoms that possess very unique properties. For example, they have very high tensile strength and exceptional electron mobility, which make them very attractive for the next generation of organic and carbon-based electronic devices.
There is an increasing trend of using carbon based nanostructured materials as components in solar cells. Due to their exceptional properties, carbon nanotubes are expected to enhance the performance of current solar cells through efficient charge transport inside the device. However, in order to obtain the highest performance for electronic applications, the carbon nanotubes must be assembled into a well-ordered network of interconnecting nanotubes. Unfortunately, conventional methods used today are far from optimal which results in low device performance.
In a new study, a team of physicists and chemists at Umeå University have joined forces to produce nano-engineered carbon nanotubes networks with novel properties.
For the first time, the researchers show that carbon nanotubes can be engineered into complex network architectures, and with controlled nano-scale dimensions inside a polymer matrix.
“We have found that the resulting nano networks possess exceptional ability to transport charges, up to 100 million times higher than previously measured carbon nanotube random networks produced by conventional methods,” says Dr David Barbero, leader of the project and assistant professor at the Department of Physics at Umeå University.
The high degree of control of the method enables production of highly efficient nanotube networks with a very small amount of nanotubes compared to other conventional methods, thereby strongly reducing materials costs.
In a previous study (Applied Physics Letters, Volume 103, Issue 2, 021116 (2013)) the research team of David R. Barbero already demonstrated that nano-engineered networks can be produced onto thin and flexible transparent electrodes that can be used in flexible solar cells. These new results are expected to accelerate the development of next generation of flexible carbon based solar cells, which are both more efficient and less expensive to produce.
Nano-engineering of SWNT networks for enhanced charge transport at ultralow nanotube loading. D. R. Barbero, N. Boulanger, M. Ramstedt, Department of Chemistry, Umeå University, J. Yu, Department of Physics, Umeå University . Advanced Materials. DOI: 10.1002/adma.201305718.
For more information, please contact:
David Barbero, Department of Physics, Umeå University

Ever wonder why a stiletto heel hurts more than a sneaker when it steps on your toe? It's all about pressure.

In physics, pressure equals force divided by surface area. Same force, smaller surface = more pressure.

Here's the math: A 120-pound woman steps on your toe. In flat shoes, her weight spreads across 4 square inches of heel. That's 120 ÷ 4 = 30 pounds per square inch.

But a stiletto heel? That same 120 pounds concentrates on just one-eighth of a square inch. Now you're feeling 960 pounds per square inch. Ouch.

This works both ways. High heels hurt the wearer too—all that body weight pressing down on tiny contact points instead of spreading across the whole foot like in running shoes.

Small surface, big pain. That's pressure.

It's not everyday you stumble across a three-storey house built entirely from a stack of shipping containers, their vibrant, freshly painted metal surfaces gleaming under the Queensland sun. But in the Brisbane suburb of Graceville, 8km south west of the city, amidst the hushed hum of quiet, contemporary properties, that's exactly what you'll find. The three-storey home, designed by Australian builder Todd Miller, comprises 31 strategically placed containers, their stark, clean lines forming a bold silhouette. Filled with all the essential items of any home, plus a whole host of extras, the structure offers a surprisingly spacious and inviting environment.

Miller, of ZieglerBuild, describes the three-storey structure as the 'first of its kind' in Australia and has been built out of brand new, not recycled, containers, their pristine, unblemished surfaces hinting at their robust origins. It contains four bedrooms positioned over three levels, with the top floor completely dedicated to the master bedroom, a quiet sanctuary offering panoramic views. Built over 706 square metres, with a commanding presence on Jaora St, Graceville, it is possibly the most innovative of its kind in the country - with only one other residential property like it in Australia.

Stepping inside, you discover a myriad of carefully considered spaces: a mezzanine reading room with warm wood underfoot and the potential for the gentle rustle of turning pages; a pull-out queen wall bed in the rumpus room, inviting soft textures; an art study; a workshop where the faint scent of sawdust might linger; a gym space; a substantial water tank; and a sparkling saltwater pool, its cool, silky water inviting a refreshing dip with the gentle sound of lapping. The only other house which rivals it has been built from five containers, and is located on just a quarter the size of the land.

The home is built to flood regulations and will be auctioned on site on December 1. Mr Miller said it was the 'hardest house he has ever built,' recalling the taxing process where every single element required deep thought, a far cry from the usual *clatter* of pre-fabricated parts. He noted the constant design challenge: 'Normally you can just go and grab a pair of stairs from a steel company and whack it on the front of the house and you're done.' But here, 'everything I did it was actually very taxing.' He said whether you 'love it or hate it, as long as people think it's quite clever, then I've ticked the design brief.'

The three-storey home designed by Australian builder Todd Miller comprises of 31 strategically placed containers. It is Built over 706 square metres and is possibly the most innovative of its kind in the country. Designer and builder Todd Miller said it was the 'hardest house he has ever built.' It features three levels and includes a mezzanine reading room, a pull-out queen wall bed in the rumpus room, as well as this saltwater in-ground pool, its clear, blue water beckoning. The only other similar house in Australia has been built from five containers, and is located on just a quarter the size of the land.

Mr Miller said he wasn't taking any chances with the structure of the property and went with new, not recycled, shipping containers, their robust metal skins ensuring longevity. It contains four bedrooms positioned over three levels, with the top floor being entirely dedicated to the master bedroom, which has built-ins and an ensuite with cool, smooth tiles underfoot. The home is flood-proof and will be auctioned on site on December 1. The house is permanently secured in position with the middle and top layer completely insulated to adapt to the extreme Queensland conditions, creating a hushed interior that offers a cool, welcome respite from the outside heat. Mr Miller said because of the 'vortex' of the design and the way he has designed the glass, allowing refreshing breezes to flow, the home doesn't require air conditioning. The containers have eight layers of paint when they come out of the factory; however, Mr Miller has added another five layers of specialized paint, which reflects the sun's rays, keeping the exterior surfaces surprisingly cool to the touch despite the intense sun. Mr Miller hopes 'someone falls in love with the house' when it goes to auction in December. The high-end container home has been described as a luxurious property, with polished surfaces and inviting textures. It has a tropical feel to it, with the humid, sweet-scented air and lush green views, and is solar ready. Mr Miller said he wanted the house to feel more like a 'warehouse' – suggesting vast, open spaces and a raw, understated elegance – than an 'industrial design'.

I laughed as I realized I was right. The universe really was a sick and twisted place. It seemed to enjoy my misery, even after I was dead. I had died much like I had expected. They finally caught me after all those years, and I was executed on the spot. No trial. No death row. It's what I had expected. They wouldn't risk me getting away again. I'm sure they had told the public that I tried to shoot them or some other similar lie. It wasn't true, I had caused a lot of deaths during my life, but I never actually pulled the trigger. That was the kind of man I was. Obviously I expected to go to hell. It was something I had accepted long before I entered the field. I was a hacker. I didn't do it for the money. I didn't do it for the thrill. I always had a purpose, and it was never strictly moral. It was easy really with all the surveillance governments liked to implement. I'd plant an incriminating document in someone's computer, then leave a digital trail for the government leading them right to the smoking gun. I ruined legacies, toppled regimes, and started more than my fair share of civil wars this way. I created a network of contacts to gather my own information, and that's when the rumors about me started to form. It took them years before I was discovered, and decades before I was caught. I had the chance to get out, to disappear so many times. I never took them though. At the time I thought I was really changing the world for the better, in retrospect though, I wonder if I just enjoyed the chaos I brought. I was always a naive optimist(an optimist that expects to go to hell, weird right?). Let me explain. I knew what I was doing was wrong. I wasn't ignorant of the deaths or the pain I was causing. In my mind, someone had to do it and so I put it on myself. I still felt the guilt of my actions, but I saw myself as some sort of martyr. I didn't just expect to go to hell, *I almost wanted to*, as a sort of confirmation that I had accomplished my goal. I never expected not to get my wish. When I died god made sure I didn't get my wish. The bastard took me to heaven. When I saw him, I demanded to go to hell. When he refused I began to plead, and then I began to do every conceivable thing I could think of to get myself cast into hell. He wouldn't do it, refused to give me satisfaction of letting me ease my guilt. He decided that I would hold that guilt, and my hell would be internal. In order to prevent me from causing too much havoc in heaven, he assigned two angels to me. They follow me around now, as guards of sorts. They fix the havoc I cause, yet never scold me. They never give me so much as a dirty look. They are the epitome of kindness. I still go and beg to be sent down everyday. He just smiles and tells me I don't deserve it. He knows that nothing in hell could cause me nearly as much pain as the guilt of not paying for my sins does. This was the one punishment I never expected. Physically I already died, but mentally and emotionally I'm dying all over again. The worst part is I know it won't stop. Ever.

"He threw you out of a window." She sighed, doe-eyed. "From the conference room on the sixteenth floor of the Chandler Building." She closed her eyes and bit her lips. The psychologist, Dr. McGinnigan, squinted and lowered his voice. "You could have died. That's a two hundred-foot drop."  

She trembled, gripping the edge of her chair, legs jittering in restraints for several moments before opening her eyes and fixing him with a gaze so guileless and trusting that he thought of his own daughter, Sara—her wide eyes framed by chocolate curls—and wondered how he would feel if she ever spoke to him with this sort of naked vulnerability.  

"Have you ever been in love, Dr. McGinnigan?" Her voice snapped him out of the memory, and he cleared his throat.  
"Yes. Of course."  
She shook her head and looked away at the pale blue wall covered in safety glass. "I mean, really in love, Doc. With someone who makes it easy to breathe. Someone you can be ugly around, or terrified, or just… yourself. No pretending. No hiding."  

She trailed off, and as he watched her—Jessica Langford, age twenty-four, no prior convictions before any of this—he saw only a young woman who was simply in love. It didn't matter to her that her lover, Bernard Voss, was a serial killer convicted on twelve counts of homicide, or that the world called her feelings 'unnatural,' 'disturbing,' even 'sick.' To Jessica, her feelings were pure—more honest and vivid, perhaps, than the daily rituals of what society labeled 'normal' relationships.  

She wasn't asking anyone to understand. She didn't judge those who condemned her, so why did they judge her? Jessica was old enough to make her *own* choices, accept the consequences of her *own* actions, and live—or die—with the results. She was, impossibly, *happy*. Was that such a crime? Why couldn't they just leave her alone?  

Dr. McGinnigan exhaled, closed her file, and nodded to the two uniformed guards outside the interrogation room window. "We're done for today. We'll continue this next session," he said as he rose. "Take her back to her cell and sedate her. I don't want another incident like yesterday."

Loved the venue!  It was my first time attending last night and it was for Knotfest! It was easy to find, easy to park and getting in was a breeze. I paid extra for reserved lawn seating which came with a fast past, I didnt use it. I did come later after the first show, so maybe that's why it went fast. They have a few locations for merch and it's well organized. Lots of food and beverage options. Restrooms were easy to find, not a long wait. The reserved lawn seating was so worth it! Very spacious, great view of the screen and stage, the sound was perfect!  Not having to worry about bringing something to sit on or purchasing something was great. Leaving was easy too, but finding my car was difficult. The reason why I'm only giving this 4 stars is because cigarette smoke was a BIG problem for me. I had to leave early because the smoke started to irritate my lungs and made me nauseous. I don't know if there are no smoking areas, but I hope there is because I want to come to this venue more often. Please make reserved lawn seating a non smoking area!

This album creates a romantic atmosphere and is ideal for candlelit dinners with a loved one. Karen's vocals are spine-tingling, and Richard's arrangements truly tug at the heartstrings. Though Karen's life was tragically brief, the musical legacy she left behind continues to touch millions of people worldwide, especially during Christmas with the many seasonal songs the Carpenters recorded. This album makes an excellent gift for holidays, birthdays, or any occasion, and is a real gem for any CD collection. What gift is better than the gift of music—especially good music? Karen Carpenter's memory lives on through the beautiful music she and her brother created.
