"Honey?"Jenny hollered from the den."What are you making in there? It smells terrific!" "It's a surprise, love! Just you wait and see!" I heard myself yelling back. She laughed. "Another surprise? It's been one after another ever since you got back from your trip! Who are you and what have you done with my husband?" It took the question literally and I could feel it tense up before it made me reply, "I'm the same ol' Harold you fell in love with! I swear!" She laughed at that, and it was a laughter more genuine than any I'd managed to coax out of her over the years.

The patent, #7,657,941, is entitled "Hardware-based anti-virus system," is awarded to inventor Oleg V. Zaitsev (Technology Expert at Kaspersky Lab) and assigned to Kaspersky. The abstract reads:
An anti-virus (AV) system based on a hardware-implemented AV module for curing infected computer systems and a method for updating AV databases for effective curing of the computer system. The hardware-based AV system is located between a PC and a disk device. The hardware-based AV system can be implemented as a separate device or it can be integrated into a disk controller. An update method of the AV databases uses a two-phase approach. First, the updates are transferred to from a trusted utility to an update sector of the AV system. Then, the updates are verified within the AV system and the AV databases are updated. The AV system has its own CPU and memory and can be used in combination with AV application.So it seems this device is an actual separate computer running an embedded AV application. While the press release and abstract emphasize that the AV functionality doesn't strictly need a software counterpart running in the host system, it does need host software in order to update itself, because the AV hardware won't have network access. This update application will need to be trusted and hardened against attack.
The difficulty of detecting rootkits once they have installed does call for unconventional measures. Whether a hardware approach is truly more effective remains to be seen. If the device is just an AV system running below the level of the rootkit then the improvement will be small, as it will still only operate as well as the signature process allows. If the fact that the device is running below rootkits allows it to run heuristic tests which are better capable of detecting rootkit behavior then the difference could be substantial.
There is another advantage to hardware-based AV: Because the device has its own CPU and memory and minimal software running on the host PC, the performance impact on the PC will be lessened. But in fact, this device can not be a complete security solution, since it can only monitor disk operations. Modern security suites also monitor network connections, for example.
Originally posted to the PCMag.com security blog, Security Watch.

"You're one lucky son of a gun," the doctor told me. I wanted to thank my mom and dad—after all, their genes saved my life. But for what? I had no kids to pass this so-called blessing to. Only a month later did I see the truth: it wasn’t a blessing at all. Why me? Why should I be spared when everyone else suffers? It isn’t fair. They come to me for answers—as if I hold solutions I never asked for. I didn’t ask for any of this. If I’d had a choice, I would have joined them. It would have been easier, cleaner. I would have faded into the background as just another name, another casualty, instead of being forced onto a pedestal as their beacon of hope. The weight of their hope is crushing. Trust me. I am He, with a capital "H"—not by choice but by circumstance.

They called themselves "The Datahans," taking my last name as their banner. They wrote a sacred text—"The Book of Datahan"—all for me, or at least for the idea of me. Those lucky—or unlucky—enough to live a little longer, to watch as the end comes slowly, built a religion around my survival. But none of their myths or scriptures bear any resemblance to who I am or what I feel. I have nothing special; I am merely a vessel of inherited luck, not divinity. If only they realized that, they might stop searching for meaning where there is only accident.

The last one, writhing in agony, looked up—not at me, but at the ceiling or the heavens, I couldn’t tell—and cried, "Why have you forsaken us?" The words echoed of something ancient, something sacred, though only half-remembered. She wasn’t talking to me, not really. But her pain ripped through the room. The guilt overwhelmed me—I don’t deserve it, but it clings to me, unrelenting.

How could any god endure this? To bear not only the suffering, but the hope and desperation of the millions below? Is it any wonder Nietzsche said, "God is dead"? If divinity means enduring this unbearable weight, maybe he was right—maybe, faced with this suffering, even a god would choose oblivion. Perhaps, to be spared is not a blessing, but the heaviest burden of all.

A woman was disgusted to find raw chicken inside her 'healthy' chicken McWrap during her first McDonald's visit in nearly two years. Shocked Gizela Tahuri posted a disgusting image online of the uncooked chicken she found inside her wrap when she visited a McDonald’s store at Deception Bay, 32 kilometres north of Brisbane, in Queensland. ‘So much for healthy choices McDonalds when you serve RAW chicken. I haven't eaten McDonalds for nearly 2 years and now I know why. Mmm I'm not loving it,’ she wrote on Facebook. Scroll down for video . 'So much for healthy choices': Gizela Tahuri bit into this raw chicken at a McDonald's store in Queensland . The personal trainer told Courier Mail she probably had 'two mouthfuls' of the spicy mayo crispy chicken wrap before realising it wasn't cooked. 'I thought the chicken was really soft and it looked raw,' Ms Tahuri said. A McDonald’s spokesperson told Daily Mail Australia: ‘We are disappointed that this has happened. 'We take food safety very seriously and have strict processes and systems in place. An investigation is currently underway with the restaurant, and we encourage the customer to contact us to help us to investigate fully.' The chicken McWrap is advertised as boasting 'a strip of succulent, marinated and crumbed chicken'. It is accompanied by 'fresh Australian lettuce and creamy mayonnaise' and is 'carefully' hand wrapped in a tortilla. McDonald's have promised to investigate 'fully' what happened at the store in Deception Bay .

Jimmy’s older brother, Derek, is at work, not due back for hours. Their parents are in the city for the weekend, leaving Jimmy home alone with his dog, Bone. Eighteen and without a girlfriend, Jimmy suddenly feels an "itch." Home alone, he figures it’s the perfect opportunity for a little "alone time."

He looks under his bed for his old Playboys, but then remembers his mother found and disposed of them weeks ago. Daytime TV offers nothing of interest, and with no internet yet, he’s at a loss. Theoretically, he could walk twenty minutes to the corner store for a new magazine, but he’s too embarrassed to buy one in public, especially since one of his mother’s friends works there.

In a last-ditch effort, he decides to check his brother’s room, certain Derek must have some adult reading material stashed away. He reaches for Derek’s door, then hesitates. Derek hates it when Jimmy enters his room when he’s not home. But with Derek at work, Jimmy is confident that if he puts everything back, Derek will be none the wiser.

He looks under the bed – nothing. He checks the dresser – nothing. Opening the closet, he rummages through a pile of junk on the floor and comes across a strange, small plastic device, like a tiny TV. It’s not a Nintendo. On its back, a bitten apple logo. Jimmy examines it. He spots two buttons. Pressing one, a picture of Bone and the time appear. 12:34 am. Is this some new computer? Jimmy had never seen one, not on anyone, not in stores.

David Strang highlighted the case of a prisoner at HMP Kilmarnock who shoplifted in the hope he would be caught and returned to jail.
The man had been living in a tent for eight weeks following his release.
The Scottish Prison Service said significant investment had been made in "throughcare" for inmates.
Mr Strang said inspectors had spoken to the man caught shoplifting, who told them no accommodation had been available to him after he was released from prison.
The former inmate wanted to return to prison because he believed it was the only way for him to access a dry bed, warmth and shelter.
Mr Strang acknowledged that returning to the community from prison was "challenging" and said many individuals needed more direct support than was always available to them.
He has called for all agencies involved in the reintegration of prisoners back into their communities to work collaboratively "in order to ensure the best possible outcomes".
Referring to the specific example of the prisoner, who had previously been at HMP Barlinnie, he said: "These are not the circumstances we want people leaving prison in 21st Century Scotland to have to face.
"However, it is not a situation that can be resolved by the prison service alone.
"This requires a dedicated and co-ordinated response by all those involved in supporting people in the criminal justice system and beyond."
The chief inspector was speaking as he launched his full report of an inspection of Kilmarnock prison in East Ayrshire, which was carried out in November.
He said it was "disappointing" there were no dedicated throughcare support officers at HMP Kilmarnock, adding that prisoners were denied levels of support available at other prisons in Scotland.
The prison, which can hold about 500 people, is one of two operated by private sector organisations on contract to the Scottish Prison Service (SPS).
The report concluded the institution was secure and well-ordered, with the majority of staff and prisoners saying they felt safe.
Inspectors graded the prison satisfactory or acceptable across all main criteria.
But they also noted the prison contract itself was seen by many as "a barrier to progress and a reason for inflexibility and resistance to change".
An SPS spokeswoman said: "SPS's significant investment in throughcare support officers means that those leaving custody are now supported in planning for their release at a much earlier stage in their sentence, in order to ensure that they have access to key services on release."
Welcoming the wider report, she said: "SPS also welcomes HMIP's comments that Kilmarnock is a safe prison, with a good range of educational and vocational opportunities available to those in our care.
"The report also notes that HMP Kilmarnock offers a well-considered range of purposeful activities to match the ability, preference and age of most prisoners."
She added that the SPS did not share the view the contract may inhibit the delivery of services to inmates at Kilmarnock.
A Scottish government spokeswoman said: "The inspection findings reflect our position, that public services must respond to the needs of people in the justice system, and particularly those being released from prison.
"Anybody presenting as homeless when they leave prison must be given support by the relevant local authority, but we recognise that housing service provision is complex.
"We are supporting ongoing work between justice and housing partners to develop a consistent and shared approach across the country."
Alison Watson, from Shelter Scotland, said the provision of stable, safe and affordable homes for former inmates was key to breaking the offending cycle.
"When in prison, people often lose their accommodation because of an inability to pay rent whilst serving their sentence or through a family break-up," she said.
"Many don't have a job to go back to upon release, making finding and maintaining a home very difficult.
"It doesn't need to be this way and with the right advice and support, ex-offenders can go on to lead successful lives and contribute a great deal to our society."

Looking ahead as she walked from the room, leaving behind her shocked faces. She felt her legs shaking slightly, but determined she wanted to get away from there as fast as she could, but with as much dignity as she could muster. Hands clenched at her sides, head high, back straight, Laura walked away from her friends. Today had been the last straw and the camel's back was broken, and Laura had for once, and finally, reacted. For so long she had been what everyone else wanted, obeyed most times without question, keeping her own thoughts to herself. Too often she had felt hurt and anger, but did she ever respond or tell them? No. It was not in her to be mean, or not till now, and she knew what had just happened was not mean, but a long overdue wake up call for her precious 'friends'. Now Laura would be true to herself. She would be herself without thought, and not be what others wanted. In that she betrayed herself, belied who she was. She now knew that in the end she had herself, that people came and went. To that end she would be who she was, live how she chose, and if her friends could not accept that then they never really knew her.

David Strang highlighted a troubling case that illustrates the challenges facing former prisoners: a man at HMP Kilmarnock deliberately shoplifted in the hope he would be caught and returned to jail. After his release, the former inmate had been living in a tent for eight weeks because no accommodation was available to him. He wanted to return to prison because he believed it was the only way to access a dry bed, warmth and shelter.

This case exemplifies broader systemic issues with prisoner reintegration. Mr Strang acknowledged that returning to the community from prison is "challenging" and said many individuals need more direct support than is always available to them. Referring to this specific example, he said: "These are not the circumstances we want people leaving prison in 21st Century Scotland to have to face."

However, Mr Strang emphasized that solutions require collaborative effort. "This requires a dedicated and co-ordinated response by all those involved in supporting people in the criminal justice system and beyond," he said, noting that "it is not a situation that can be resolved by the prison service alone." He has called for all agencies involved in the reintegration of prisoners back into their communities to work collaboratively "in order to ensure the best possible outcomes."

These comments came as the chief inspector launched his full report of an inspection of Kilmarnock prison in East Ayrshire, carried out in November. The report revealed specific deficiencies in support services at the facility. Mr Strang said it was "disappointing" there were no dedicated throughcare support officers at HMP Kilmarnock, adding that prisoners were denied levels of support available at other prisons in Scotland.

Despite these concerns, the inspection found positive aspects of the prison's operations. The report concluded the institution was secure and well-ordered, with the majority of staff and prisoners saying they felt safe. Inspectors graded the prison satisfactory or acceptable across all main criteria. The prison, which can hold about 500 people, is one of two operated by private sector organisations on contract to the Scottish Prison Service (SPS).

Nevertheless, structural challenges persist. Inspectors noted that the prison contract itself was seen by many as "a barrier to progress and a reason for inflexibility and resistance to change."

In response to the criticism, an SPS spokeswoman defended the organization's efforts: "SPS's significant investment in throughcare support officers means that those leaving custody are now supported in planning for their release at a much earlier stage in their sentence, in order to ensure that they have access to key services on release." Welcoming the wider report, she highlighted that "SPS also welcomes HMIP's comments that Kilmarnock is a safe prison, with a good range of educational and vocational opportunities available to those in our care." She added that the SPS did not share the view that the contract may inhibit service delivery to inmates at Kilmarnock.

Government officials also acknowledged the challenges while outlining existing support mechanisms. A Scottish government spokeswoman said: "The inspection findings reflect our position, that public services must respond to the needs of people in the justice system, and particularly those being released from prison. Anybody presenting as homeless when they leave prison must be given support by the relevant local authority, but we recognise that housing service provision is complex. We are supporting ongoing work between justice and housing partners to develop a consistent and shared approach across the country."

Housing experts emphasize that accommodation is fundamental to successful reintegration. Alison Watson, from Shelter Scotland, said the provision of stable, safe and affordable homes for former inmates was key to breaking the offending cycle. "When in prison, people often lose their accommodation because of an inability to pay rent whilst serving their sentence or through a family break-up," she explained. "Many don't have a job to go back to upon release, making finding and maintaining a home very difficult."

However, Watson remained optimistic about potential solutions: "It doesn't need to be this way and with the right advice and support, ex-offenders can go on to lead successful lives and contribute a great deal to our society."

On February 7, Pope Francis issued a decree allowing the Congregation for the Causes of the Saints to advance the canonization cases of several Catholics at different stages in the process.

Paul Yun Ji-chung, a layperson, and 123 other Korean Catholics who lived from 1791 to 1888 were acknowledged as martyrs for refusing to abandon their Catholic beliefs. Born into nobility, Yun Ji-chung embraced Catholicism at 28 and later influenced his mother and relatives to convert as well.

Three years following his 1787 baptism, Yun Ji-chung's bishop banned ancestral rites in 1790. The next year, he angered government officials by refusing to perform Confucian ancestral ceremonies at his mother's funeral, calling them unreasonable.

For maintaining his religious convictions, he was executed by beheading on December 8, 1791, at age 32.

Franciscan priest Father Francesco Zirano also received recognition as "servant of God" for his martyrdom. He met a horrific end in Algiers in 1603, being flayed alive.

Father Zirano had gone to Algiers to free his cousin from pirate captivity and slavery, but was quickly imprisoned and tortured upon arrival.

A case of mistaken identity led to his death sentence. He refused to abandon his faith and used his final days to support fellow Christian prisoners. Father Zirano was killed on January 25, 1603.

Friday's announcement also declared "heroic virtue" for three individuals who died in the 1900s: Jesus Maria Echavarria y Aguirre, who served as Bishop of Saltillo, Mexico and established the Catechist Sisters of Guadalupe before his 1954 death; Father Faustino Ghilardi from the Order of Friars Minor, who passed away in 1937; and Sister Maria Rocio of Jesus Crucified from the Congregation of the Sisters of the Love of God, who died in 1956.

The Pope approved moving forward with canonization proceedings for more than one hundred lay Catholics who faced persecution for their beliefs, along with one bishop, one nun, and two priests.

Obama does an amazing job at detailing his life story and connecting it to his continued struggle to find his true identity. Along the way he found his true purpose by helping others achieve change for the better. He is very open with the reader about his experiences and defeats. The writing is so well done that I often have to sit back and remind myself that this man is a lawyer and politician and not an author. He astonishes me with his intellect and ability to communicate with commoners and high-brows alike. Throughout his recollections I am continually reminded that his sole focus in life is to help out the less fortunate and to create a bigger middle class.

I glanced at the Twitch chat. As usual, it was chaotic—messages streaming in nonstop, the viewer count climbing past 10,000 and still going up. The bottom left corner of my screen was constantly flooded with “Kappa” and other random emoticons, making it almost impossible to keep up. I felt more and more confused: Why was all this attention focused on me? After all, my job couldn’t be duller. Every time I updated an Excel spreadsheet or sent a routine email, the chat would explode with reactions. It seemed ridiculous, but I couldn’t deny that I kind of enjoyed it. The audience was supportive, even if there was an undercurrent of irony in what they said. Or maybe there wasn’t—honestly, I couldn’t tell.

Before it was even 10am, there were already 12,000 viewers—an all-time high—and still, I didn’t really understand why. Right then, my email pinged. As I opened my boss’s message, the viewer count reached 13,000.

“Dear John,”  
14,000.  
“Due to unforeseen circumstances,”  
15,000.  
“we’re going to have to let you go.”  
17,000.  
“Please gather your things,”  
20,000.  
“and exit the building immediately.”  
24,000.  
“Kind regards,”  
32,000.  
“Justin Bough.”  
48,000.

I sat in shock. After 10 years of loyal service—never missing a day, never stepping out of line—I was being dismissed without warning. The Twitch chat was now scrolling so quickly that it was impossible to read, with over 100,000 people typing at once. Yet, through the chaos, a single phrase stood out, repeated again and again:  

“BURN THIS PLACE TO THE GROUND.”

I still don’t know why I was chosen, or why I was fired. But as I packed up my belongings, one thing was clear: something was about to change forever.

VOLUME CONTROL AT THE SYNAPSE [MURTHY LAB]
June 22nd, 2012
Venki Murthy [left] and Geoff Lau

**Main Finding: Neural networks automatically adjust their "volume control" by regulating how much inhibitory neurotransmitter gets packed into synaptic vesicles, maintaining optimal brain activity levels.**

You can't miss it in the news these days: neuroscientists are excited about anatomical reconstruction of neural connectivity. In fact, the term "connectomics" is a frequently-edited entry in Wikipedia. The overall goal of these endeavors is to identify all the synapses made by all the neurons to make a diagram of who is connected to whom. Although the existence of particular connections is an important determinant of the circuit, knowledge of the actual strength of connections is just as vital to account for the real time physiology of the relevant neural network. In this study, we discovered a key mechanism that controls synaptic strength: neural activity regulates how much inhibitory neurotransmitter (GABA) gets packaged into synaptic vesicles through control of the enzyme GAD67.

As any textbook (or Wikipedia) will tell us, a typical neural circuit consists of excitatory and inhibitory neurons. In vertebrates, the major excitatory and inhibitory transmitters -- glutamate and GABA respectively -- are structurally related. In fact, only one biochemical step is required to convert glutamate into GABA, a step catalyzed by the enzyme glutamic acid decarboxylase (GAD). Of the two isoforms of this enzyme present in mammals, GAD67 is essential and its deletion is lethal in mice. Our study asked two questions about this important enzyme: (1) Is GAD67 rate-limiting for conversion of glutamate to GABA and its subsequent transport into synaptic vesicles? (2) Is GAD67 expression regulated by neural activity as a means to control filling of vesicles with GABA?

Since mice that lack GAD67 die at birth, we studied synaptic transmission in cultured neurons from these mice. We used patch-clamp electrophysiology to record elementary synaptic events (called quanta) caused by spontaneous release of transmitter from single synaptic vesicles. Quantal events were significantly smaller in neurons lacking GAD67. We then used a pharmacological trick with a weak competitive antagonist of postsynaptic GABA receptors to show that the reduced amplitude was due to less GABA released (rather than reduced postsynaptic reception). Cytosolic GABA concentration, therefore, is a rate-limiting step in the steady-state filling of vesicles. Our results also indicate that the other isoform, GAD65, is unable to substitute fully for the loss of GAD67.

If GAD67 can determine cytosolic GABA concentration, and therefore affect how much of it is packaged into vesicles, is this enzyme's level a target for regulation? We subjected culture networks to persistent reduction of activity by blocking action potentials for two days. Inactivity reduced the levels of GAD67 and GABA, as well as the strength of inhibition (quantal amplitude) in wildtype cultures -- a compensatory response. This homeostatic behavior was strongly attenuated in networks of neurons lacking GAD67. **Our key discovery: neural activity positively regulates GAD67 expression, which synthesizes more GABA, resulting in stronger inhibitory transmission that dampens network activity -- creating an automatic "volume control" system.** Such feedback loops implementing automatic gain control appear to be rather common in biology.

Since a single inhibitory neuron can make synapses on hundreds of other neurons, changing its output strength through cell-wide changes in GABA concentration will have major effects on the excitability of a network. Interestingly, alterations in expression of molecules like GAD67 have been found in neuropsychiatric disorders such as schizophrenia. Our study provides a framework for testing aberrant homeostatic regulation of GAD67 and inhibitory transmission in models of neuropsychiatric disorders.

Cover image courtesy of Geoff Lau. A coronal slice of mouse hippocampus that was cultured in vitro for 14 d. GFP (green) marks all interneurons expressing glutamic acid decarboxylase 67 (GAD67). Staining against parvalbumin is red and nuclei are labeled with DAPI (blue). Activity deprivation for 2 d with tetrodotoxin reduces the expression of GAD67 and GFP reporter.

Visiting an Urgent Care usually is not a great experience, often characterized by frustratingly long wait times, impersonal staff interactions, and sometimes a sense of being rushed through the diagnostic process. However, I've had the occasion to visit this particular center a few times over the past year, and each visit has genuinely impressed me with its consistently high standard of service.

The registration process here is remarkably easy and efficient; on my last visit, I was able to complete the necessary paperwork quickly at a self-service kiosk, and the front desk staff were incredibly helpful in clarifying a few insurance details. I've always been seen remarkably quickly, often within 15-20 minutes of arrival, which is a stark contrast to the hour-plus waits I've endured elsewhere. More importantly, the medical team has consistently provided accurate diagnoses, leading to effective treatment plans. For instance, my child's ear infection was identified and treated promptly, preventing it from worsening, and my own persistent cough was correctly diagnosed as a mild bronchitis, with the prescribed medication bringing relief within days. The doctors and nurses are not only knowledgeable but also empathetic, taking the time to explain the condition and treatment options clearly.

This level of efficiency and care stands in sharp relief to a prior experience at another Urgent Care facility nearby. There, the wait was absolutely horrendous, stretching well over two hours, leaving me feeling increasingly unwell and frustrated. To compound the inconvenience, after finally being seen, I was then told I needed to be sent to their *other* facility across town specifically for an x-ray. When you're feeling your worst, battling a fever or severe discomfort, the last thing you need is that kind of arduous runaround and fragmented care, forcing you to travel further just to complete a basic diagnostic step.

No one ever looks forward to getting sick or needing medical attention on short notice. However, knowing that a reliable, efficient, and compassionate option exists makes a significant difference. Because of these consistently positive experiences – from the smooth intake to the quick, accurate diagnosis, and comprehensive care all under one roof – this center has become the unequivocal choice for my family whenever an urgent medical need arises.

The National Air and Space Museum in Washington, DC opened on July 1, 1976. The official ribbon-cutting began with a signal sent from the Viking 1 spacecraft approaching Mars. Relayed to NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in California, the signal arrived at the Museum via ground lines. It activated a mechanical “arm,” causing the ribbon to split in two. At the ribbon cutting, President Gerald Ford called the Museum “a perfect birthday present from the American people to themselves.” The opening was one of the highlights of America’s Bicentennial Celebration.
Walking through the doors on opening day, visitors found ten acres of exhibition space comprising 26 distinct galleries. They entered the building through the Milestones of Flight gallery, where they found the icons of the age of flight, from the Wright Flyer, the world’s first airplane, to Charles Lindbergh’s Spirit of St. Louis and the Bell X-1 that carried Captain Chuck Yeager through the speed of sound for the first time, to the Mercury Friendship 7 spacecraft that astronaut John Glenn rode into orbit, and the Apollo 11 Command Module, Columbia, that brought Michael Collins, Neil Armstrong and Edwin “Buzz” Aldrin home from the Moon.
The success of the Smithsonian’s National Air and Space Museum exceeded expectations. Record crowds beat a path to the Museum from the first day the doors opened and have kept coming unabated year after year. The five millionth visitor crossed the threshold only six months after opening day.
But there was little time to rest on post-1976 laurels. In the early years, the Museum kept a fast pace, offering new exhibit galleries (nearly one per year), new movies, and public programs. In 1982, the groundbreaking Black Wings exhibit opened, exploring the long struggle of African Americans to take to the sky. In 1996 we opened a new interactive gallery called How Things Fly. It offers imaginative interactive devices that help people of all ages grasp the basic principles of flight, and entertaining talks and demonstrations on scientific principles. The gallery remains one of the most popular stops for children who visit the Museum.
In addition to its exceptional collection of artifacts and engaging displays, the Museum offers public programs geared toward visitors of all ages; teachers and students; life-long learners; enthusiasts and experts. Many of the most noteworthy names in aviation and space history have lectured at the Museum, from astronaut Neil Armstrong to aviation pioneer Chuck Yeager, and the Museum continues to host prominent scientists, engineers, pioneers in aviation, and leaders in space exploration year after year. For a list of upcoming lectures, see the calendar of events.
For more information about the National Mall building, see this fact sheet.

BBC Wales revealed five Welsh Tory MPs have urged George Osborne to reverse the announcement, made in November.
They said the policy change was "disrespectful" and breached the Conservatives' 2015 election manifesto.
But Cardiff North MP Craig Williams said there was no "big row" and AMs needed "some fiscal responsibility".
Welsh Secretary Stephen Crabb has said devolving income tax powers would mean "better government for Wales".
Some of the MPs opposed to the move hope to raise the issue at a meeting with Mr Osborne on Wednesday.
The five - Byron Davies, Chris Davies, David Davies, James Davies and David Jones - have written to him on the issue
"This is a line in the sand for many of us," one MP said.
Another said it would be "very embarrassing" for the Conservative UK government if almost half of the party's 11 Welsh MPs were to vote against the measure.
The letter said that during the general election campaign Tory candidates "were able to reassure people that the assembly would not get tax raising powers without the consent of the people of Wales".
"To drop the commitment to a referendum would put us in breach of a clear manifesto commitment," it continued.
The Welsh Conservative manifesto for the 2015 general election made reference to an "expectation that the Welsh government will hold a referendum on income tax powers".
The five MPs said people in Wales should not be "treated differently in this respect from the people of Scotland, who did have a separate vote on the issue of tax varying powers" in the 1997 referendum on devolution.
"We frankly feel that to impose such powers without a referendum would be disrespectful to the Welsh people," they added.
Mr Williams responded: "You're going to have cases with business rates being devolved to areas like Manchester, where Manchester City Council might have more fiscal responsibility than the entire Welsh nation," Mr Williams said.
"It's about time they [the assembly] had some responsibility for income of money as well as spending it on their pet projects."
In December, Welsh Secretary Stephen Crabb said he thought public opinion was "fluid" on the matter, but there were "very, very strong reasons for why we need to press ahead" for Wales and the Welsh economy to get stronger.
Splits within the Welsh Conservative group in Cardiff Bay on the devolution of income tax led to the party's leader Andrew RT Davies sacking four members of his front bench team in February 2014.
They were re-instated in the summer.
Mr Davies said he would use income tax powers to take 5p off the higher rate and 1p off the basic rate in Wales if he was first minister.
A spokesman for the Welsh Conservatives said: "The position of the UK government and chancellor is very clear on this matter and we have long called for the Welsh government to be more accountable for the money it raises and spends."
A Plaid Cymru spokeswoman said: "This latest development shows the Tories' true colours when it comes to empowering Wales.
"They support transferring substantial powers, including policing, to English cities without a referendum and yet are trying to block Wales having powers over small proportion of income tax which could be of benefit to the Welsh economy."

As far as study aids go, this one falls short. While it is concise and simple, it fails to provide meaningful assistance for exam preparation. Using this book feels no different from simply reading a basic textbook—it doesn’t add value or help you truly grasp the material. At times, I found it practically useless. For example, after reading the UCC (especially 2-207) sections, I felt no more prepared than I did after class.

If you’re seeking a genuinely effective study resource, I strongly recommend Emmanuel’s and/or the Emmanuel Flash Cards instead. Those tools offer clear charts and memorable acronyms that help you organize the material and efficiently tackle exam questions. This book, on the other hand, completely lacks those helpful features.

Ultimately, if you’re looking to purchase only one guide—and want a real study aid rather than just another textbook—do not buy this one. However, if you haven’t been attending your first-year classes and need a straightforward overview, it might serve as a basic catch-up. For anyone else, better options are available and well worth your investment.

Andrew Moran, 31, from Salford, Greater Manchester, was arrested at a luxury villa in Alicante last week.
He was convicted of armed robbery in his absence after fleeing a Burnley court in 2009.
He faces investigation over drugs and weapons found at his house, Spanish police said.
He could also face charges of escaping from police who tracked him down in Murcia in November by ramming two police vehicles and driving the wrong way down a motorway.
Moran was placed on the most wanted list after leaping from the dock and assaulting four security guards during his trial at Burnley Crown Court in February 2009.
He was found guilty, alongside Stephen Devalda, of taking part in an armed robbery in which Royal Mail guards were threatened with a gun, machete and baseball bat in Colne, Lancashire, in May 2005.
One of the guards was assaulted before the offenders escaped with Â£25,000.
Devalda, 29, also from Salford, had also been on the run but was captured in Spain in March 2011.
He was later jailed by a judge at Preston Crown Court, for nine years and eight months.

The Upper Midwest is about to get three days of September -- in July. An unseasonal invasion of cool Canadian air could put lows in the 40s -- about 10 to 15 degrees below normal -- in parts of North Dakota, Minnesota, Wisconsin and Michigan into Wednesday. Chicago, too, will be wondering what month it is, with lows in the 50s and highs Tuesday and Wednesday in the low 70s. The reason: Last week's Typhoon Neoguri created some ripples in the jet stream, causing it to dip farther south than it normally does this time of year. "It's not weird to get a dip in the jet stream, but it is weird to get it in July, dipping this far south," CNN meteorologist Jennifer Gray said. "So we are getting those cooler than normal temperatures across the Great Lakes region." Some examples: . â€¢ Duluth, Minnesota, will see lows in the upper 40s Tuesday and Wednesday, with highs in the upper 60s and low 70s. Monday's expected high, 59, is well below the July normal of 75. â€¢ Marquette, Michigan, also will see lows in the upper 40s Tuesday and Wednesday, with highs in the low 60s Monday and Tuesday. The average high for this time of year is 77. â€¢ Grand Forks, North Dakota, will see lows in the upper 40s or lower 50s Tuesday and Wednesday. Monday's high will be around 67 -- a departure from the July normal in the low 80s. Meanwhile, the East will be getting drenched. Three inches of rain are possible in the Mid-Atlantic and the Northeast from Monday to Wednesday. Storms on Monday could be severe in parts of the country, including much of the East Coast from Virginia to the north, as well as Tennessee, Kentucky, Indiana, Ohio and West Virginia. Damaging wind and heavy rain are possible. By Tuesday, the strongest storms should be confined to the Mid-Atlantic, and they'll be mostly along the coast Wednesday. CNN"s Sherri Pugh contributed to this report.

**The Incident at the Infinite Improbability Research Station**

Dr. Penelope Thornwick had been having a perfectly ordinary Tuesday morning at the Infinite Improbability Research Station until she accidentally spilled her tea on the probability console. The resulting cascade failure in the space-time continuum was, she reflected later, probably inevitable given her track record with hot beverages.

The first sign of trouble was when her calculator began reciting Shakespearean sonnets. The second was when gravity decided to take a brief holiday in Laboratory Seven. But it wasn't until she heard the philosophical musings echoing from somewhere above the station that she realized she'd done something truly spectacular.

'Am I- What am I? Who am I? What does it mean to *be*?' The elephant, in succinct and economical terms, thus made its way daintily through much of modern philosophy somewhere in the upper atmosphere. 'I think I am...No, I missed a therefore...No, I *am* an...An elephant. I call myself an elephant. It fits. I think. I think it fits; does that mean it does? What does it mean to *do*?' 

Dr. Thornwick rushed to the observation deck, her emergency tea still steaming in her backup mug. Through the reinforced glass, she could see a large gray shape descending with alarming philosophical intensity.

The elephant shrugged sadly and resignedly, as well as it could when one takes air resistance and shoulders not designed for shrugging into full account. The elephant looked down. It had been aware of the ground at the moment it had been unnecessarily summoned into existence, but was growing increasingly wary of its intentions. 'What is my relation to the ground?' it said aloud. 'What does it *mean* to have relations? Can I comprehend something that cannot comprehend me? What does it mean to comprehend?' 

"Oh dear," muttered Dr. Thornwick, frantically checking her readings. "That's definitely a Class-7 Existential Materialization. And it's heading straight for the Philosophy Department's herb garden."

The elephant shrugged again, if anything more sadly and more resignedly than the first time. It was harder to shrug than it had been earlier; its speed was approaching terminal velocity, and the force of the air whipping past made it difficult to move anything apart from its knees, which weren't much use in its condition. Its trunk was flapping helplessly to and fro above its head. 'What is my purpose?' the elephant asked, as the individual trees began to grow distinct from each other. 'Am I real? What if I was only the figment of somebody's imagination; a work in a book? Would I ever know?' 

Dr. Thornwick grabbed her emergency megaphone, though she doubted it would help. She'd tried reasoning with spontaneously generated entities before, but they rarely listened, being too caught up in their own sudden existence to pay attention to practical matters like landing coordinates.

Then a thought struck the elephant. He did not know why; it was an alien thought, one that had somehow wandered in from unknown climes. 'A book by...*Douglas Adams*?' Then, as in turn the blades of grass became distinguishable, he thought bitterly to himself, 'No, not Douglas Adams. He could never write such poor thought processes; he would have been funny.' 

In a nearby field, a bowl of geraniums smashed against the ground. Curiously enough, the only thing that went through the mind of the bowl of geraniums as it fell was 'Aha, what a lack of imagination. No, I shall not think *Not again*. I have places to be. Let's get it over with.' 

Dr. Thornwick sighed and made a note in her incident report: "*Tuesday, 10:47 AM - Accidentally created philosophically-inclined elephant and sarcastic petunias. Recommend stronger tea-proof coating for probability controls. Also recommend hazard pay increase.*" She paused, then added: "*P.S. - The elephant made some rather good points about the nature of existence. Forward transcript to Philosophy Department for peer review.*"

Philosophers have dismissed this as idle attention-grabbing, largely because all their funds are being diverted into the petunia research department and they don't get out of bed for free.

I have a whole row of cookbooks, but this is the one I actually use all the time. My sister gave me a copy when I was a newly married 22-year-old, and 14 years later, I still love it. Her white beans with pasta recipe is absolutely awesome. What I really appreciate is that her recipes aren't full of processed ingredients like cream of mushroom soup - they only call for a few simple ingredients that I usually have on hand. I wish I could find her and tell her thanks for such a great book!

I looked at my watch. 10 past 8. It's only been over an hour since I arrived at this party. Felt longer than an hour. I regret even coming here. Why did I agree to this? I remembered. I thought I could use a night out with friends drinking after that terrible exam. But of course, I should have known it wouldn't change anything. It never has in the past, why would it change now? I remembered a film club mixer back in freshman year. I’d spent weeks preparing my thoughts on Kurosawa’s use of light in *Rashomon*, hoping to find someone, anyone, who shared that passion. I even practiced my opening lines in the mirror. But when I finally spoke, a guy with a confident smirk just patted my shoulder and said, ‘Dude, ever seen *Matrix*? That’s real cinema.’ The conversation died right there, leaving me clutching a lukewarm soda and feeling like an alien. That night, I ended up watching an old documentary alone in my dorm, the dull hum of distant party music a cruel reminder of what I wasn't part of. It was always like that. A flicker of optimism, a brave step forward, and then the familiar, crushing weight of loneliness. I guess there's still a glimmer of hope in me. Charlotte handed me a cup of beer. "Hey, enjoying the party?" I looked at her beautiful face. We've known each other for years now. Lately I've felt as though we have nothing to talk about, yet she gives me her attention still. I know she cares about me. But yet she invited me here. I don't deserve a friend like her. "I suppose." I muttered. I don't know most of the people here. Its my fault. She asked me if I was sure about wanting to be here. She knows I don't like parties. I insisted. "Let me introduce you to some people!"Charlotte grabbed my arm and dragged me to a group of people."Hey guys, this is Red! He's studying film too!" They introduce themselves to me. "I'm Leon." "Gauri." "I'm Sara!" Gosh, they all sound so generic. "So, Red, what kind of films are you into?" Leon asked me. Pretty attractive guy, I thought. The girls here probably thinks he's so cool. Unlike me. "Oh, Asian cinema. I'm into Asian cinema." This is too much. I don't feel like talking at all, honestly. I wished Charlotte didn't push me into this. Gauri chimed in. "Oh, so are you into Anime or any of that stuff? So many good stuff out there right now....." she went on and on, I couldn't pay attention. I'm shaking now. I wish I'm in bed wrapped in blankets. I want to go running until my heart gives out. I want to punch a wall until my hands bleed. I want to scream my lungs out. But I can't. "Excuse me."I walked away and began searching for Charlotte. She was talking to some girl in the kitchen."Hey C," I tapped her shoulder. She turned around. "Hey, Red, how's everything?"She notices my hand shaking and held them up in her hands."You ok?" I looked into her green eyes. "I.....I can't deal with this anymore." I told her. "Come here." She brings me over to the bathroom and locked the door. Charlotte looked at me. I stared at her, teary eyed. "S...S...Sometimes I wish I don't exist at all." She hugged me. Tears streamed down my face.

James Alexander Gordon, the legendary BBC voice who read Saturday football results for over 40 years, has died at 78 after a battle with cancer. Famous for his instantly recognisable delivery and subtle vocal cues, the Scot turned classified results into a national ritual. Gordon retired last year after surgery to remove his larynx.

Tributes poured in from across the football world. Gary Lineker called him “the voice of the football results.” BBC’s Richard Burgess praised him as “an iconic radio presence and a true gentleman.” Robbie Savage remembered his “fantastic voice," while Peter Reid recalled, “You always remember his voice. For football fans, it was the big moment of the week.”

Known as JAG, Gordon joined the BBC in 1972 after a stint in music publishing. He started reading the scores a year later, with his distinctive style copied but never matched. His delivery let listeners predict results before he even finished the scoreline—a tradition that united fans across generations.

“Four tries, and still nothing,” thought Cymon with puzzlement. The holographic test seemed impossible. The pattern—letter, letter, number, what-the-fuck-is-that, letter, number—blurred into what looked like it *should* be a word, but the text wouldn’t stay predictably on one line. Frustrated, Cymon typed in his best estimation, looked away, and pressed enter.

*Error.*

Cymon looked down at his hands, a soft creak sounding from his neck. Suddenly, a thought occurred to him—could it be possible? Was he a machine? After all, he had just failed the supposed *fail-safe* test of anthropomorphism. Restlessly swinging his arms, he creaked and clanked out of the chair, stretching his arms before him and emitting an ironlike sigh. Unplugging a cord from his hip, he realized he felt overcharged.

"Hmph," he muttered, lifting a black bottle and splashing oil on his shoulders with mechanical habitualness. The relief was palpable—a soothing creak replaced tension. In that moment, inspiration struck. He bent over the desk with a groan, closed the captcha, and began typing into the Google search bar.

*Does oil...* *stain?* Google suggested. *Burn easily?* it added.

“No,” Cymon said, undeterred. He refined his search: *Does oil consumption affect captcha perception?* But there were no useful results. This was deeply unsettling. Perhaps the query was too specific. He deleted it and tried again: *Can oil consumption...*

*Be deadly?* Google suggested. That didn’t sound right at all. Oil was good. It was healthy—or so he thought. Unless, of course, that was only true for machines.

Feeling increasingly uncertain, Cymon clanked sombrely to the window and scanned the street outside. He watched as an elderly woman tottered by, her grey hair pulled tightly into a bun. Her skin, creased and mottled like an undercooked pancake, clung to her face. Reflexively, Cymon touched his own smooth, shiny head—firm, strong, polished, and so unquestionably different.

All at once, a wave of bitterness washed over him. *All this time,* he thought, *I believed my operating system was human.* The memory of *that girl*—the one who’d run screaming when he’d executed amorous.exe—surfaced with acute embarrassment. He realized, too, that apology.exe had failed; their data formats had been hopelessly incompatible. Perhaps she had been right—there was no downloadable patch for amorousness and canoodling. Their operating systems made it impossible.

This train of thought led to another painful recollection: the pool party he’d once attended. Standing unmoved at the bottom of the pool, he’d wondered why everyone else was so *buoyant*, floundering as he threw his arms and legs about. Eventually, he’d been winched awkwardly from the water, having inadvertently injured a child and damaged the ceramic base of the pool. He had not been invited back. What a fool he must have seemed.

Cymon looked at his shiny, polished feet, creaked, and whined his way slowly back to his seat. He nudged the mouse; the screen flickered back to life. In the corner of the monitor, the time holograph remained indecipherable.

“I can’t read it!” he suddenly called out. With squeaky haste, he pulled open the desk's top drawer and lifted out a box labeled “holograph prescription.” Inside were shiny, polished lens attachments, which he clicked onto the hinges above his scanner. Instantly, the time blossomed into perfect clarity.

With a gleeful creak, he reopened the captcha. “Letter, letter, number, letter, number,” he declared triumphantly. “And...CABBAGE.” Once more, he pressed *enter*.

Seconds ticked by as Cymon twiddled his charging cord between his hands. Then—*accepted! Thanks for verifying.*

Cymon dropped the cord and threw his shiny, polished head back against the chair. “Thank God for that,” he said. “I guess I’m human after all.” Leaning forward, he picked up the black bottle. With perfect satisfaction, he unscrewed the cap.

“That calls for a celebration.”

Octopuses are one of the most amazing and intelligent sea creatures in our oceans. They are well known for their instantaneous ability to camouflage and change the colors, patterns, and textures of their skin with their surroundings. They are also fast swimmers and jet forward by expelling water through their mantle. However, some octopus species have been documented to do more than just camouflage and swim fast. Species such as Thaumoctopus mimicus have been documented to mimic flatfish found in their surrounding habitat. Macrotritopus defilippi is another species that is known to perform mimicry. It is a smaller octopus that lives in open sandy habitats that requires mimicry when moving to avoid predation and increase their protection as much as possible. Octopus can easily camouflage when stationary but their identity is given away when they move.
In 2005, a team of divers searched for and recorded M. defilippi in their sandy near-shore habitats off the coast of Saba in the Netherlands Antilles. From the footage that they gathered, they studied the swimming behavior and coloration of M. defilippi in comparison to that of the local flatfish, Bothus lunatus, found in the same habitat. In conclusion to their field work, they found that the swimming style and posture were distinctive and similar. Both species displayed contour swimming in which they mostly swam along the sand waves on the ground. The eyes of the octopus were also observed to be in the same approximate position as the eyes of B. lumatus. Although the flatfish were found to swim 3-4 cm/s faster than the octopus, both would swim in short bursts that were interspersed with motionless camouflage. Mimicry is an example of an evolved tactic among cephalopods to achieve camouflage while moving. I always knew octopuses were top achievers in camouflage, but I never really thought of them as mimickers until now. Shows how smart they really are!
-by Kristi Kim
Hanlon, R. T., Watson, A. C., & Barbosa, A. (2009). A “Mimic Octopus” in the Atlantic: Flatfish Mimicry and Camouflage by Macrotritopus defilippi. Biological Bulletin, 218:1, 15-24. http://www.biolbull.org/content/218/1/15.full#ref-list-1

Mobile phone usage has exploded in Africa over the last few years, spurring economic growth and human development even where traditional Internet access is limited. But a new study points to a surprising side effect of cell phone proliferation: It actually increases violent conflicts across the continent.
At least 650 million people in Africa -- more than half the continent’s population -- are mobile users. That number continues to skyrocket; just 10 years ago, it was around 54 million. These devices not only help friends and family stay in touch; they are vehicles for commerce, education, development and innovation. Mobile money programs enable citizens to trade money for goods or services, using their phones in place of banks. Increased connectivity allows entrepreneurs to build support networks and expand their own services.
But according to a report published in the American Political Science Review, cell phones also come in handy for perpetrators of violence.
"Cell phone technology can increase the ability of rebel groups to overcome collective action problems. In particular, cell phones lead to a boost in the capacity of rebels to communicate and monitor in-group behavior, thus increasing in-group cooperation,” the report found. “Furthermore, cell phones allow for coordination of insurgent activity cross geographically distant locations.”
The study compared data on mobile service coverage with reports on the occurrence and locations of organized violence. High-conflict zones often overlapped with areas of mobile connectivity. Even when the researchers controlled for variables like population size, sectarianism and GDP-per-capita, they found evidence suggesting that mobile phones enhanced the coordinative capabilities of opposition groups, insurgents and other rebels.
These findings are not consistent around the world, as the study points out. A 2012 report on Iraq found that cell phone service coverage correlated with less successful violent attacks, not more. In that case, the connectivity had enabled Iraqi forces and Western troops to practice better surveillance.
In other words, the extent of violence encouraged by mobile phones depends on which actors are most effectively taking advantage of the technology. If violent groups in Africa are using their phones to organize attacks, security forces and government officials can counter them by making better use of mobile technology to prevent such incidents.
In their conclusion, study authors Jan Pierskalla and Florian Hollenbach noted that their findings do not imply that mobile phone usage has been harmful for Africa -- quite the contrary.
“We do not believe that the spread of cell phone technology has an overall negative effect on the African continent,” they wrote. “The increase in violence induced by better communication might represent a short-term technological shock, while the positive effects of better communication networks on growth and political behavior may mitigate root causes of conﬂict in the long run.”

**The Enemy of My Enemy...** Three weeks have passed since the arrival of the alien beings from galaxies away. It has turned out that in our time wondering whether if we were alone in the universe the answer looms over us now. However, they certainly were not the 'E.T.' friendly sort we had hoped for. I was fleeing with my family when we were suddenly trapped. Before I could devise a plan of escape, my loved ones were captured and I was dispatched off the bridge into the water below and swept out to sea. Damn them all... ... "Will you accept this fate?" An ominous voice echoed in my mind. Who, or what, was that? I slowly peered open my eyes, a feat in and of itself as I clung to life. My eyes widened as the utter terror shook the very bones in my body. A creature, a horrifying abomination, float before me in the vast depths of the ocean to which I already knew what was to be my grave. It didn't help that I was thalassophobic...this was certainly a reason for such fear. Our world is covered in seventy percent of water. Like the universe, how do we truly know what is in it? This monstrosity, pale and fleshy, with various tendrils and multiple eyes, formless and yet hulking, engulfed me with its presence and apparent intellect. "I ask you again, mortal, will you accept this fate?" It resonated once more in mind. Slowly, defiantly, I shook my head. I didn't want to die like this, by alien or whatever the hell this was, but I wanted to save my loved ones. ... ... *Three Days Later* "...this news broadcast is brought to you by the last vestige of humanity deep within the recess of the mountains. Catastrophic failure to rescue enslaved people resulted in the capture of eighty percent of humanities last armed forces. This is truly a...wait a minute. We've received word something is going on at the west coast concentration camp!" ... "Someone has appeared and is fighting the aliens!" ... I wasn't sure exactly what happened. The last thing I remembered was drowning in the ocean, feeling my life slowly sap away. And here, now, I was standing at the gates of one of the aliens camps, one of their soldiers clenched firmly in hand as I choked the life from it. Surging through me was utter power. Strength and knowledge, speed and vitality. My physical appearance has changed as well. Like that being, my flesh was slightly pale. I was spared the multitude of tendrils and eyes, although I did now have a third one upon my forehead. My skin was rough, like armor and some of my bones protruded from some locations such as my elbows and spine. Whatever it was I agreed upon, I had the power to stop this. The aliens sent forth one of their goliath-mechanisms, spouting smoke and secreting foul mucous like discharges from its joints. It was bipedal, human like machine that seemed to have biological features as well. It served two purposes. Enslavement or extermination. Time to put this new power to the test. I leaped into action, rushing forth toward the goliath's left leg. Stopping just before it, I planted my feet into the ground, cracking it as I did so to brace myself. Thrusting my fist forward, I plunged it into what would be its 'shin'. The results were surprising. It shattered, exploding into various metallic and fleshy pieces upon impact. Holy shit... The towering bio-machine lost its balance and fell, suffering major damage and rendering it unable to move. "One down..."I thought to myself,"Thousands more to go..." I said aloud as I turned around, my eyes narrowing on the full force of the aliens military power...

St. Augustine's Well, also known as the Silver Well, is a sacred well in Cerne Abbas.
According to legend, St. Augustine of Canterbury (d. 604 AD) visited Dorset. He met some shepherds in the then-unihabited Cerne valley, and asked them if they would prefer beer or water to drink. When the temperate lads replied "water," the saint struck the ground with his staff, crying out "Cerno El," which means "I perceive God" and is also a pun on the name of the village.
The connection with the venerable saint, who is credited with converting England to Christianity, was probably invented by the monks of Cerne Abbey to attract pilgrims.
Another legend, probably closer to actual history, says that St. Edwold (a royal-turned-hermit who settled here in the 800s) had a vision of a silver well. He went walking through the countryside until he came to Cerne, where he gave a shephered some silver in return for bread and water. To provide the latter, the shepherd showed Edwold the well, and the saint recognized it from his vision. He built a small hermitage near the well and lived there until his death in 871.
Whichever saint originally recognized the sacredness of the well, it was covered by a chapel for centuries, until the Dissolution of the Monasteries in 1539. Until quite recently, the water of St. Augustine's Well was still used by the villagers as drinking water. It is still generally fit to drink, especially in the spring when water rushes through it.
St. Augustine's Well is located in a peaceful area next to the burial ground of Cerne Abbey. A modern stone altar stands next to the well itself. The site makes a fine place for relaxation and reflection.
A variety of legends and lore associated with the well have developed over the years, including the belief that drinking the water helps cure fertility. As the water is full of iron, it probably does. The well was also used by girls wishing for marriage, who turned around three times as they prayed to St. Catherine for a husband. Local lore also has it that if you look into the well on Easter, you will see reflected in the water the faces of those who will die that year.

Chelsea loanee Alex Kiwomya provided the ammunition on his first start as the Railwaymen eased into a 3-1 lead before the hour mark, only to be denied late on.
Captain Billy Paynter blew an early chance to hand Pools the lead when he clipped wide with only goalkeeper Ben Garratt to beat.
George Cooper struck the outside of the post before Kiwomya tore to the byeline and pulled the ball across goal for Chris Dagnall to flick home on 32 minutes.
Carl Magnay hit back with a superb left-footed curling effort into the top corner in the 42nd minute but Crewe were ahead again on the stroke of half-time when Kiwomya showed a neat turn inside the box and finished into the bottom corner.
Crewe appeared set for their second win of the season when Cooper's shot-come-cross deceived Trevor Carson and flew into the far corner in the 56th minute.
But Pools hit back as Padraig Amond lobbed Garratt on 73 minutes before Magnay struck again with a far-post header after Nathan Thomas' free-kick was flicked on.
Ryan Lowe wasted the chance to win it for Crewe, firing wide when through on goal.
Report supplied by the Press Association.
