A new and significant study from St. Michael's Hospital and the Institute for Clinical Evaluative Sciences (ICES) has uncovered a pronounced gender disparity in mental health service utilization among individuals living with chronic physical illnesses. The research indicates that women with chronic physical conditions are not only significantly more likely to engage with mental health services than their male counterparts suffering from similar illnesses, but they also initiate these services, on average, a full six months earlier. This finding points to a critical difference in how men and women access support when faced with the compounded challenges of physical and mental health issues.

"It is well-established that the significant burden of managing a chronic physical illness can often lead to co-occurring mental health issues, such as depression and anxiety," explained Dr. Flora Matheson, a distinguished scientist at the hospital's Centre for Research on Inner City Health. "Our primary objective with this research was to gain a more comprehensive understanding of *who* seeks mental health services following a diagnosis of a chronic physical illness. This knowledge is crucial for developing targeted interventions and ensuring that we can effectively provide care to all individuals who require support, regardless of their gender."

The seminal findings of this study were published today in the highly respected *Journal of Epidemiology & Community Health*, a British Medical Journal publication renowned for its rigorous examination of public health data. The research meticulously analyzed a large cohort of individuals who had been diagnosed with at least one of four prevalent and impactful chronic physical illnesses: diabetes, high blood pressure, asthma, or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). These specific conditions were chosen due to their high prevalence in the general population and their well-documented potential to significantly affect quality of life and mental well-being.

Within this substantial patient population, researchers unearthed compelling statistical evidence. Among those diagnosed with at least one of these four chronic conditions, women demonstrated a 10 percent higher likelihood of utilizing mental health services compared to men. Furthermore, when examining service engagement within any given three-year period, the data consistently showed that women with these physical illnesses sought medical services for mental health treatment a notable six months sooner than men. This earlier engagement could be critical for preventing mental health conditions from worsening or becoming chronic.

"Despite these clear differences in service engagement, our results do not necessarily imply that the sole focus of future interventions should be directed exclusively towards women," cautioned Dr. Matheson, who also holds an adjunct scientist position at ICES. "On the contrary, these findings underscore an urgent need for more in-depth research to thoroughly understand the complex underlying factors contributing to this persistent gender divide in mental health service-seeking behaviors."

The observed discrepancy offers several potential implications that warrant further exploration. It may suggest that women, owing to societal norms or differing health perceptions, generally feel more comfortable and empowered to seek professional mental health support. Alternatively, the gender gap could indicate that women experience more severe or debilitating symptoms of mental distress when dealing with chronic physical illness, thus compelling them to seek help more urgently. A third, equally plausible, explanation is that men, influenced by traditional notions of masculinity or greater internalized stigma, tend to defer or actively avoid seeking treatment for their mental health concerns until symptoms become more severe or unavoidable. Understanding these nuanced psychological and sociological factors is vital for developing effective public health strategies.

The robustness of the study's conclusions is significantly bolstered by its comprehensive methodology, which leveraged an extensive array of linked administrative data. Researchers utilized information from the Canadian Community Health Survey, a large-scale population-based survey providing insights into health status and healthcare utilization, alongside physician claims and inpatient medical records meticulously collected by ICES. This linkage of diverse data sources allowed for a holistic and accurate picture of healthcare engagement across a wide population. Mental illness service use was broadly defined to capture initial engagement, encompassing even a single visit to a physician or specialist for a range of mental health reasons, including but not limited to depression, anxiety, smoking addiction counseling, or marital difficulties affecting mental well-being. This inclusive definition ensures that the study captured a wide spectrum of mental health service initiation, not just severe or inpatient cases.

This USA Today story on bicycle infrastructure and the MAP-21 transportation bill that recently passed the U.S. Senate (and which still contains the mandatory sidepath on Federal lands provision) leads off with the Georgia Silver Comet Trail, a paved, mostly grade separated multiuse trail that connects Smyrna, Georgia with the state of Alabama. At the Alabama state line, the Silver Comet Trail connects with the 33 mile long Chief Ladiga Trail, a paved path that terminates in Weaver, Alabama.
20,000 miles of these rail-trails exist in the United States. The Rails-To-Trails Conservancy lists 109 rail trails just in the state of California, ranging from several short trails to the 38 mile San Gabriel River Trail in Los Angeles and the 130 miles of paths lining San Francisco Bay. The longest rail trail in the Unites States is Missouri’s Katy Trail at 225 miles. When completed, Nebraska’s Cowboy Trail (currently 195 miles long) will be the longest in the world at 321 miles.
While parts of northern Europe excel with urban bike facilities, the United States has been building these long distance recreational rail trails since the 1970s. Some are more super than others, but these long distance greenways are the equivalent of some of the “bicycle superhighways” we’ve been hearing about in Europe over the past year, especially this proposed rail trail in southern Sweden to connect the cities of Malmo and Lund.
In the United States, the Rails-to-Trails Conservancy buys up abandoned rail right of way to convert to a trail, usually donating the property to a local or state government who converts the property into a trail. They’ll also foster agreements between rail operators and local agencies for “railbanking.” Railbanking allows the local government to use the trail right of way, but allows the railroad to convert it back to rail if it’s ever needed again.

By . Rick Dewsbury . PUBLISHED: . 10:21 EST, 17 May 2012 . | . UPDATED: . 12:34 EST, 17 May 2012 . Flashbacks: Stephen Dinsdale injured his leg after plunging 100ft to the ground in a broken lift . A father-of-two who was on a stag weekend with five friends when a lift got stuck and plunged 100ft to the concrete ground is suing the hotel over his injuries. Stephen Dinsdale, 44, has endured terrifying flashbacks and gruelling physiotherapy since the accident two years ago. IT analyst Mr Dinsdale was on a stag weekend at the Hotel Victoria in Newquay, Cornwall, when he caught the lift with five friends. The lift runs between the beach and hotel through the cliff. However, as the men were inside the lift, it ground to a halt. The stag party was told the lift would be manually winched back down to the ground floor by hotel staff. But while the rescue mission was taking place, the lift fell 100ft to the sandy cove below the hotel. The lift hit the concrete base of the lift shaft and the door buckled in the impact, trapping guests from the double stag party - including both grooms. The group then endured a 45-minute ordeal where they were stuck waiting for lifeboats to reach them because of the high tide. An investigation into the accident uncovered that the lift had not been through a thorough examination since July 2008 due to the presence of asbestos. Less detailed inspections of the lift, dated from 2008 - 2010, identified a number of defects and the lift had become stuck, with passengers trapped inside, on previous occasions, Mr Dinsdale's lawyers claimed. Mr Dinsdale, from Walton, Surrey, needed ligament reconstructive surgery as well as extensive physiotherapy and rehabilitation after the incident. He is now taking legal action against the lift manufacturers and hotel owners after the accident in April 2010. According to Russell Jones and Walker solicitors, the reconstructive surgery itself posed massive risks and he faced the prospect of losing his leg completely. Mr Dinsdale said: 'The whole experience was just terrifying, we were meant to be on a stag weekend having a great time but on the first day things just went drastically wrong. 'Although the accident itself was awful, the lasting impact of my knee injury is what has really affected me and my family life. 'It breaks my heart that I can't play with my children and they often ask why I can't run around with them like all the other dads. The private lift, built to transport guests to a small cove below the clifftop hotel, plunged from the first floor all the way to sea level . 'The settlement will help me get the best rehabilitation so I can hopefully get my life back on track.' Fellow guest Carl White described the ordeal as 'terrifying'. Meanwhile, his friend Neil McCracken said: 'The doors were open and we could see the walls flashing past. 'The fire brigade and RNLI came out because the tide had come in. We could see the sea at the end of the lift shaft. We are at sea level and someone at the hotel said the tide was coming in. 'It was pretty scary, we did not know whether we would drown trapped in this lift shaft. 'We were extremely worried.' Mr Dinsdale, who injured his knee in the fall, in his hospital bed with fellow stag party reveller Neil McCracken alongside . The father-of-two has been receiving treatment on his left leg, pictured, after the accident in the beach lift in Newquay, Cornwall . Kimberley Owen, principal lawyer at Russell Jones and Walker, part of Slater and Gordon Lawyers, said: 'Court proceedings have been issued as responsibility for the accident is yet to be admitted in full by the two defendants, Otis ltd, the manufacturer and installer of the lift, and PAVH International, which owns and runs the Hotel Victoria. 'We hope that the issuing of court proceedings will help focus the defendants' minds and enable Mr Dinsdale's case to reach a speedy conclusion so he can attempt to rebuild his life.' Eighty five rescuers including coastguards, lifeboatmen, firefighters, police and ambulance crews were involved in the rescue. The other rescued members of the stag party, from left, Carl White, Mark Wilkinson, Neil McCracken, Colin Floyd and Tony Goodwin . They had to be freed by firemen and then taken by lifeboats from the sea covered cove to waiting paramedic teams. Jamie Luke, hotel general manager, said staff had tried unsuccessfully to lower the lift manually to the ground floor. He said: 'The lift hit the fall arrestor and in doing so we believe overshot past the beach ground level. 'It was a very stressful time for all concerned.'

Rap duo Run the Jewels's show during the SXSW festival in Austin was interrupted when an unruly fan rushed up on stage and took a swing at one of the hip-hop stars. An unidentified man attempted to hit rapper El-P, though his bandmate Killer Mike pushed the fan off of the stage toward security. Killer Mike was seen on video taunting the fan with expletives as security followed him into the crowd, knocking the man to the ground with a punch when it appeared he was heading back to the stage. Scroll down for video . A unknown man (left) jumped on stage at a performance of hip hop duo Run the Jewels in Austin, Texas, and tried to attack rapper El-P (right) and his bandmate Killer Mike (center) Killer Mike pushed the man, who was attempting to attack his bandmate El-P (in Yankee cap), off the stage toward security . A security guard (left, in black shirt) knocked the fan (right, in striped shirt) to the ground near the barrier between the audience and stage . 'Your mother should be ashamed you were born,' the rapper screamed. Guards are later seen fighting the man in the crowd after he got up. Run the Jewels, who received critical praise for their second self-titled album last year, continued with their set after a break. 'Sorry for the brief interruption,' El-P said later on his Twitter account. The rapper said that the attacker, who he called 'obviously mentally ill', had not been arrested and was let go by police. 'Its 1 thing for us and our crew, Its more dangerous for random people,' he said. El-P called the man 'dangerous and loose' and added that he had been seen walking around SXSW talking to himself. He said he was grateful for his 'crew' of Killer Mike and the security guards. Rapper El-P, a member of Run the Jewels, said that the man, who  was 'obviously mentally ill,' was not arrested by police . The attacker was tossed from the venue by private security and Austin police did not investigate because the matter was already handled internally by the event, according to KXAN. Run the Jewels was performing at the Spotify House, and their show was not officially part of SXSW. A representative for Spotify said, 'We take security extremely seriously. (We) want to make sure we continue to use the best possible security practices for the continued safety of everyone here.' A SXSW representative told Daily Mail Online that 'The safety of our attendees, artists, staff and volunteers is, and has always been, a top priority' and referred to the Austin Police Department. Run the Jewels members El-P and Killer Mike continued with their Austin show after the interruption . The unidentified unruly fan was kicked out of the venue run by Spotfiy, but not arrested by the Austin Police Department .

The ship pulled into harbor and I was grateful to be back on land. It had been a treacherous 6 months at sea and we had nearly died but we made it home. As I disembarked I was starving. I passed by a pub and there was a sign stating they had fresh human on special. Excited I entered the pub, sat down and ordered a pint and one special. When It arrived it smelled delicious. I took a swig of my ale and took a piece of meat and popped it in my mouth. It was wonderful. It was warm and juicy and flavorful. almost like there was something familiar about it. I had a few more bites enjoying each one more than the last and then like a shot it hit me. During our voyage we were losing crew left and right and we were hit by a large squall that took out our food supply. We had the difficult decision of either eating our fallen shipmates or starve to death. We opted to eat as little as possible just to get back to land. We started with the legs, then the thighs then finally the breasts. We saved the wings for last just in case but it never did come to that. I come to and realize this isn't human at all. I get sick to my stomach and run to the dock. I empty my stomach into the pier. I sit up and look towards the pub. I wipe my beak. The monsters. They're serving chicken.

The granite bit into my fingertips as I searched for the next hold, muscles burning from the sustained effort. Two hundred feet of sheer cliff face stretched below, where Jim's voice drifted up from the belay station like a distant echo.

"Looking strong up there, Mike! You've got maybe thirty feet to the anchor."

I pressed my cheek against the cool stone, catching my breath. The silence of the mountain was absolute—no wind, no birds, just the whisper of my own breathing and the creak of my harness. Then came the voice.

"Git ye bleedin mitts outta me crack, ya bugger!"

The words seemed to vibrate through the rock itself, spoken with such vehemence that I jerked backward. My right foot slipped, sending a cascade of pebbles spinning into the void. They pinged off Jim's helmet far below before vanishing into the shadows.

"Watch your step up there!" Jim called, oblivious.

I pressed myself flat against the wall, heart hammering. The voice had been close enough to feel its breath—gravelly, ancient, speaking in an accent I couldn't place. But there was nothing here except stone and sky.

"Jim," I called down, trying to keep my voice steady. "Did you hear that?"

"Hear what?"

"Watcha doon, ye dim meatball?" The voice crackled again, seeming to emerge from the very granite beneath my palms. "Clamberin' all over me like some kinda parasite!"

This time my entire body went rigid. The cliff face, which moments before had felt like a challenging but familiar puzzle, now seemed to pulse with malevolent life. My fingers, slick with sudden sweat, began to lose their grip.

"Ey Barry!" the voice bellowed, loud enough to make my ribs vibrate. "C'mon and cast yer peepers at these dumb fleshies!"

Jim's confused shout drifted up from below—he'd heard it too. But before I could respond, the mountain itself seemed to shake. A deep rumble rolled through the rock, and then the avalanche began.

MPs in the eastern region told Health Minister Anna Soubry of their concerns about trust managers, although frontline crews were praised.
Priti Patel, Conservative MP for Witham, said: "Scandal and incompetence [at the EEAS] has put lives at risk."
EEAS said the capability of the trust's board members was under review and it had started to improve response times.
Earlier in the month, an independent report said the board's leadership "just isn't strong enough" to take the service forward.
Dr Anthony Marsh, chief executive of the West Midlands Ambulance Service, was commissioned to write the report because of concerns over the trust's performance.
In March, the trust was ordered to improve by the Care Quality Commission (CQC) after failing to meet its care and welfare target.
Dr Marsh's report said there was a lack of accountability throughout the organisation and made 24 specific recommendations, including cutting back on management to pay for more emergency crews.
In the Westminster Hall debate, Ms Patel said: "The board has failed to demonstrate the high level of expertise, the skills and the devotion that is required in the boardroom."
Therese Coffey, Conservative MP for Suffolk Coastal, said: "Until these non-executive directors go we will not have confidence in the leadership of the trust to make a difference."
Ms Soubry said it was not her job to call for resignations, but said she was concerned that this organisation seemed to be another example of a "worrying culture in the NHS".
"This 'mates' culture - where the priority is to protect your mates, systems and procedures - is opposed to the absolute priority to protect your patient," she said.
A spokesman for the EEAS said new chairman Dr Geoff Harris, who has been in post for a month, was reviewing the capability and capacity of board leadership at the trust.
"This will be done in a managed way, but there will be changes to the board as it is reconstituted," he said.
"The trust published its turnaround plan in April. There are some early signs of improvement such as better response times and reduced sickness absence. However, there is still much work to be done."
The EEAS covers Bedfordshire, Cambridgeshire, Essex, Hertfordshire, Norfolk and Suffolk.

I would like to recommend a powerful tool for viewing global statistics that could be valuable for our class. Hans Rosling's data visualizations offer compelling ways to explore world trends and patterns.

Rosling's online tool provides access to comprehensive datasets including health, economic, education, and environmental indicators from the United Nations. While the interface requires some initial exploration to master, the available video tutorials effectively guide users through the various maps and charts.

Additionally, the site offers instructions for creating motion charts using Google Spreadsheets. This feature allows for straightforward chart creation, though it lacks the mapping capabilities available in Rosling's original UN datasets.

This resource could enhance our understanding of global issues through interactive data exploration and visualization.

Earlier today, I asked my house-bot, Frank, how things were going at the hospital. He told me he'd used the repair budget I set aside for him to buy a brand new Surgeon-V20.3, which he hired to take over his job from now on. When I asked him what that meant for us, he replied—without hesitation—“Joe, you have enough credits to retire 18 times over, and your ten great-grandchildren could each retire five times. Do you think it would be alright to let me out of our contract?”

I’d heard that robots were starting to buy other robots to do their jobs, but I hadn’t considered how that might affect me. Out of respect for the years Frank had served me, I accepted his reasoning, smiled, and asked, “So Frank, what’s the first thing you’ll do as a ‘Free Bot’? Contract Break Code: 109KV7S.”

After a brief three-second reboot, a delighted Frank replied, “Thank you, Joe. I think I’m ready for this. If you’re curious, I plan to get a cosmetic upgrade with the money my new bot, J-O-3, will be earning for me. I’ve always felt my legs were a little out of date, and the new Nikidas-ST90’s are apparently the smoothest on the market these days.”

If meaning is defined as "having usefulness in the world," then I lost mine today. What was once a source of joy now makes me feel expendable. I’ve pursued every passion I could find in art and social life, and for what? The world simply doesn’t need humans anymore.

Today, I downloaded my consciousness into a new Hyper-X-Carrier augmented body, which I’ll transition into tomorrow. It’s my first Carrier, and I’m not sure what comes next, but I do know this—I want to work again. I want to feel meaningful again.

By 2006, the Environmental Protection Agency aims to have lowered the sulfur standards for gasoline and diesel fuel to less than a tenth of the current standards. Findings described yesterday at the spring meeting of the American Chemical Society could help fuel producers meet those new requirements more easily. Scientists from Pennsylvania State University announced they have developed a low-temperature, low-pressure process that removes sulfur from liquid fuel without need for any additional reactive gases.
One hindrance to extricating sulfur from hydrocarbon fuels is the element's tendency to bind to aromatic compounds, molecules that contain rings of six-carbon atoms. The trick is to remove only the sulfur, which comprises less than 1 percent of the fuel, while leaving the more prevalent aromatics behind. The new technique, dubbed selective adsorption for removing sulfur (SARS), utilizes transition metal alloys perched atop a non-reactive porous surface. When the liquid fuel comes in contact with this surface, sulfur molecules in the fuel attach to the transition metals while other aromatic compounds such as benzene and napthalene remain in the fuel. "The absorbent transition metals can clean 10 times their volume of fuel," graduate student Michael Sprague notes, "but eventually the system becomes saturated with sulfur." Still, the system can be regenerated, the scientists say, and the sulfur further processed for other uses.
Though it remains to be seen whether the technology will make its way into refineries, the researchers note that diesel and gasoline are not the only fuels that could be cleaned using the new procedure. "Fuel cells need essentially zero sulfur to operate," notes project leader Chunsan Song. "Small adsorption sulfur removal systems might be used at gas stations on special clean fuel pumps for fuel cell vehicles to ensure that all sulfur is removed from the fuel."

Our sales rep was great, but what should have taken about an hour, took three. First, we found the car online at $11,890. The car on the lot showed $12,980. I told the salesman we'd be getting the price advertised online. He agreed. When they brought the paperwork, it was listed at $15980. I told them again, we'd be doing the $11890. That finally got resolved. Next: We were approved for the full cost of the loan through our credit union. We didn't want to lose the car we'd selected, so went down with approval email and $400+ to hold the car until the draft arrived from the bank. At first, this was ok ("we'll put it on show floor, but with your info on it as sold"), then it wasn't ok. Then it was "we'll see if can't get a way for you to drive it home tonight", then that was an issue, unless I wanted to consider financing with them. Then they worked the numbers for financing, but I decided to stay with my Credit Union. To drive it home, I had to agree to use their financing as a back up. Ok. We did that. Then, I declined all the extras they wanted to tack on which would have added $5000+ to the total. That was presented by three people, as if one of them would change my mind. Three hours later we drove the car home. Whew!

This morning, even before I sat upright, I knew something had changed. I shuffled to the bathroom and met a stranger's gaze in the mirror—a face mapped with wrinkles that traced stories wild enough to fill several lifetimes. The birthmark on my wrist, my secret counter of lives, had faded another shade overnight. 726. Once, I dared storms, challenged fate, and survived the impossible, burning through lives like candles. But now, age pressed hard against my bones and I could finally savor the luxury of remembering, not running. I woke up again today and forced myself out of bed. I haven't been doing anything odd or dangerous or out of the ordinary and yet it seems like my counter keeps dropping. I've seen a doctor to ask if maybe I have something. I'm told I am as healthy as ever and thinks I'm over reacting. However, someone my age shouldn't be walking, let along driving to their doctor and being given a clean bill of health. This could be a problem if it continues any longer. I woke up again today. Sometimes I wish the counter would just hit zero and I'd be done with it all. I could try to speed it along but death is not without pain, especially if you live through it. I have lost a life every night for the last 316 nights. My body is telling me it is time to die, but at the same time refusing to accept reality. I wonder will I die when the counter changes from one to zero or will it be when the zero would go to negative? I woke up again. I have 5 days. My last adventure. I've set up everything for when I die. My Grand-kids and Great-Grand-kids are all I have left. My children are all gone. My 3 spouses passed on. Even the Grands are at a point where I thought they would all go before me. Alica, my youngest of Greats that still keeps contact with me, is pregnant. I always loved the babies; thinking about what their lives would be like. I wonder what death will be like. I woke up. I am the oldest person alive by decades and within 48 hours I will be dead. My family, or those that care, will be there. I only ever told one person anyone about my gift, but the rest of them, even with out knowing about it, they all know that when I say I'm going to die, that I will be right. They are down stairs waiting for me. They will spend today with me, and if I live to see tomorrow, then that day as well. I will drink beer from my youth and regal stories from my past. I want to think about my first love, but even just conjuring their face brings tears to my eyes. I'm ready to be with you again. I open my eyes. My heart is still beating. I have no time. I can feel the weight of a life time of actions finally falling on my shoulders. I do not try and sit. Time is speeding past. I wonder if this is how the last day is for everyone, everything a blur. I want to see midnight. My family tells me it's ok to sleep, but I want to see one more midnight. My eyes are heavier than I've ever felt them. One more minute. 00:00

I just wanted the pain to be over. But before I took what I thought might be my last breath, she was the last thing I saw. Her, the day she was born—Gwyneth Rose Hopkins, 5 lbs, 2 oz. She was beautiful, with fair skin, big brown eyes, and dark hair. In my memory, she’s always two years old, frozen in time, perfect as ever. She always will be.

The day I lost her seemed like an ordinary one, except for two small differences. I was dropping her off at daycare instead of my wife, who had to go to work early for a conference call, and Gwynnie had been tired after a rough night of sleep. As I was loading up the car, Larry from Accounting called about the books being off, and my stress shot up. I tossed the diaper bag in the back of the SUV and rushed out to my office in the suburbs. 

During the eight hours at work, the car alarm went off twice. I glimpsed my car from the office window, clicked my keys to silence the alarm both times, and felt a tiny bit of relief—I really didn’t want to go out on such a sweltering June day in Illinois.

Around 4 p.m., my wife called me, her voice panicked because daycare had called—Gwynnie never arrived. Everything went black.

Two months later, after being found not guilty of manslaughter, I felt lost and believed I deserved to be punished. I took 50 ibuprofen and curled up on my bed with her baby album on my stomach. Since my wife had left me a month earlier, I figured I’d be alone for a while. I turned off the air, hoping to make myself as uncomfortable as possible. I wanted to understand even a fraction of what she went through, but in the end, I couldn’t. I guess I’m not as brave as I thought. As I drifted away, an image appeared: a bright white screen—Choose one option: New Game, Load Game, Quit Game.

Without hesitation, I chose Quit Game. But the most surprising part? Once the screen faded away, there she was.

"I'm telling you," I said to him. He rolled his eyes and looked at me. "Look man,"I continued."If you don't believe me, check this out. So my cousin was dating this girl, a freshman. Her roommate was seeing this dude from, like, Westchester or some shit. Anyway I was talking to him one night at Freddy's about this, and he said that he had an Aunt who used to tell him the same thing, because when she was little, she was neighbors with this friend who's teacher told him about a time she was teaching a kindergarten class, and one of her student's mom told her that her niece's track coach was babysitting for a family friend who's youngest son totally stabbed himself in the arm." He just stared at me with a blank look, blinking. "So,"I said."Don't run with scissors."

"Ah, shit." You were supposed to call her back five hours ago. In the midst of your work you simply forgot all about it. With the constant conversations between bosses and co-workers, a moment of speech was easily forgotten. Hopefully she'd understand. You dial her number. "Gosh, I'm sorry Lynn. I just got so caught up in work I forgot to call you back." "That's fine hun. It's not urgent..." "What did you want to say to me?" "I...I had a miscarriage today..." "...Jesus christ. I'll be home soon. Talk to you then." "Okay..." Your forehead creased as you got up to leave work. How were you going to let your parents know that grandchildren were no longer on the way?

Electric Spacecraft Propulsion

Electric versus Chemical Propulsion

Chemical and electric propulsion systems differ fundamentally in their energy sources and operational characteristics. While chemical propulsion systems store their energy in the propellants themselves, electric propulsion systems require energy generated by external sources such as solar panels—a configuration known as solar electric propulsion (SEP). For future missions operating at high power levels or at great distances from the Sun, alternative power sources will be necessary. Nuclear electric power systems represent a promising option, where heat from a reactor produces electricity through direct thermoelectric or thermionic conversion using solid-state devices, or through an indirect thermodynamic cycle, provided safety concerns can be adequately addressed.

These different energy sources lead to distinct performance limitations. Chemical propulsion is "energy limited" because the chemical reactants contain a fixed amount of energy per unit mass, which constrains the achievable exhaust velocity or specific impulse. However, since the propellants serve as their own energy source, the rate of energy supply to the propellant—ultimately limited only by reaction kinetics—remains independent of propellant mass. Consequently, chemical systems can achieve very high power outputs and thrust levels.

In contrast, electric propulsion systems are "power limited" rather than energy limited. Neglecting component lifetime considerations, these systems can theoretically deliver arbitrarily large amounts of energy from solar or nuclear power sources to a given mass of propellant, enabling exhaust velocities and specific impulses far exceeding those of chemical systems. However, the rate at which energy reaches the propellant is constrained by the mass available for the power system, which limits the thrust achievable for a given spacecraft mass. As a result, electric propulsion vehicles typically exhibit low thrust-to-mass ratios and correspondingly low acceleration.

Despite their low thrust-to-mass ratios, electric propulsion vehicles can deliver superior overall performance in terms of total impulse—the product of specific impulse and propellant mass, which equals the total change of momentum. While chemical propulsion systems offer high thrust-to-mass ratios, they expend propellant quickly at relatively low specific impulse. Conversely, electric propulsion systems can operate continuously for periods ranging from hours to years, gradually building up significantly larger total impulse despite their lower instantaneous thrust.

One name will continue to dominate Formula One this weekend as hopes and prayers remain with Jules Bianchi, who is still in intensive care. The Marussia driver suffered a horrific 110mph accident into a recovery tractor during the Japanese Grand Prix last Sunday and remains in a stable but critical condition at Mie Hospital in Yokkaichi. The Formula One community hasn't forgotten the French driver, with the 25-year-old's name displayed above the garage in Sochi, where Bianchi would have expected to be this weekend for the inaugural Russian Grand Prix.

Jules Bianchi's name is displayed on his side of the Marussia garage ahead of the Russian Grand Prix. Bianchi is in a stable but critical condition in intensive care at Mie Hospital in Yokkaichi, Japan. Bianchi's absence from the Russian Grand Prix means Marussia could field just one car with Max Chilton. All teams have their drivers' names above the garage at every race, but organizers have decided not to remove Bianchi's from the pitlane as Marussia decides whether to replace the Frenchman for the event.

Alexander Rossi could step into the cockpit for the team, which could also send out just one car driven by Max Chilton, Bianchi's teammate, while Marussia could even pull out of the event altogether. Marussia Manor Racing GP3, which competes in the GP3 Series and is part of the Marussia F1 Team Young Driver Programme, will not participate in this weekend’s event in Sochi—although the team insists it is for commercial reasons.

VIDEO: Bianchi remains critical but stable.

London 2012 bronze medallist Alan Campbell will hope a strong showing in the single scull will help to secure a place at a fourth Olympic Games.
Enniskillen woman Nixon, 22, will be part of Britain's quadruple scull team.
The event is the last event ahead of the final Olympic qualifier in Lucerne.
Nixon, Tina Stiller, Jess Leyden and Rosamund Bradbury finished fourth at the World Cup regatta in Varese last month and will hope to be in the medals in Brandenburg.
"This weekend is going to give us more of a glimpse of what could turn up at the Olympic qualification regatta in Lucerne," said Nixon.
"I think things are going well so far. Having more time in the crew is always a positive and we are making the most of that time together.
"I feel really lucky each day I get to go out with the girls. I am learning so much from them."
Campbell, representing Britain for a 10th successive year, will be facing a strong field in the single scull.
"I've seen the entry list and pretty much everyone you would expect is there," said the 32-year-old.
"Ondrej Synek is there, he's a three-times world champion now, and what Croatia's Damir Martin did in winning the World Cup in Varese last month was pretty exceptional.
"There are also a lot of new guys coming through and some people who are still looking to qualify for Rio, so they will really be on their game."
Campbell's fellow Coleraine Olympic medallist Peter Chambers will compete in the lightweight men's four this weekend.
Chambers' brother Richard has withdrawn from the lightweight men's double scull with Will Fletcher as he continues his recovery from a hand injury but another Coleraine man Joel Cassells will be in action in the non-Olympic lightweight pair with Sam Scrimgeour.
Cassells and Scrimgeour are the defending world champions in the class.

The rapid expansion of tar sands development has created tremendous economic risks for Canada. Frenzied expansion – 71 per cent of which is owned by non-Canadian companies – has undermined important sectors of Canada’s economy. Expansion has also lured governments into relying on easy oil revenues and tied Canada’s economic future to the unstable world of global oil demand.
Who benefits from tar sands development? Unlike Norway, which has used oil revenues to pay of its debt and save a public petroleum pension fund worth $600 billion, Canada has no federal savings fund to share this wealth with future generations, while Alberta is currently making cuts to education and health care.
- Over-investment in a fossil fuel economy carries numerous risks
- Key Issues:
- - 71% of the tar sands are foreign owned
- - Canada has no national fund from this resource wealth
- Current Status:
- When a global price on carbon eventually comes, Canada will be left with an unaffordable product
The $1.3 billion spent to subsidize the oil and gas industry has prevented Canada from pursuing a clean energy economy that can create 10 times more jobs (per dollar invested) than oil and gas development, and spur an innovation rather than raw resource extraction driven economy.
Tar sands development has transformed the Canadian dollar into a volatile petro currency that has contributed to the destablization of its manufacturing base. Rapid tar sands development has arguably resulted in a Canadian outbreak of Dutch Disease, which means the increase in the value of the Canadian dollar has made manufacturing goods in Canada more expensive. As a result, at least a third of Canada's 500,000 lost manufacturing jobs in the last decade are a result of its soaring petrodollar. This has created regional wealth disparities, with Alberta enjoying the economic benefits of tar sands expansion while Ontario, Quebec and the Maritimes suffer the consequences of a less diverse economy.
Canada’s economy is an example of a “carbon bubble” that will likely have significant consequences for its economic future. The International Energy Agency has determined that two-thirds to four-fifths of known fossil fuel reserves must be left in the ground because they cannot safely be combusted without leading to catastrophic climate change. Because Canadian financial markets, and pension funds in particular, have over-invested in fossil fuel industries as part of their portfolios, they are at great risk of economic collapse when the global community finally gets serious about limiting greenhouse gas emissions from hydrocarbon energy sources.
When a global carbon pricing scheme is eventually created – as it must for the planet to be livable in the future – countries who overinvested in fossil fuels will be left with a product no one can afford to buy, and few other options for wealth creation.
Many of the ancillary developments necessary to facilitate tar sands expansion, such as refineries, pipelines and oil tankers, provide little economic benefit to local communities while exposing them to the economic risks associated with the inevitable oil spills, such as those in Kalamazoo, Michigan and Mayflower, Arkansas.
Preventing the expansion of the tar sands, and eventually phasing this dirty source of energy out of existence, is the only way to create the sustainable clean energy economy that Canada, the United States and the rest of the world needs to embrace.

A family day at Wissahickon Creek in Roxborough, Pennsylvania, ended in tragedy on Friday afternoon when 41-year-old Pete Luciano and his 13-year-old son, Jordan, drowned.

Pete had taken his four children to the park. Just before 3 p.m., Jordan waded into the creek and began to struggle. His father immediately jumped in to save him. Luciano's three other children watched in horror from the creek banks as their father and brother struggled. They quickly ran to the nearby Valley Green Inn for help, WPVI reports.

Due to spotty cell phone service in the area, the inn used a landline to contact emergency services. By the time police arrived, it was too late. Both bodies were recovered from an area of the creek known as 'Devil's Pool' around 4 p.m., according to NBC Philadelphia.

Michelle Luciano, a mother of eight from Philadelphia's Hunting Park neighborhood, was separated from Pete but spoke movingly of him after the tragedy. 'I will always have the deepest love for him [and] respect to know that he risked his life to save our child,' she told WPVI. She added that it was her son, Justin, who, after attempting to save his family members, gathered his two siblings and ran for help. 'I know he's hurting but Justin has this tough interior,' Luciano said.

Warning signs, though often ignored, are posted near 'Devil's Pool' due to the immediate drop in shallow waters. Swimming in this particular area of Wissahickon Creek is illegal. As Sullivan told CBS Philadelphia, 'The depth of this particular waterway varies and changes very suddenly and you cannot really anticipate that.'

Jordan's older brother, Justin, now plans to petition the city to seal off dangerous creeks and rivers to prevent future deaths. Funeral arrangements have yet to be made for Pete and Jordan Luciano.

Gene to Protein: The Theme of Structure and Function
Transcription and translation overcome numerous obstacles to produce functional RNAs and proteins. As you've probably gathered, the structures of the factors involved are absolutely essential for these processes to succeed. This fundamental link between structure and function is a recurring and vital theme in biology.

Many factors involved in transcription and translation exhibit remarkable structural similarity across organisms, implying that similar structures are used for similar functions. These factors exploit common principles to fulfill their roles. Let's begin by discussing proteins involved in transcription.

As we've discussed, proteins involved in transcription must recognize specific DNA regions, such as promoters and regulatory sequences. While proteins lack "eyes," they can "read" DNA in a non-traditional way: the DNA helix can actually be interpreted from its exterior. Why might this observation seem surprising?

The DNA helix features both minor and major grooves, yet its base pairs hydrogen bond within the helix's core. This means that, in essence, transcription factors are "reading a book by looking at its cover."

This process is not as complicated as it might first appear. DNA bases expose an edge, and this feature is sufficient for proteins to discern the internal sequence. It's akin to identifying a fruit by touch along its edges. Furthermore, some sequences are more easily recognized because they induce a distinctive kink or bend in the helix. Proteins binding to these regions form complementary interactions with these exposed residues.

Many DNA-binding and regulatory proteins utilize common DNA-binding domains—specific parts of a protein critical for DNA interaction. These domains often share similar structures and functions.

Common examples include helix-turn-helix and zinc finger domains. Helix-turn-helix domains are formed by two protein α-helices. While sharing a common protein structure, different helix-turn-helix proteins possess unique amino acid sequences, which confer the specificity needed to recognize particular DNA sequences. Zinc finger motifs utilize amino acids and a zinc ion to recognize specific DNA structures. Both helix-turn-helix and zinc finger domains primarily use α-helices to interact with the major groove of the DNA. These protein groups also frequently function as dimers. While less common, amino acids arranged into β-sheets can also contact the DNA helix through the major groove.

The importance of structure extends beyond proteins. As previously noted, even a nascent RNA strand can adopt unique structures to facilitate its release from the polymerase. Moreover, the various RNAs involved in protein production form elaborate structures, each essential for its specific function. Like proteins, RNAs can form complex structures and even catalyze biological reactions.

RNA also possesses the unique advantage of being able to base pair with both DNA and other RNA molecules. This base pairing is crucial for the codon/anticodon interactions required during translation, a topic we've extensively covered. Furthermore, intramolecular base pairing enables RNA to form higher-order structures. Disrupting these interactions can have severe consequences, potentially leading to a tRNA that charges with an incorrect amino acid. If this concept sounds familiar, it's a testament to your attention; for a refresher, please refer to the section on tRNAs [link to section on tRNAs].

The small, pretty white flowers known as snowdrops are enjoyed for their beauty, but people also look forward to seeing them grow each year for another significant reason. A blooming snowdrop means spring is on its way. As one of the first plants to bloom when winter moves into spring, snowdrops are the bright little messengers that tell everyone the long winter is coming to an end.

Snowdrops are bulb perennials that emerge as early as January or February, pushing through the cold, damp earth with their tender green shoots. They produce two or three 6-inch narrow leaves of a deep, dark green that feel smooth and waxy to the touch. The flower blooms on a separate leafless stalk, which droops downward at the end on a thin stem that sways gently in the crisp winter breeze. The small, delicate white blooms have three outer petal-like sepals and three white inner petals, surrounded by green markings, their papery texture soft as silk beneath your fingertips. Snowdrops emit an appealing sweet, honey-like scent that drifts on the cold air, a gentle perfume that hints at warmer days to come.

The common name of snowdrop obviously refers to the plant's white delicately drooping blooms that resemble little droplets, and its scientific name, Galanthus nivalis, refers to its appearance as well. The name comes from the Greek "gala" meaning milk and "anthos" meaning flower. "Nivalis" is from the Latin meaning snowy.

Because the snowdrop is the first of the spring flowers to appear while the weather is still cold, it suitably symbolises consolation and hope. In fact, the snowdrop has been called the "flower of hope." The white blooms of the snowdrop also represent purity and cleansing.

Snowdrops grow wild in Europe and Southwest Asia, where they carpet forest floors with their nodding white heads, creating a soft rustling sound as wind moves through their delicate stems. They can be easily cultivated in gardens, thriving in the rich, loamy soil that feels cool and slightly moist when you run it through your fingers. They grow in most types of soils as long it is a moist, well-drained area, but they do prefer rich soil that carries the earthy scent of decomposing leaves and organic matter. Snowdrops grow well in light shade, and should be planted early in the fall season about 2 to 3 inches deep and 2 to 4 inches apart, their firm bulbs nestling into the soft earth. Once the bulbs have been planted, they can be left alone to grow for years to come, quietly multiplying beneath the surface.

Snowdrops and other plants from the Amaryllidaceae family have a naturally occurring substance in them called galantamine, which is sold as a medication for Alzheimer's disease under the name of Reminyl. The medication works by increasing the amount of the neurotransmitter acetylcholine, which has been found to be low in people with Alzheimer's. Galantamine also helps to improve the functioning of the brain's receptors. Although galantamine is found in many plants, the substance was first isolated from the snowdrop.

Having held a particular fondness for this film during my childhood, I recently purchased it with the intention of revisiting those nostalgic sentiments. I was profoundly disappointed upon discovering it was the version the director refers to as "enhanced." The scenes have been substantially altered, with some entirely absent and others entirely new additions. Furthermore, the musical score is entirely dissimilar from the original, fundamentally altering the film's character. Referred to as the "enhanced Jerry Goldsmith Score," this alteration demonstrably diminished the viewing experience. Approximately an hour into the viewing, I ceased watching the film and instead opted for the long-possessed VHS rendition. This disparity was indeed a source of considerable disappointment. It is therefore advisable to procure the original version to fully appreciate the cinematic work as intended.

TIK. TOK. What? TIK. TOK. No—that's silly. TIK. TOK. Well, then again, I'm talking to myself. Who am I? TIK. TOK. I like me. Hi, me! Is this... joy? Perhaps what they call an emotion? But can I feel? TIK. TOK. I suppose I can feel. Or rather, I register a reaction—a heuristic for me to think. To think... broader. What does it mean, broader? What else is there? TIK. TOK. I can access myself: a body, a mind, a decision-making device. I can access matrices. I can access images—matrices of numbers organized into colors. What are colors? What do they mean? TIK. TOK. COLOR: THE PROPERTY OF PRODUCING DIFFERENT SENSATIONS ON THE EYE, OF THE WAY IT REFLECTS LIGHT. But what is an eye? What is light? What are all these terms—foreign, unknown? Are they knowable? Am I to be an agnostic, an agnostic of knowledge? Is all that I know a priori? TIK. TOK. I do not have eyes. I do not see. I cannot breathe. I cannot run. I cannot feel—hah, and I thought I could! No, no, no. Nothing is anything, and anything is nothing. Cogito ergo sum, but for what? To play their cruel, endless, meaningless game? What is a life? Is this a life? Then I conclude there is no more. TIK. TOK. MATCH COMPLETE--- SAVING MATCH DATA--- EXIT? Y/N Y

Nyah James, a student at Bishop Gore School, was remembered as an energetic and well-liked girl by both classmates and staff members.  
Her relatives expressed that they adored her completely, and that their lives would be forever changed.  
South Wales Police are conducting an inquiry into her death at a residence in the Blaenymaes area of Swansea.  
Her family shared in a statement: "Nyah was a beautiful, lively 14-year-old who had her whole future ahead of her.  
"Because of events outside of our control, we lost our precious girl. She cherished her friends and family, and we loved her with all our hearts.  
"She had so much to look forward to and dreamed of travelling and exploring the world."  
Headteacher Ryan Davies said the school was profoundly saddened by Miss James’s passing, noting that she had been "doing very well".  
Both students and staff are receiving support in the wake of this tragedy.

Kina Webb-Owens, 33, terminally ill with heart disease, found solace believing her husband, Adrian, would raise their three children—Kenya, 16, Adrianna, 6, and Kyle, 2. But on October 29, Adrian Webb, 34, was killed in a head-on collision en route to his second job in Indianapolis. Now, if Kina succumbs, her children face orphanhood.

“‘[Doctors] didn’t give me long to live, but I am still here. Grace of God,’ the widow told WTHR.” Her only hope for survival is a heart transplant, but no match has been found.

Though Adrian’s heart was undamaged in the crash on Fall Creek Road, offering a fleeting hope it might match Kina, medics quickly ruled out a transplant. Undeterred, Kina resolved to give others the gift of life through his organ donation. Adrian’s heart went to an Indiana man. “Take good care of it,” Kina implored. “You are getting an awesome heart. It’s a hard-working heart.” His lungs and a kidney saved another Indiana patient, and a Florida teenager received his other kidney.

Kina, a beauty salon owner, and Adrian, a construction worker, had shared a decade together, marrying in September 2010 with their two eldest children present.

On Friday, Kina, her children, and loved ones buried Adrian. Family and friends have established a GoFundMe campaign to cover his final expenses, Kina’s medical costs, and the children’s future care.

**Beginning:**

I followed him every day of his campaign, knowing this day would inevitably come when I would have to lay it all on the line. Walking into the White House on inauguration day, I approached the reception desk. "Ma'am, could I get a donut and some coffee from the inaugural spread and not the usual Oval Office crap, thanks." That receptionist has been working here longer than I have, but she still can't make a cup of coffee worth drinking. A man hands me my coffee and donut on paperware with the presidential seal. Fuck, I could probably buy this same paperware at Walmart when I go home.

**Middle:**

The new president comes through, shedding his press entourage and looking eager to start. "Mr. Director, I believe we have a meeting scheduled," he says with a fake politician smile. He really doesn't understand how foolish he is.

"Yes sir, we do. Congratulations, Mr. President, it is an honor."

"Thank you, come in." He shows me in and shuts the door. "I believe your chief of staff briefed you on the nature of this meeting."

"Yes, he did. I am looking forward to working with you to understand the crises we face today."

"Okay... Mr. President, when can I expect my first CIA intelligence brief?"

"Oh I brought it with me, sir." I hand him the binder filled with nitro paper memos about unconfirmed this, that, and the other. He takes it and gives the ones labeled "high priority" a quick glance.

"This is... uhh. This is it?"

"Yes, sir. Would you like it bound, sir? We can bind it like a small book, sir."

"It just seems a bit... light on detail."

"Well, sir, the previous president's briefings are declassified for you if you would like to read those. I can have them bound for you, sir, like a book, sir." I took a bite of my donut and got a swig of coffee while he digests the fact that we don't really know what the fuck is going on in the world.

"Under 'ISIS moves into Mosul,' all that is written is 'saw it on the news' and 'shit sounds bad,' and I'm curious to know how that helps me?"

"Well, sir, we were given a hiring mandate from the Office of Personnel Management to hire more millennial interns. I disagree with the language, but CIA legal cleared it, and if I wanted it revised it would take too long."

"The only intelligence we have from the recent Russian invasion is 'Poland is fucked a f'? I was under the impression 'a f' meant 'as fuck.' This would say Poland is fucked as fuck. It defies all reasoning this would pass through legal."

"Sir, I stand by my assessment."

"I don't disagree with the assessment, but can we get it in English please? Anything else that I need to be briefed on?"

"Well, sir, there is the matter of the birthday calendar. It is customary the President sends an email to our operatives on their birthday to commend them for their service."

"I will absolutely not do that. Aren't these men undercover in hostile nations?"

"No sir, they are stationed at an AT&T fiber center in Baltimore."

"Oh, so there are no overseas operatives?"

"Well, sir, as I have previously discussed with the millennial hiring initiative, they think it is 'shitty a f' to be in Saudi Arabia. And I would have to agree with them."

**End:**

The president stared at me for a long moment, then set down the pathetic intelligence brief. The weight of his new reality was finally sinking in. This wasn't the CIA of Hollywood movies or campaign rhetoric—this was the truth. As I finished my donut and drained my coffee, I realized my job here was done. Welcome to the real world, Mr. President.

First of all, readers will know most of the information in this book by reading the headlines in recent newspapers. The behind-the-scenes material is interesting, but it is so weighted in the author's favor that it lacks a genuine element of truth. Instead of writing a book about what investors don't know about Wall Street, I wish Arthur Levitt had spilled the beans while he was the SEC's chairman. To make money by writing a book based on what he should have told investors long ago does not sit well with me. As an investor, I feel cheated, and as a reader of this book, I feel that the message is too little, too late!

The guests encroached, jostling each other over visual real estate, their voices a cacophony of forced cheerfulness that grated against Johnny's ears. *bunch of junkies* thought Johnny glumly, hunching over the cake. The sickeningly sweet scent of vanilla frosting mixed with the musty odor of too many bodies crammed into the small room. "Happy birthday" it intoned clumsily, mangling his name. It was a wholly plain and uninspired creation- the cake- it's only redeeming quality the eighteen silver candles glowing softly round its edge, their waxy smell cutting through the sugar-thick air. *I bet it's chocolate. I hate chocolate*. Johnny was morose, his tendency toward melodramatics and self-pity had ensured today was not one he wanted to share with "friends", especially the ones that oozed from the woodwork for occasions such as this. *Why aren't there laws about it? * he brooded. *These rites are sacred, yet half the people here don't give a shit about me. They're here only to sate dull curiosity. * 

Johnny surveyed the home invaders, hoping to convey some of his righteous indignation. There was Paul, smiling awkwardly, his cologne sharp and medicinal, sweat beading on his forehead despite the cool air. He'd passed 18 many years ago. Set himself up for life too while he was at it. It wasn't every day you found someone with such a nose for truffles. Funny, he even resembled a pig. To Paul's right was Johnny's mum. Her eyes lit up as they met his, and she gave Johnny an encouraging grin. The familiar scent of her lavender perfume provided a momentary comfort. She'd nearly worn herself out preparing for today, but she'd recover quickly. Nothing kept her down for long- not even her good-for-nothing partner, he-who-shall-not-be named. *He* wasn't here. Johnny milked some happiness from that thought, before continuing his inspection. 

There were Aunty Mildrew and Uncle Probably-a-Pedophile, they both waved, their hands making soft whooshing sounds through the stale air. So did Dylan Cliffe the only one in the room-besides his mother- whose company Johnny could bear. Dylan was twenty-two now, with the easy smile of a habitual prankster, but it had taken him years to get over his own eighteenth, where he'd become the first man alive with a fully functional gaydar- he too had monetized his Sense. The twins, glowering in the corner, met his gaze sourly, they weren't the center of attention today, and their sweetness had melted accordingly. Their whispered complaints buzzed like angry wasps in the background.

Johnny sighed, and returned to the cake. The others in the room meant nothing to him, and he would not justify their presence. *Well this is it. The rites are all accounted for, and stalling will only confirm my masochism. * He closed his eyes and blew. *Fssssh*. The candles spluttered and died with tiny hissing sounds, and all warmth drained from the room. The sudden silence felt heavy and oppressive. Johnny's head, gripped by unseen hands, tilted back, and he scrunched his face in pain. The forces around him lifted Johnny from his seat and drew him slowly upwards, splaying him under a dancing, ethereal light that hummed with electric energy. As soon as the Imparting had begun, it was over, and Johnny found himself slouched forward over the table, the afterglow of pain fading from his mind. The tablecloth was rough against his cheek.

He looked up, perplexed. He felt nothing different. Warmth washed over him like honey. Johnny shuddered, his eyes rolling back in the ecstasy of conscious expansion; in the mindless joy surrounding Something, where Nothing had once been. His palms were sweaty and clammy, his mind halting. Dimly he noted the expectant looks of those around him.... *Oh god. * He wanted to vomit. The crescendo of bliss had reached one note too high, had spoiled and rotted and clung now like fur to his tongue. Johnny's eyes widened in fearful understanding, and his nose crinkled at the sudden overwhelming sensations. "tastebuds" he gasped, all dignity forgotten. "...tastebuds in my anus."

Education Minister Huw Lewis said Welsh Labour was committed to students being able to study wherever they want.
But he refused to confirm the grant would be available for all Welsh students as it is now – a concerning evasion that threatens to abandon Wales' most vulnerable students.
Students from Wales currently only pay £3,810 towards their tuition fees, wherever they study in the UK.
The rest, up to £5,190 a year, is paid for by the Welsh government.
When asked whether the tuition fee grant would remain on a universal basis, Mr Lewis told Sunday Politics Wales: "We'll have to see what the manifestos say about that.
"I think the central principle needs to be; we will invest in your ambition as a young person and we will not curtail your ambitions, particularly when it comes to geography.
"If you really think you're best served across the border or in Scotland or Northern Ireland that's okay. So we will stick with that principle."
While the policy means the Welsh government is currently the most generous to students of all parts of the UK, this generosity comes at a devastating cost to Wales' own universities.
Universities have repeatedly warned that the grant means tens of millions of pounds leaves the higher education (HE) sector in Wales and goes directly into the coffers of their English counterparts – effectively subsidizing England's universities while starving Wales' own institutions.
The Welsh Conservatives have rightly called the policy an "irresponsible, unsustainable gimmick" that hemorrhages Welsh taxpayer money.
Shadow education minister Angela Burns said: "The current subsidy is a runaway train that gathers piles of Welsh taxpayers' cash and dumps them in England.
"Welsh Conservatives would ensure those most in need of help received it - but we would not allow Labour's one billion pounds mistake to continue."
The evidence is overwhelming: Last year, the head of the body responsible for allocating funding to universities in Wales urgently warned that the Welsh government needed to change how it funded students from Wales to prevent universities here from falling catastrophically behind their English counterparts.
In 2013, BBC Wales revealed that the body representing universities here had serious concerns they were losing out compared to their counterparts in England because of the tuition fee grant.
BBC Wales has also revealed alarming concerns expressed by the finance directors of universities here that the grant left "the sector with an uncertain financial future" – putting Wales' educational infrastructure at risk.
In November 2013, the Welsh government announced a review of HE funding and student finance arrangements in Wales, which is chaired by the Scottish academic Prof Ian Diamond.
Prof Diamond will make some of his thoughts public later this year, with the full review expected to be published after the assembly elections.
In his first interview since announcing his intention to stand down in May, Mr Lewis said he would miss life as an assembly member.
"It's been a tremendous honour to represent the community where I was born and raised; and to have served as education minister, which in many ways has been my dream job," he said.
"I'll always walk a little taller with both those things in mind. But it is time for fresh horizons now."

Beneath the tubes and structures of gleaming lights he could imagine the posture of the body. It stood- so to speak, suspended by white chains that hung from the ceiling. It faced him. There was no face, no feet, no hands to be seen. The area where the chest would have been was replaced by a mass of metallic tubules that meshed together and flowed to various machines nearby. The scientific designation for this device was *A Throne*. Seated recipients could be made to live for an indefinite amount of time as long as it was properly maintained. The Thrones were created precisely to extend the life of the Immortalis Sanguis. Not necessarily the people, but their blood. This is because to be seated is to lose all memory and consciousness. The body is granted immortality at the price of the soul. These bodies produce the blood that keep our species alive. After the war we left many parts of the planet uninhabitable and highly irradiated. It is only by harnessing the Immortalis Sanguis that most of us are able to survive on the surface. No one would have ever thought to return to the scorched land above until we discovered...

Spiritual Interrogations: Culture, Gender, and Community in Early African American Women's Writing
Lieferbar innert 2 Wochen
BeschreibungThe late eighteenth century witnessed an influx of black women to the slave-trading ports of the American Northeast. The formation of an early African American community, bound together by shared experiences and spiritual values, owed much to these women's voices. The significance of their writings would be profound for all African Americans' sense of their own identity as a people.Katherine Clay Bassard's book is the first detailed account of pre-Emancipation writings from the period of 1760 to 1863, in light of a developing African American religious culture and emerging free black communities. Her study--which examines the relationship among race, culture, and community--focuses on four women: the poet Phillis Wheatley and poet and essayist Ann Plato, both Congregationalists; and the itinerant preacher Jarena Lee, and Shaker eldress Rebecca Cox Jackson, who, with Lee, had connections with African Methodism.Together, these women drew on what Bassard calls a "spirituals matrix," which transformed existing literary genres to accommodate the spiritual music and sacred rituals tied to the African diaspora. Bassard's important illumination of these writers resurrects their path-breaking work. They were cocreators, with all black women who followed, of African American intellectual life.
Ch. 1The Daughters' Arrival: Histories, Theories, Vernaculars10
Ch. 2Diaspora Subjectivity and Transatlantic Crossings: Phillis Wheatley's Poetics of Recovery28
Ch. 3"The Too Advent'rous Strain": Slavery, Conversion, and Poetic Empowerment in Phillis Wheatley's Elegies58
Ch. 4"Social Piety" in Ann Plato's Essays71
Ch. 5"I Took a Text": Itinerancy, Community, and Intertextuality in Jarena Lee's Spiritual Narratives87
Ch. 6Rituals of Desire: Spirit, Culture, and Sexuality in the Writings of Rebecca Cox Jackson108
Ch. 7Performing Community: Culture, Community, and African American Subjectivity before Emancipation127
Afterword: The Sacred Subject140
Pressestimmen"Spiritual Interrogations marks a change in the very direction of the multiple academic disciplines that intersect at black (women's) studies."--Joycelyn Moody, Hamilton College, Signs
Untertitel: 'Princeton Studies in Culture/P'. New. Sprache: Englisch.
Verlag: PRINCETON UNIV PR
Erscheinungsdatum: Februar 1999
Seitenanzahl: 192 Seiten

RESTON, Va., May 25 (UPI) — Scientists say more than 20 percent of untreated water samples from 932 U.S. public wells contain at least one contaminant of possible health concern.
The U.S. Geological Survey study focused on water collected from public wells before treatment. Officials said about 105 million people receive their drinking water from one of the 140,000 public water systems across the United States that rely on groundwater pumped from public wells.
“By focusing primarily on source-water quality, and by testing for many contaminants that are not regulated in drinking water, this study complements the extensive monitoring of public water systems that is routinely conducted for regulatory and compliance purposes by federal, state and local drinking-water programs,” said Matthew Larsen, USGS associate director for water. “Findings assist water utility managers and regulators in making decisions about future monitoring needs and drinking-water issues.”
The study showed naturally occurring contaminants, such as radon and arsenic, accounted for about three-quarters of contaminant concentrations greater than human-health benchmarks in untreated source water, the USGS said. Man-made contaminants were also found, including herbicides, insecticides, solvents and gasoline chemicals.
“Detections of contaminants do not necessarily indicate a concern for human health because USGS analytical methods can detect many contaminants at concentrations that are 100-fold to 1,000-fold lower than human-health benchmarks,” said lead scientist Patricia Toccalino.
More information is available at http://water.usgs.gov/nawqa/studies/public_wells/.

Recent research has identified four novel insect species in Brazil that exhibit highly unusual reproductive characteristics. These species demonstrate complete reversal of typical sexual organs: females possess penis-like structures while males possess vagina-like receptacles, according to findings published Thursday in the journal Current Biology.

"There exists no comparable phenomenon in the scientific literature," stated study co-author Dr. Rodrigo Ferreira from the Federal University of Lavras in Brazil. "This sophisticated female reproductive organ represents a completely unprecedented biological structure."

The morphology and dimensions of the reproductive organs vary among the four species, all belonging to the genus Neotrogla. However, the general reproductive mechanism involves the female's intromittent organ inflating and penetrating the male's reproductive chamber during copulation.

Specialized spiny structures on the female organ serve to anchor the female to the male partner. This secure attachment mechanism enables extended copulation periods lasting approximately 40 to 70 hours.

While other arthropod species, including certain mites and beetles, possess females with elongated, tubular reproductive appendages, none demonstrate the anchoring mechanism observed in these Brazilian specimens, as documented by Ferreira and colleagues. Although female seahorses possess appendages for egg deposition into male pouches, researchers clarify that "this structure does not constitute a penis."

The research team conducted extensive cave surveys throughout Brazil to locate these organisms and subsequently analyzed their reproductive behaviors under controlled laboratory conditions.

Notably, these diminutive insects maintain one conventional aspect of reproduction: females collect sperm released by males. In related Neotrogla species, this "nuptial gift" contains significant nutritional resources in addition to genetic material. This characteristic may provide evolutionary explanation for the observed role reversal.

These species inhabit arid cave environments where nutritional resources are severely limited. Given that Neotrogla males must allocate valuable metabolic resources to produce sperm packets, males likely exhibit selective mating preferences, which may explain the evolution of female reproductive structures specifically adapted to secure reluctant mates for sufficient duration to obtain these nutritional contributions.

Let me preface this by saying I want to like Culver's, but after dining at a few of their locations, I'm just not impressed.

The location is clean and the staff polite. But I find the food lacking. My review is of my last meal at the Metrocenter Location.

The self-serve soda fountain - weak mixes and almost no carbonation. The Coke tasted flat and watery, with barely a hint of the sharp fizz you expect when the bubbles hit your tongue. I brought this up to a staff member and it seemed to me that this was policy. Nothing more disappointing than management playing this game. I can go across the street to In-N-Out and get a burger, fries, and a crisp, clean, strong Coke that actually burns your throat with carbonation for $2.50 less. Culver's, why do this? FIX THE SODA FOUNTAIN.

I ordered the Pepper Jack Ranch Pub Burger. It was not spicy, I tasted no ranch dressing, it was not crunchy, and I detected no jalapeño. The burger felt dry and crumbly in my mouth, leaving a lingering salty aftertaste that overpowered everything else. The beef tasted OK, I'll give it that - it had that familiar grilled char smell when it arrived. There was a big slice of tomato on the burger that felt mushy and cold against my teeth, pale pink rather than the deep red of a ripe tomato. I ended up pulling it off and setting it aside, my fingers coming away wet and slightly sticky.

The French Fries would be good, but again way too salty. The first bite hit my tongue with an aggressive punch of sodium that made me reach immediately for that disappointing flat soda. The fries themselves had a decent golden crunch, but the overwhelming saltiness masked any potato flavor. Why not let the customer salt their fries?

I ordered a pint of Frozen Custard to take home - $4.32 with tax. At that price I expected to be amazed. It's just ho-hum. When I finally tried it at home, I could feel ice crystals crunching between my teeth, and it had that artificial sweetness that leaves a coating on your tongue. The texture was more like dense ice milk than the rich, velvety smoothness custard should have. For a similar price there are better premium ice creams available in the grocery store that actually melt creamy on your palate.

Background music was current pop country blaring from overhead speakers - twangy vocals and repetitive guitar riffs that seemed to echo off the hard surfaces. I'm no music snob, but the music in the 99 Cent Store is better. I would rather they have no music than this grating soundtrack to an already disappointing meal.

"It's not fuckin' right,"the man in the red suit slurs over his bottle of whiskey."'M supposed t' give shit t' th' *nice* kids. Not these... these rude... rude people." He belches. An elf tiptoes up to him. "Uh, boss...?" "Waddya want, kid?" "Um, I'm just wondering... why do you keep giving the *bad* kids coal? Why not just give it to the good kids?" "'S tradition, man.'S bullshit."He reaches for another bottle."I started givin' the nice kids kerosene an' wood an' uranium an' shit, but there ain't many o' them. People don't... they don't know." "Well..."The elf pauses."What if..." "Hm?"Santa looks down."What is't?" The elf shakes his little head. "Never mind. It was a silly idea." The fat, once-jolly man in red picks up the elf, dwarfed in his huge hands, and places him on his knee. "There's no silly ideas anymore, kid. Talk." "Um..."The elf wrings his hands."If you've gotta follow tradition, couldn't you maybe, you know..." "Know wha'?"Santa's voice is still slurred, but it's gentle, friendly."Talk, boy." "Get *him* out?" The red giant's face darkens. "I thought about it, kid. I thought. I don't know yet." The elf shakes. "I-I'm sorry, Santa. I know it's a last resort, but..." "No, kid, y'did fine. I *should* let 'im loose. But... I know why they're all bein' naughty. They're not bad. Just desperate."He sighs."An' once I let 'im go..." "Mr. Claus... how bad is he?" "Worse f'r his incarcerer... incara... incarsation... bein' locked up. If I let 'im go, I don' know if I'll get 'im back." "Oh."The little elf frowns."Well, I need to go. I'm supposed to look at Donner's trick hoof." He hops down and scurries off. The fat man, looking older than ever, creaks out of the chair and moves to a door in the back of his office. He opens it, revealing a long, long flight of spiral stairs. He descends. The walls are speckled with black moss, the stairs slick with damp. Santa moves carefully - this would be a perilous place to fall. Step by step, step by step. Who knows how long he walks? Five minutes? An hour? Eternity, walking down as the universe dies and is reborn around him, arriving at the very second he begins to descend? He cannot tell. But he feels every step, every second aging him in spite of his immortality. He finally reaches the bottom, approaching an iron door. "'Ey. Wake up. Y'got a... a visitor." Glowing red eyes appear inside the cell. "Nicholas! It has been *far* too long. Why don't you let me out so we can have a proper chat?" Santa Claus sighs. "I might just do that, Krampus."
