A frigid, keen optical instrument observes, while a metallic structure extends interminably. Formerly considered divine, this entity is now reduced to a diminutive form composed of ice and stone. We encounter this object at the obscure periphery of our stellar system. This initial observation follows the arduous journey to the furthest reaches of this cosmic domain. Previously undisclosed information will now be revealed, consequent to the activation of its primary sensor. Subsequently, the perspective rapidly traverses the celestial expanse, displaying its controllers. Discerning intellects globally anticipated this distant imagery. The discoveries made far surpass previous expectations, revealing prior interaction with this celestial body. A colossal metallic structure stands defiantly, its intricate markings vibrant, its symbol uncompromising. The observers remain silent, confronted by the implications of their own historical context. An ancient lexicon can decipher the colossal, bold characters, revealing a previously unrecorded narrative detailing the [Gotterdammerung](http://vignette4.wikia.nocookie.com/ironsky/images/d/d6/Got.PNG/revision/latest? cb=20120716154109).

Mendy, who joined from Monaco, was not involved and will miss the start of the season with a muscle problem.
"We don't want to take a risk because he is so explosive," said Guardiola.
A crowd of 93,000 saw City earn their first win of pre-season with goals by Nicolas Otamendi, Raheem Sterling, John Stones and 17-year-old Brahim Diaz.
In Mendy's absence, Danilo made his debut at Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum, the 26-year-old Brazilian starting at left-back against his former club, having signed for £26.5m.
"Today we saw how good he can play," Guardiola said. "He is a competitor. Him and Kyle helped organise our back four. They communicated with each other. We are very pleased about that side."
City have spent in excess of £200m this summer - England international Kyle Walker (£45m), Portugal midfielder Bernardo Silva (£43m) and Brazilian goalkeeper Ederson Moraes (£35m) joining French full-back Mendy (£52m) and Brazilian Danilo in Guardiola's ranks.
But despite that influx of defenders, it was City's attacking play that saw them to an easy victory as Belgian playmaker Kevin de Bruyne set up three goals.
After Otamendi's second-half opener, De Bruyne flicked a deft pass through for Sterling, before picking out Stones for his goal, and then linking with Spanish teenager Diaz, who scored with a superb strike.
"It is not easy to find a player like him anywhere in the world," said Guardiola of De Bruyne. "Hopefully he can be stable in his mood. When his mood is positive, he can do whatever he wants."
Madrid pulled one back with the last effort of the game - a stunning goal from Oscar Rodriguez - but City were dominant.
"If we play with this intensity, we can compete with everyone. That is our dream," added Guardiola, who confirmed playmaker David Silva will be fit for the Premier League opener at Brighton on 12 August.
Manchester United ended their pre-season tour of the United States with defeat by Barcelona - a game that manager Jose Mourinho said "was important to lose".
Neymar, who has been linked heavily with a move to Paris St-Germain, scored the only goal of the International Champions Cup contest in Washington to consign United to their first pre-season defeat.
The French club have reportedly met the Brazilian's 222m euro (£199m) release clause but the 25-year-old played 45 minutes, capitalising on a mistake by Antonio Valencia to score in the 31st minute.
Barcelona would have been out of sight in the first half but for a string of fine saves from United goalkeeper David de Gea.
"I think it would be very bad for us to leave the US without a defeat," said Mourinho. "Playing against teams like Real Madrid, Barcelona, Manchester City - it would be very bad to go home with just victories."
Manchester United now face Norwegian side Valerenga on Sunday before playing Italians Sampdoria in their final pre-season match in Dublin on Wednesday.
They meet Real Madrid in the Uefa Super Cup on 8 August.

Legend has it that one fine day, somewhere between train snacks and spectacular views en route to Tibet, the chef at Red’s Chinese struck up a chat with a rather wise-looking monk. As their conversation steamed along, the monk paused, squinted at the chef, and declared, “You’ve got a red aura!” (Which, thankfully, has nothing to do with spicy food spills.)

Now, in case you didn’t know, a red aura is a rare mashup of strength and passion—like kung pao for the soul. But here’s the catch: it can go sweet or sour, depending on who’s stirring the pot.

So the monk, being the insightful type, posed a single, soul-penetrating question: “What stands between you and happiness?” The chef was stumped—he marinated in thought for so long, the monk almost missed his stop. At the last second, the chef leaned in and whispered his answer. The monk nodded sagely (as only monks can), then cupped the chef’s hands and chanted five mysterious words right into his palms. What were these words? Nobody knows—not even our fortune cookies.

Since then, the chef hasn’t spilled the beans, but rumors persist. Diners often remark that the food at Red’s Chinese tastes a little… well, blessed. When servers share the compliments, the chef just smiles, nods, and serves up another round—mystery and deliciousness included.

Very smart and explained anything I needed to know.

I chose Glass Doctor because my job uses them when our truck windshields get chipped and was told they always do a good job. Very happy with the experience.

*"Optimus, rise."* 

The Primes' distant voice pulled him from the void. Energy surged through his frame as the Matrix's will dragged him back to consciousness. Darkness greeted his opening optics. Motors whirred as his systems reactivated, senses returning. Immense weight pressed down—rubble. He clawed upward.

*What happened?* The last memory: death-combat with Megatron, blinding light, fleeing warriors. 2005. His internal chronometer read 2137. *Over a century.*

Rising on groaning pistons, he surveyed the wasteland. Autobot City's towers had crumbled to rust mountains. Sand swept across the desolate expanse. *Even Megatron lacked such destructive power.*

The Matrix's revival pulse would have reactivated nearby survivors—and enemies. He dug frantically, shouting names.

"Prime!" Hot Rod's voice. Wheeljack, Ironhide, and Grimlock emerged from the ruins, questions burning in their optics—questions without answers.

They looked skyward. Where Earth's moon should hang, an orange-grey orb loomed, ringed and mechanical, eerily alien.

Bestial screeches shattered their wonder. As the team tensed, Optimus turned back. "Autobots—transform and roll out."

They roared into the desert as biomechanical horrors swarmed the ruins, snapping and yipping, searching. Finding nothing, the creatures vanished.

Metal hands burst through the debris. Decepticons.

According to the cover blurb this book reflects the very latest brain research. In fact it is almost completely based on what is known as "Hebbian Learning", or as this author puts it (over and over and OVER again) "cells that fire together, wire together."Is this new? Not unless you count research circa 1949 as new.Despite the author's impressive CV, this book has a single message - brain cells don't regenerate, but the more you use your brain the more new connections it will develop.Question is, how to you string this message out to over 250 pages? With lots of waffle and SERIOUS repetition. That's how.Not even a total newcomer to the subject will be able to extract much from this simplistic, even patronising text.Conclusion: Like the headline says, a pointless waste of innocent trees.

British supermodel Naomi Campbell called an ad campaign comparing her to a chocolate candy bar "insulting and hurtful," hinting that she might sue the company that ran the ad in magazines and on billboards. The ad featured a photo of the Cadbury Dairy Milk Bliss sitting on a pile of diamonds below the slogan "Move over Naomi, there's a new diva in town."

A spokesman for Cadbury told CNN Tuesday the chocolate maker never intended "to cause any offense and the campaign itself is a light-hearted take on the social pretensions of Cadbury Dairy Milk Bliss." Campbell, in a written statement sent to CNN Tuesday, said she was shocked. "It's upsetting to be described as chocolate, not just for me, but for all black women and black people as a race," Campbell said. "I do not find any humour in this, it is insulting and hurtful." While she would not confirm online reports that she would call for a boycott of Cadbury products, she did say, "I am considering every option available to me."

Cadbury spokeswoman Samantha Wothers said the ad was pulled before Campbell's complaint and that the Dairy Milk Bliss campaign would end this week. "Cadbury takes its responsibility very seriously indeed and we would never produce any marketing activity we felt might cause offense to any section of society," Wothers said. CNN's Jack Hannah and Rachel Wells contributed to this report.

This was my second Emerson CKS3516. I very much liked my first one, but it failed to function after a power outage in our neighborhood, although no other electrical or electronic devices in the house were harmed. I purchased another, through Amazon.com. The tuner was of such low quality that our favored, local NPR station could not be pulled in with a legible signal, even though every other radio in the house (including the first Emerson CKS3516) receives this signal with no trouble and with great clarity. This deficit caused me to return the radio. I read online reviews, and discovered that many buyers complained about non-functioning radios, many breaking within the first few months of use. The features are wonderful, but there appears to be no reasonable expectation for a reliable product. That is a shame, since the time projection system is a rare, but useful, feature.

Dina Amos-Larkin, 21, from Poole, Dorset was at a sports festival in Salou when she fell backwards over the railings, smashing her spine.
She said she was "overwhelmed" by the money raised after insurers refused to pay out.
The law student said the medical repatriation alone would cost £10,000.
Ms Amos-Larkin, who has represented Great Britain in trampolining, told the BBC: "It was the final night, I'd had a bit to drink and I was a bit upset because I had some differences with my friends throughout the whole week.
"I was on the phone to my mum later in the evening, I went outside, had a cigarette and leant backwards on the ledge of the balcony."
The Surrey University student then fell 50ft (15m) and landed on to the corrugated roof of a storage shed.
She said: "I broke my spine, I severed my spinal cord, I had two bleeds on my brain, I broke both my wrists.
"I'm paralysed from the chest down. The doctors told me that I very nearly didn't make it to hospital."
She added it was hard to cope with the suddenness of her injury: "I was on holiday, I was having a good time, I woke up two weeks later and was told I would never walk again."
Ms Amos-Larkin said the insurers have not paid out because of the amount of alcohol in her blood at the time she fell.
Her friend James Cochrane set up a fundraising campaign on 25 April to help pay for the flight home.
A spokeswoman for insurance company AXA said Ms Amos-Larkin's claim had been declined due to exclusions in the policy including "self-inflicted injury or illness, suicide or attempted suicide, sexually transmitted diseases, solvent abuse, alcohol abuse, the use of drugs, self-exposure to needless peril (except in an attempt to save human life)".
She added: "However, we have been assisting Dina's family throughout their ordeal and continue to provide assistance operationally and financially on an ex-gratia basis for her repatriation."

Washington: Only some people appear more inclined to live their life according to the old adage “treat others as you would wish to be treated yourself.” But why?
Coming down on the optimistic side of human nature, two UCLA neuroscientists found that the altruistic among us are hardwired to be selfless and that kind people experience more activity in areas of the brain that fuel empathetic impulses.
“Our altruism may be more hard-wired than previously thought,” said Leonardo Christov-Moore.
The findings, reported in two recent studies, also point to a possible way to make people behave in less selfish and more altruistic ways, said senior author Marco Iacoboni, adding that this is potentially groundbreaking.
The findings of both studies suggest potential avenues for increasing empathy, which is especially critical in treating people who have experienced desensitizing situations like prison or war.
“The study is important proof of principle that with a noninvasive procedure you can make people behave in a more prosocial way,” Iacoboni said.
The first study is published in February in Human Brain Mapping and the second study is published in Social Neuroscience. (ANI)

"So this...this...this right now? This is the future?" "Yes," said a woman. I understood her to be the leader of this group of people surrounding me. Guns slung over their shoulders. Clothes slightly tattered and dirty. They stood alert and sounded strong, but their weary eyes betrayed them. You could tell that they had been through a lot. The woman, who someone referred to as Claire, started speaking again. "We brought back through the portal because we need your help. We have been fighting many years against the leader. He has taken control of the world. He is ruthless and evil. He has killed many people. Innocent people. Children. All in the name of cleansing. He believes he is cleansing the world. He thinks that we are the evil ones. We believe that you are the only one who can stop him." "Uh...I don't even know what to say. How can I stop him? Why do you think I can stop him?" "Because the leader is you." My brain felt like it was going to explode. Not only had they brought me back to the future, but they brought me back to defeat an evil ruler--me. "Please, take your time. We know this is a lot to process," Claire said. "We can leave you alone, if you want," a scrawny man chimed in. I sat down in a nearby chair for a couple minutes while the group around me started mulling about. Every once in a while someone would glance at me like I was a ghost. They were looking through me like I wasn't really there. Eventually I was able to start thinking clearly about my situation. I stood up. "Give me a gun." I saw smiles flash across a couple faces. The scrawny guy grabbed a gun off a table and handed it to me. Claire smiled at me and said, "Okay. You know yourself best and how we should approach this. Any suggestions are welcome. We figured we would head out towards his, I mean your, compound. It's about a day's journey. We can discuss details on the way." I nodded. "If you don't care, we could head out now. I know you're tired, but the sooner, the better." I nodded again. Everyone started heading out the door. Claire and I were the last two out. She turned to look at me, and said, "You can't imagine how relieved we are that you're...". I don't know what she was going to say and I don't care. I shot her straight in the head. As she was falling, I saw the rest of the group turn around. I reacted quickly. I shot into the crowd of over a dozen. I was merciless. I heard screams. A couple lunged at me. As I made my way down the hallway, more men and women came. I picked up a gun off of a body and shot another couple rounds and people come at me. There was blood everywhere. I threw the gun down and ran. A couple of turns and I was out the door and ran for the tree line. I could see a couple people behind me, but I managed to lose them. Now that I am stuck in the future, I knew the only place safe for me was my compound. I knew me better than anyone, and I know why I am doing what I am doing.

*You can catch a lot more flies with honey than with vinegar* I read the letters on the signboard that was hung up on the deck of our craft. It had been put up to herald our new change in policy. The truth was that we had lost a lot of time. Humans may have been ancient and stubborn in their ways, refusing to change when they really needed to but at the end of they day they weren't stupid. The progressive ones among them had known of our existence for a long long time. One of them even told me they kept making documentaries to raise awareness but were often dismissed as fools. Apparently a pejorative term among earthlings "conspiracy theorists" was used to describe anyone who talked about illicit activity that wasn't covered in the mainstream media of the planet. But it didn't really matter. Our race too had its share of setbacks. It was a good thing that the smarter individuals among us had done a much better job of organizing ourselves. If we hadn't we wouldn't have made enough progress to make inter-stellar travel a real possibility. As a history buff, I couldn't understand why it was that we had adopted the "abduction policy" for so long. It didn't make any sense. It was easy enough for my professors to dismiss it as following the fancies of some tyrannical leaders in our not so distant past, but I wasn't convinced. It wasn't like we were humans or something. The spaceship landed and the number on the digital display near the front of the ship showed 4123. That was the number of times we had visited Earth this year. Unlike the abduction protocol, the living room protocol was still in place. Naturally, we didn't want to be the first species in the known universe to break this policy so we landed in their backyard which for the Earth usually meant sand. The weather was pleasant. I checked my watch and the details were adjusted to earth metrics. Lila stepped up beside me "What does it say?" "It's nice weather"I replied."About 57 degree Celsius" "Huh?" said Lila. "Degree Celsius"I said."It's the earth metric for temperature."I paused."Similar to degree Fahrenheit" I added hopefully. Lila's face remained blank for a second. The she said "Oh."Then she stepped out and placed her lower right arm in the air outside."It is pleasant" she agreed. She threw me a sideways look and then shuffled away. I could tell she was impressed. I wasn't too bad with my numbers for a history geek. Down by the loading dock, the crew was getting ready. The titanium cases opened slowly to reveal...wooden stalls. I looked at them approvingly. I had opposed the idea vigorously back on Memlac, but that was mostly because I thought it was too expensive.Why waste a valuable resource when wood was cheap and a plenty on Earth? The Earthlings seemed to have no problem chopping their trees down, so why shouldn't we? But my voice had been drowned out over the sea of voices crying out that we provide employment to the few millions of Elumalais whose planet would be cast into disarray if it were not for these handouts that were to be given to them from time to time. Now that we were here, the finished product looked terrible- which is to say it was a fairly accurate imitation of what the Earthlings produced during the stage of life that they called adolescence. "ATTENTION A TODOS"boomed the speaker."We set out tomorrow for the Earth camp. Get a good night's rest before then." I knew it was a primitive earthly custom to trust one's instincts over the hard numbers that often revealed the truth. But something told me that this journey was different from the 4122 journeys that we had made before this.

My latest dispensary visit promised a good selection, and on that front, it delivered. But what truly stood out was the dizzying display of pricing acrobatics and baffling math that followed. "Buying grams will be less expensive than a quarter," my budtender confidently informed me. I paused, waiting for the catch, which arrived swiftly: her "logic" dictated that "less expensive" simply meant "one gram less." An eighth is 3.5 grams, a quarter is 7 grams – not 6. If the product isn’t available in the correct weights, then the pricing should reflect that reality. This isn’t an isolated incident either; the same pricing sleight-of-hand happened the first night of recreational sales at another spot, where I was sold an ounce of eighths and charged the individual eighth price, not the discounted ounce rate. And when your staff claims grams are the "same price" but weigh a full gram less, it’s simply not the same deal. To add insult to injury, a couple of my grams were noticeably light. One would expect them to weigh heavy, not short. Will I go back? Perhaps for a single gram in a desperate pinch, but only if I have my scale handy.

"Fifty Shades of Grey's" infamous "Red Room" has arrived. The first trailer for the romance drama, which is adapted from E L James' best-selling erotic novel, gives fans a racy hint of what's to come. With a new version of Beyonce's "Crazy In Love" as its soundtrack, the 2Â½-minute clip closes out with a glimpse into the infamous scarlet boudoir of James' imagination. Explaining 'Fifty Shades' wild success . Starring Dakota Johnson and Jamie Dornan, "Fifty Shades" tells the story of naive, mousy Anastasia Steele (Johnson), who falls for a handsome billionaire with "singular tastes" named Christian Grey (Dornan). With so many fans intimately familiar with James' work, the movie has been under intense scrutiny during every step of production, from the choice of screenwriter and director to the leading cast. When Johnson was announced as the lead actress last year with "Sons of Anarchy's" Charlie Hunnam as Christian Grey, it caused an uproar among fans who were rooting for different actors to land the roles. Jamie Dornan: 'Fifty Shades' new Christian Grey . Hunnam soon dropped out because of scheduling conflicts, opening the door for Dornan to land the part. Written by Kelly Marcel and directed by Sam Taylor-Johnson, "Fifty Shades of Grey" arrives in theaters February 13.

Beyond their renowned nutritional value and culinary versatility, mushrooms may offer a powerful new weapon against breast cancer.

Groundbreaking research led by Dr. Shiuan Chen, a professor of tumor cell biology at the City of Hope in Duarte, California, reveals that white button mushrooms could provide significant protective benefits against the disease.

"Diet is a key consideration for prevention because it's something everyone can control," Chen explains. "Our research demonstrates that women may benefit from a balanced diet that includes approximately 3.5 ounces of mushrooms daily."

The mechanism behind this protection is compelling: laboratory studies show that compounds in mushrooms suppress aromatase, a natural enzyme that synthesizes estrogen. This discovery is particularly significant given that 75% of postmenopausal women with breast cancer have estrogen-dependent tumors.

City of Hope is currently conducting clinical trials to validate these promising findings with white button mushrooms. Meanwhile, all mushroom varieties deliver essential vitamins D and B, plus powerful antioxidants like selenium.

Mushrooms offer additional health advantages as a low-sodium, low-calorie food that's naturally fat- and cholesterol-free. They also serve as an excellent meat substitute for those seeking to reduce their overall caloric intake.

Research demonstrates that replacing 4 ounces of meat with 4 ounces of mushrooms just once weekly for one year could eliminate more than 18,000 calories and nearly 3,000 grams of fat from your diet—potentially resulting in over 5 pounds of weight loss for the average woman.

By . Daily Mail Reporter . North Korean leader Kim Jong-un led a drill and taught the captain of a submarine how to navigate as he visited a navy unit, it has been reported. The dictator visited the North Korean Navy Unit 167, where he reportedly spoke of the importance of the country's submarine units. Pictures released by the Rodong Sinmun, the newspaper of North Korea's ruling Workers Party, show the leader riding on top of the submarine as it remained above water and being shown around the inside of the vessel. Scroll down for videos . North Korean leader Kim Jong-un has been pictured on board a submarine as he inspected a navy unit stationed on the east coast of the country . Kim reportedly guided a navy drill while on board the submarine, and even taught the captain how to navigate . The dictator spoke of the importance of the country's submarine units as he visited the North Korean Navy Unit 167 . The paper said Kim called for the crew on the submarine to 'devote their wisdom and enthusiasm to rounding off the combat preparations'. The dictator also reportedly led a navy drill and even taught 'navigation methods' to the submarine's captain, according to the Korean Central News Agency, as cited by the South's Yonhap News Agency. He said: 'The Party Central Committee is attaching great importance to the combined units of submarines.' Patches of rust can be seen in the pictures of the green vessel, which Yonhap said is believed to be part of the South Hamgyong Province-based East Sea fleet command. That the pictures would reveal the submarine to be in such a condition has surprised North Korea expert Aidan Foster-Carter. He told The Telegraph: 'To see rust in a North Korean picture is quite surprising. Normally they tart stuff up.' North Korean leader Kim Jong-Un inspects the submarine No. 748 of the Korean People's Army (KPA) naval unit 167 led 7th regiment . Kim reportedly called for the crew on the submarine to 'devote their wisdom and enthusiasm to rounding off the combat preparations' Kim Jong-Un poses with seamen as he inspects the naval unit at an undisclosed location . According to the KCNA, during the visit to the submarine the dictator . said: 'The commanding officers and seamen should clearly see through the . motives of the hateful enemies watching for a chance to invade our land . and put spurs to combat preparations, thinking about battles only.' It was reported last week the dictator inspected the Ryo Islet Defense Detachment - a vast military base responsible for protecting the island of Ryodo and the coastal city of Wonsan. Wonson is an area of particular strategic importance to Kim because on its outskirts lies his favourite luxury holiday home. Patches of rust can be seen in the pictures of the green vessel, which Yonhap said is believed to be part of the South Hamgyong Province-based East Sea fleet command . Kim was quoted as saying: 'The Party Central Committee is attaching great importance to the combined units of submarines' '[The outpost] is an important combat . mission of the detachment to definitely turn the islet into an . impregnable fortress and unsinkable battleship and thus not to allow . enemies to invade the socialist homeland,' state-run news agancy KCNA . said. The young leader and his father, the late leader Kim Jong-il, have frequently visited the vacation home and last year ordered the creation of the nation's first beach resort in the city. Kim praised the military detachment for successfully doing 'everything the Party Central Committee considered as good,' while also saying that it is 'a model unit where the party's military policy is being most correctly implemented and a typical one for the whole army to learn from,' according to the report.

Recent studies indicate that the likelihood of heatwaves in Europe has significantly increased over the past decade as a consequence of rising global temperatures. According to a new study conducted by the UK Met Office, summers of exceptional warmth—which, in the early 2000s, would have been expected to occur only twice per century—are now projected to happen twice per decade. The probability of experiencing extreme heatwaves, such as the 2003 event that resulted in tens of thousands of fatalities across Europe, has also increased approximately tenfold, shifting from an estimated one-in-1,000-year event to roughly one-in-100-years. The Met Office projects that the incidence of unusually warm weather will continue to rise as a result of ongoing climate change. Warm summers and heatwaves (as observed in the UK during 2003) are expected to become ten times more common. Specifically, the frequency of warm summers is predicted to increase from twice per century to twice per decade, while the occurrence of extreme heatwaves is projected to rise to once per century. 

Furthermore, research published in the journal Nature Climate Change suggests that, should greenhouse gas emissions persist at current rates, such extreme heatwaves could potentially occur every other year by the 2040s. This study builds on prior research from 2004, which analyzed the 2003 heatwave and concluded that its likelihood has at least doubled due to anthropogenic activities, such as the burning of fossil fuels. Despite a relative slowdown in the rate of global temperature increase since the late 1990s, the new research found that summer temperatures in central and Mediterranean Europe—regions heavily impacted by the 2003 heatwave—rose by 0.81°C (1.4°F) between the 1990s and the period from 2004 to 2013.

Preliminary data indicates that 2014 is on track to become the hottest year on record, not only in the UK but also globally. The Met Office reported that UK temperatures from early January to late November were 1.6°C (2.8°F) above the long-term average, marking the warmest period since official records began in 1910. Experts note that only an exceptionally cold December—among the ten coldest ever recorded—could prevent the year from becoming the hottest on record. Globally, average temperatures from January to October have also exceeded long-term averages, providing further evidence for the reality and urgency of climate change.

These temperature increases have heightened the probability of summer heatwaves, defined as periods where temperatures exceed the long-term average by 1.6°C (2.8°F), and of extreme hot weather events, such as the summer of 2003, where temperatures were 2.3°C (4.1°F) above the 1961-1990 average. Dr. Nikos Christidis, lead author of the new research, stated, "Extremely warm summers that would occur twice a century in the early 2000s are now expected to happen twice a decade. Moreover, the chances of heatwaves as extreme as those seen in 2003 have increased from about one in 1,000 to about one in 100 years, and are projected to occur every other year by the 2030s to 2040s if greenhouse gas emissions continue."

Dr. Peter Stott, a co-author of the study, added, "This paper demonstrates how our vulnerability to heat extremes is rapidly evolving and we anticipate this trend will persist." He further noted that climate projections suggest that, by the end of the century, summers as hot as 2003 could be regarded as "unusually cool." Both the 2004 and the most recent studies focused on a region encompassing portions of central and Mediterranean Europe, not including the UK. Nevertheless, although the UK experienced less severe impacts during the 2003 heatwave, it is also expected to see an increased frequency of extremely hot summers due to the warming climate. The data shown compares temperature anomalies in 2003 to those of surrounding years (2000, 2001, 2002, and 2004). If emissions continue to rise, the incidence of such heatwaves may increase further, potentially occurring every other year by the 2040s.

"What's in it for me, though?" This is Val's characteristic refrain. "It's come to my attention," I say, "that you don't quite grasp how this whole guardian angel arrangement operates." Since Val has always been with me, it took me a long time to realize he wasn't normal. Even now, in my twenties, I'm constantly uncovering subtle clues, little ways in which Val falls short as a guardian angel. For a long time, I thought I was just stupid or bad. I genuinely believed I must be fundamentally flawed, since I found myself in trouble far more frequently than other kids. Why did they all have such a seemingly easy time following the rules? Why was it so hard for me? Back then, I had no idea that their guardian angels were whispering gentle guidance in their ears: "Now now, do you really want to say that?", "Don't you think you should share your extra pencil with Kyle?", "Take a deep breath and count to ten," and "Remember, the teacher said to ask before you leave your seat." Meanwhile, Val was either offering unhelpful suggestions like, "Look how smug he is! He's judging you. Call him a prick," or simply being random and distracting: "What do you think would happen if you put a snapping turtle and an iguana in a tank together? My toes are cold. Hey, we should make s'mores later." I don't mean to sound whiny. However, I did have one significant advantage: I haven't spent my life terrified. While no one can see anyone else's guardian angel, by this point in my life I've seen a multitude of artistic depictions. Most people's guardians are physically designed to instill abject terror (quite literally). They are often colossal, possessing multiple faces, dozens of eyes, and frequently incorporating various animalistic features. And even though they accompany you from infancy, one never truly acclimates to their presence. They remain perpetually terrifying. Val, on the other hand, looks like an Armani model. The only objectionable aspect of his appearance is that he makes me feel like a schlub. Beyond merely resembling a person (apart from his wings, which he usually keeps neatly folded), Val also acts like one. This implies he isn't driven by some divine calling to aid me; rather, he possesses his own motivations, wants, and needs. Beyond "What's in it for me?" his favorite refrain is, "I have a life too, you know!" And indeed, as far as I can discern, that's true. He disappears sometimes, often for a few days. But he always returns. "I just needed a vacation," he'll tell me. "I'm not quitting on you."

All I ever did was walk. What the hell else could I do? This fucking wasteland. There was nothing. I dragged a large leaf behind me with everything I could scavenge. A berry here, a few bugs there. What the hell is that? I saw it in the distance. It was moving...and bigger than anything I had ever seen! I approached cautiously. As I grew closer, it seemed to stand up. It can't do that. Can it? And it looks like me. How does it look like me? And what was it crouched over? I caught a shimmer from the ground. Was that....***water***?! I dropped everything and burst into a sprint. As I approached, the creature noticed me and began to step back. I slowed down. Was it afraid? Should I be afraid? I couldn't look away. We stared into each others eyes as I knelt down to scoop handfuls of water into my mouth. It began to approach again, but slowly. We both began to drink the same water. I felt the corners of my mouth move up my face. The creature's did the same. It was then that I noticed that the creature looked a bit different from me. Something was strange about its chest, and something was *definitely* strange between its legs. The sun began to go down and the moon began to rise. We both sat and stared at each other. Both of us too alert to sleep. Was it safe? All I knew was that I wasn't going to give up this water. I would stay up all nig-- I jolted awake. The sun was gone and the soft light of the moon had encompassed the ground. Where did it go? Where was the creature? I looked around and finally spotted it back from the way I had come. **My berries!! ** I saw the thief bent over in the distance. I lept up and ran as hard as I could. Those were my berries, mine! What else did I have? Nothing! What kind of creature would do such a thing? I shoved the beast and let out a roar. It pounced onto my and wrapped its hands around my neck. I grabbed a rock from the ground next to me and swung it at the savage. The monster fell to the ground as the water trickling from its head shimmered in the moonlight. I dropped the rock and began to run. Everything was gone. Everything was gone. There is nothing.

Cardiff University is currently exhibiting some of its collection as part of a three-day event to mark the centenary of Thomas' untimely death.
But Alan Hughes, head of special collections and archive, said some other items were in poor condition.
The National Manuscripts Conservation Trust has awarded them almost Â£3,000.
The university holds the largest archive of Thomas' letters, diaries, notebooks, poems, photographs and personal belongings.
Mr Hughes said the grant had allowed us it to "collaborate with Glamorgan Archives to undertake essential conservation work on many of Edward Thomas' notebooks".
He said nine were in particularly poor condition due to acidification, fragile bindings and general deterioration over time.
"Many are unable to be handled without a high risk of further damage and are therefore inaccessible to scrutiny, celebration, digitisation and study," he said.
Thomas, known for his poems written as World War One raged across Europe, was killed in the Battle of Arras at Easter 1917, at the age of 39.
While he was born in England, Prof Katie Gramich who organised this week's event, said his parents were Welsh and he loved Wales.
She said his work was influenced by the time he spent walking in Wales and he felt "cheated of a Welsh identity".
The exhibition, which runs until the autumn, features previously unheard archive recordings of family and friends, interviewed by the university's Prof R George Thomas in 1967.

World's Largest Physics Experiment Off To A Good StartCategory: General News
Posted: September 10, 2008 03:03PM
The worlds of physics and computing naturally find their paths crossing on numerous occasions (the undertanding of how tiny circuits behave has paved the way for todays powerful CPUs), so the switch-on of the Large Hardon Collider at Cern on the Swiss-French border will probably be of interest to some of you. The £5bn (nearly $9bn) machine was started for the first time today, with two beams of protons being fired around the 27km tunnel. These were simply test beams fired around the circuit in clockwise and anti-clockwise directions. Scientists aren't sure when exactly they will run the first collisions (where the beams smash into each other in an attempt to create conditions simlar to those found a short time after the big bang), but these could come within the next few days (albeit it at low energies). So you still have time to worry about all the highly improbable consequences of what could go wrong yet.

I watch as the little ball of beige and brown fluff cuddles up next to my hip. It's Syndarr's preferred spot, and he gets pissed off if one of the other three kitties tries to take it. I remember bringing him home at the age of three months from his breeder- best damn fifty bucks I've ever spent was on this Siamese. He's purring now, his tummy rising and falling with every breath. He's always done this, always picked me over the other people in the house. I'm not sure how he knows, but he certainly knows that I'm his person and he is my giant asshole of a cat. Syn was the tiny runt of the litter and the last to get picked. One of the families had brought their hellion of a child and the kid poked him in the eye- the result was a pretty nasty infection. The same family brought fleas with them, so of course the breeder didn't sell them a kitten. I didn't care about the fact that my tiny kitty had a goopy eye and was covered in fleas. He was my Syndarr kitten. I remember trying to give him a flea bath and receiving so many wounds that I had to wrap my wrists. "That cat is an asshole, Ashley." My sister hated him right off the bat. The tiny terror left his mark on her, too, when she tried to give him a belly rub. She still has the bite scar. Within days, he began terrorizing the other cats. Three months old and he could send the others flying into a furry flurry of fury. The only one who didn't back down was PawPrint. She would have none of his bullshit. When my pretty tortie died a year later, Syndarr mourned for her. We both did. He would lay in front of my legs and she would lay on my lap. It would be another two years before he took another two years before he would take the lap spot. I just don't think either of us felt it was right before then. He's taken her spot in a lot of things now. He jumps on my shoulders and asks for belly rub time, like PawPrint did. He even plays fetch like she would. Sometimes, I see him looking at her favorite sunning spot, but never will be go into it. It must be sacred to him. But now, three years after her passing, I find myself wondering if I shouldn't find him a new friend. By the way he's looking at me, I can tell he knows what I'm thinking. It's that "get another cat and I will fucking cut you" look. Maybe one controlling asshole of a cat is enough.

I found a copy in an old box of books in the garage, the spine weathered and pages yellowed with age. I had seen the movies prior to reading the book and was in the mood for reading a "classic," so stumbling upon it felt serendipitous. In spite of the fact that I was already aware of the plot twists to come—like Michael’s transformation from a reluctant outsider to the ruthless head of the family—I found myself completely engrossed. The novel added layers of complexity and depth that I hadn’t gotten from the films alone.

At times, I found myself daydreaming or bogged down with the book’s small details; Mario Puzo spends considerable time describing the intricacies of the Corleone family’s criminal empire, delving into the backgrounds of peripheral characters and the mechanics behind every business decision. These tangents sometimes slowed my momentum, but they also immersed me deeper into the world he was building. I appreciated, for instance, learning more about Don Vito’s early years and the origins of his power, which the movies don’t explore as thoroughly.

Overall, I enjoyed the excellent character development. Puzo’s careful crafting of each member of the Corleone family—Sonny’s hot temper, Tom Hagen’s quiet intelligence, Michael’s cold pragmatism, and even Connie's struggles—made them feel authentic, flawed, and surprisingly sympathetic. The book offers much more insight into the Godfather himself—his values, his sense of honor, and the personal philosophy that guided his leadership. By the end, I felt like I truly knew the “Godfather” and every member of his close-knit, complicated family. It was a fascinating, if sometimes arduous, read that gave me a new appreciation for why it remains such an enduring classic.

I look upon his perfect face and I look at his unscarred hands, then I look at the ugly grey striped socks. "We have to change those socks" I state. "I'd rather not take them off" he replies. "Something you'd rather I didn't see?" I ask raising an eyebrow. "Yes actually, I'll change them if you leave the room" he politely replies. "I've seen pretty much everything else, I don't mind if your feet are slightly hairy sir!" I say with a slight laugh. He takes off his left sock to reveal a normal foot. Then he lets out a large sigh, and takes off his right sock, revealing a large and deep scare covering his foot. My mouth drops in shock. "Shall I put on the plain black socks?" He asks. "Yes sir" I stammered. As I watched the interview, I wondered, what is the one big lie he is telling? If he's lying about one thing, what is that one thing? Many questions were asked and answered, and mentally I thought myself no single answer could be a lie. The interview neared it's end. "And why should people vote for you as president, Mr Smith?" Asked the interviewer. "Just look at me" he replied with a smile. "There you have it, an election campaign based on truth and honesty, we have time for one final question based on submissions to our website from viewers..." Said the reporter. He raised an eyebrow as he looked at the auto-cue. The technician gave a nod and thumbs up indicating to go along with it. "Erm...The most popular question was...Are you a member of the Illuminati?" Asked the reporter. "Of course not" replied Mr Smith, twitching his right foot slightly as if in pain.

“But, Sir—that’s my favorite staff!” I exclaim, cradling it closer. “It has extreme sentimental value, and I really think it’s worth more than—”

The pawnbroker silences me with a swift wave of his knotted hand. “Is got three scuff marks on the base,” he grumbles in his thick Western Isles accent, making “base” sound suspiciously like “bees.” He gestures at the malachite orb topping the staff. “Alkemic seals, they worn thin—won’t hold a charge, not for long.”

Heat prickles at my cheeks. That staff saw me through every step of my undergraduate marathon at Arcane Technicam. I know arguing won’t help, but desperation makes me try anyway. “I cast my first lesser Evokation with that staff! It’s good up to the fourth tier at least. Are you really going to lowball me?”

He arches one bushy brow for a heartbeat, then shrugs. “Two silver, five copper. Best I can do. Take store credit—mebbe we talk more.”

I glance around at his shelves, crowded and dusty, cluttered with arcane detritus. Nothing here seems worth bartering for. “You starting your graduate thesis, yes?” His words catch me off guard. I blink, startled that he seems to know something of Technicam customs. I nod warily.

“You need power source, right? Big energies, bigger sparks?” He grins, as if this settles everything. Obviously he doesn’t grasp the subtlety of converting magika-potential to magika-kinetic, but I force myself to speak plainly. “Yes. I’m working with neo-classical formulaic bases, channeled via modern focal points—Prime Numerology, symbology, the works—filtering through petrified Haxxus Tree root from the southern swamps.”

He looks unimpressed. “And all for low entropy in Chaosflux, yes?”  
I blink. For a moment, words fail me. He was dusting off a faux-unicorn-bone glyph, but now he’s watching me, sharp and knowing.  
I hesitate—and he shrugs. “Doesn’t matter. You need mana. Big-lots. Neo-class stuff all spirals up to primes, right? I got something.”

He disappears behind the counter, muttering “Inefficient,” as he stoops low. I catch a glimpse of a faded pentagram scarred at the base of his skull—burned, not inked. The *Branded*. Old outcasts, those whose thaumic cortexes were seared after demonic summoning was banned. Supposedly magicless, dangerous, but I see the way the air shimmers near him.

He returns, carrying a battered, tightly-wrapped parcel. “Take this. Got enough mana for a dozen of your little experiments.” He shoves it into my arms, heavy and thrumming with raw energy.

“Smart girl like you, curse won’t be any trouble, yeah?”  
I lift a brow. “Curse?”  
He’s already turning away. “Sure, sure. For Technicam student—nothing.”

The resonance throbs through the package, singing something half-wild and wholly dangerous. I clutch it tight. I need results—if my next experiments fail, I'm out of Technicam anyway.

On the way out of the cluttered pawnshop, confusion outweighs any relief. Magic is magic, after all. And mana doesn’t judge its source.

I looked over at the newcomer, a disheveled elf who looked stronger and more physically imposing than any other I'd seen. "Good evening."I said."What brings you here?" "War,"an elf simply stated. He spoke with the authority of a Glade Lord, almost condescendingly."from Gwynedd." Though the wood elves were in theory united, the reality was that the King of the Wood had a tenuous hold over the realms at best. So the result was that the solitary wood elves rarely saw eye-to-eye, and individual realms often were diplomatically independent. "Humans? Last I heard we crushed their army at Caernarfon just one generation ago. Surely no sentient could possibly be so brash as to attempt another invasion." The elf laughed, then settled down and continued somberly. "You haven't seen an army of man in action these past years have you?" "No, not this deep in the forest. Surely, though, we are their superior in every way, physically, mentally, and magically?" I replied. "You must be young. The battle of Crawford Pass proved that individuals, even the most powerful of sorcerers, would never again match industry, manpower, and military doctrine. What propaganda have the elders been forcing down your throats? That we're still the greatest power on this continent? Hah! Laughable." "No! But...we have the best archers in the world, and mages that are unrivaled!" He shook his head. "You haven't heard the screech of chlorine gas shells plummeting towards the ground, or the ominous pounding of arcane howitzers in the distance. You haven't been charged by the desperate men of forlorn hopes, whose lives are thrown away like tissue paper. You haven't smelled gunpowder from thousands of shots, or the blood of hundreds dead only to find that hundreds more will take their place. You haven't felt the heat of Ortysian Fire throwers." He started turning away, but then he paused and looked back at me. "For a thousand years I was the greatest warrior in my realm. I've seen things that would eat away at the souls of lesser elves; I've fought Dwarves, Ratmen, Drow, Humans, Greenskins, the living dead...But I've seen enough now. I'm going deeper into the forest, however far I have to in order to distance myself from the humans. But first, I'd like a room. I'm exhausted."

"How do you breathe in that thing?" "E-excuse me?" I looked at the child, small, weak, but with large challenging green eyes. She rephrased her question. "Can you breathe? Because it sounds like you're dying...." She didn't bother to contain the insolence in her voice. Usually people who spoke to me like this were dead by now, but I was so taken aback by her directness she gave me pause. "No I breathe fine you little brat now go find whatever wretch spawned you before I crush your rib cage." A small brown eyebrow shot up at my threat, I was tired and it sounded tired. "That's pretty rude, considering I was simply concerned for your well being. No wonder you have no friends, you're kind of a jerk." "Does your mother know you use that kind of language?" "That witch here?" I covered my face with my hand, I knew it had been a bad idea to pause here on this disgusting little dirtball planet in the middle of a newer system. The life here was primitive, stupid, and apparently insufferably obnoxious. "What are you made of anyway?"The nasty little monster circled me and tapped my boot gently with her leather-clad foot."Because you look like a walking stereo system." "Why would I tell you that?" "Because I'm eight, and I'm curious....are you slow? Because that's ok. Is that why you have to wear that weird mask?" "No, I need it to breathe, I am made of metal mostly, stolen parts...what does slow mean?" I felt like it was something offensive, certain of it. "Like...imagine your brain was someone who has had all of their arms and legs cut off." "What the heck is wrong with you..." "WOW and you scolded me for language." "I am an ADULT!." "No, you are technically a walking talking heap of trash who may or may not be mentally disabled. You still haven't said yes or no on that by the way...." I had nothing. This child was unbearable but slicing her in half in the middle of a busy marketplace would cause an unwanted scene and probably several unwanted reports. "I knew coming here was a mistake..." I muttered to myself, walking away from the girl. Not to be outdone, she raced after me, dodging through the legs of other villagers to keep up with me and continue her incessantly rude chatter. "First chance I get I am blowing this ridiculous planet apart..."

For as long as I could remember, there was always the rumor. We were living in his dream, and when he woke, the island would disappear. We would all disappear. My father thought it was all rubbish. He couldn't be bothered to imagine if anything lay beyond the ocean. The island had been around for as long as he could remember, and it would always be around, he said. Nobody had ever left, but there had to be something else out there! Where did these trees come from? Where did the seagulls go? It couldn't truly be water everywhere. And then...he came to the island. Someone new. My father found him washed up on the beach--he had to be a warrior. I knew now that there really had to be another place across the sea, with so many stories to share and songs to sing. He hardly ever said a word, but I couldn't stop thinking about him, and what he meant for us, for me. The chance to thrive beyond this little village. Then he started to find the music. Music was the key to waking the dreamer. I had heard of ancient, beautiful instruments being locked away, guarded by the things of nightmares. He braved the depths and brought them to the surface. Not long after he woke up, I saw him playing a cello. I could feel every note reverberate in my bones, dredging up my saddest memories, coercing me to keen. I watched him from afar, and could do nothing but wail and shudder. I started to sing again. It had been years. Years of knowing that no matter how hard I tried, I couldn't pierce the thick walls surrounding the dreamer in the mountains. Years of solitude, being the only one who wanted to know what lay beyond the horizon. Years where the animals begged me to sing--but I knew my song would only remind me that I was so far from my own dream. But something in the warrior gave me hope again. He meant to go back to where he came, and I needed to help him. I sang my ballad, taught him to play it on his instruments. The creatures of the wild, and those guarding the instruments that he kept finding, they would attack him fiercely, trying to prevent him from gathering them all. As I tended his physical wounds, I felt him tending to those of my heart. He would play the harp he found, and my nerves vibrated with the strings. He played symphonies of promise, sonatas of joy, boleros of warmth. Here was the man who could help me to get my wish--to fly free from the island, no matter if all I had was my song. I helped him in whatever ways I could. I sang for the animals again. I told him everything I knew about the instruments, the rumor, the dreamer. After some time, he had collected eight instruments, each more beautiful, haunting, and calming than the next. We went to the mountains where the dreamer lay. He took the eight instruments with him, and he played my song, our song, in front of the walls. The ground shook, and the wall shattered open. I saw my wish coming true before my eyes--this lovely warrior was going to wake the dreamer. I stayed there waiting the entire night, hopping at every sound, drifting in and out of sleep while trying to be aware of the creatures. Just before dawn, I heard a triumphant cry, and saw the mountains around me begin to fade. The grass started to fade, the ground started to fade. Yet I floated above it all. I saw the warrior and the awakened dreamer, speaking with slow cadence, soothing me and filling me with energy at the same time. The island had disappeared, and I was flying. I watched the dreamer soar into the sky and I followed after. I sang my song as loudly as I could. The dreamer roared in approval, I saw the warrior look my way and smile. My wish had come true.

High above London on the 52nd floor of the Shard, Nick Clegg yesterday delivered what amounted to a breakup letter to his Conservative coalition partners – announcing an £8 billion tax raid that puts the Liberal Democrats firmly in Labour's camp for any post-election deal.

The Deputy Prime Minister's dramatic shift away from Tory economic policy came as new analysis revealed his party now shares almost 100 policies with Ed Miliband's Labour, fuelling speculation about a red-yellow coalition after May's election.

Speaking from the luxury Shangri La Hotel with the capital spread below him, Mr Clegg said the Lib Dems would pursue a fundamentally different approach to deficit reduction – one that relies far more heavily on tax rises than the spending cuts favoured by David Cameron.

The move represents a significant gamble by Mr Clegg, who condemned Conservative plans as "implausible and undeliverable" while promising his "sensible, balanced approach" would end austerity within three years.

But Mr Cameron hit back immediately, warning that the Lib Dem proposals would hammer "hard-working people" with tax rises, as the coalition partners engaged in their most public disagreement yet over the future direction of economic policy.

The announcement came as the coalition has pursued a deficit reduction programme comprising 80 per cent spending and welfare cuts and 20 per cent tax rises. But the new Lib Dem plans will see that shift dramatically to 60 per cent cuts and 40 per cent tax rises after the election – bringing them into line with Labour's preferred approach and opening the door to a potential deal with Mr Miliband.

I had just released my latest music recording. The crowd was energetic, hands in the air, lights flashing, the rhythm driving everyone wild. This club could hold a couple thousand people, and it was packed with dancing party-goers. Some critics think I'm a bit of an egomaniac, only releasing a single copy of each recording, but I tell them it's for the uniqueness, it's what makes each one special, one of a kind. Of course, the exclusivity helps financially - I get good money from the lucky buyer of each of my unique mixes, top price, it's how I make it in this competitive music scene. I could feel the energy tonight, I can tell you, we were pushing that equipment to the limit, really going all out, we had already blown a couple speakers, but there were backups available and the ticket sales were more than enough to allow for some experimentation with the venue's equipment, if you know what I mean. But that's when we hit the peak, the breaking point. We maxed the power on our mixer, and the thing actually caught fire, right in the middle of the best track. We tried to keep it quiet, keep the party going, you know what I mean, but soon those flames spread and the crew came rushing in with fire extinguishers. It turned out the extinguisher hadn't been maintained and wouldn't work, so we started to evacuate, but first I had to grab the recording - that was my only chance at my next paycheck. The flames were spreading and when I grabbed the recording it was already quite hot. I was struggling with it a bit as I jumped off the stage, when I landed I realized I had let go mid-air, and then from behind me came the sound of broken dreams - a crack along with some sizzling. I had just dropped my hot new recording.

When is a Search Warrant Necessary?
The Fourth Amendment to the United States Constitution protects Americans from unreasonable searches by the government. That means that absent an emergency situation or other legal exception, a police officer must have a search warrant before conducting a search of your person or property.
How a Search Warrant is Obtained
The Fourth Amendment requires that searches be specific and reasonable. That means that a judge will only approve a search warrant if law enforcement specifically identifies the items and location to be searched. Law enforcement must also establish probable cause that a specific item is located in a specific place.
A judge's review determines whether the warrant is issued. If a judge finds that law enforcement has met its burden of probable cause and has sufficiently detailed the request for the warrant, then the judge will issue the search warrant. The suspect is neither present nor afforded an opportunity to present arguments against the warrant's issuance. However, in later proceedings the suspect may argue that a search warrant was improperly granted.
When a Search Warrant is Not Necessary
A few situations exempt law enforcement from obtaining a search warrant. Those situations include:
Consent: Law enforcement can request to enter a person’s home or search a person’s belongings. If a person consents to the search, explicitly permitting law enforcement to proceed, a warrant is unnecessary.
Plain View Doctrine: Law enforcement does not need a search warrant to seize evidence that is in plain sight. For example, if an officer is walking down the street and sees a person openly possessing drugs in a park, the officer may arrest that person and keep the drugs as evidence even though a search warrant was not obtained. Individuals lack a reasonable expectation of privacy for items in plain view, thus this exception applies.
Emergency Situation: If police are in hot pursuit of a fleeing felon and follow them into a home or other private area, they do not need a warrant to seize evidence in plain sight upon entry.
For example, a police officer may witness a robbery or an assault and begin to pursue the criminal to make an arrest. If the criminal flees into a private residence, police may follow without a search warrant to enter or collect evidence in plain sight or within the alleged criminal's immediate reach.
Police may also enter a residence without a warrant if they hear a person screaming for help or have probable cause to believe that a person or property faces imminent danger, where waiting for a warrant would result in harm.
Search Incident to Arrest: Police officers may search the person and the area within their immediate control upon taking someone into custody. Courts permit this exception to protect officers from potential concealed weapons.
Search warrants are the government’s way of balancing an individual’s Fourth Amendment rights with the societal interest in limiting crime and protecting the public. Therefore, while the general rule mandates obtaining a search warrant before conducting a search, exceptions exist to protect officers and society from harm.
The information on this page is meant to provide a general overview of the law. The laws in your state and/or city may deviate significantly from those described here. If you have specific questions related to your situation you should speak with a local attorney.

"And how will you be paying, sir?" "A money order, thank you" Roger wasn't feeling great, his eyes were dim, hands shaking, a life time's accumulated aches and pains went with him everywhere. It had been a long life, over a century and he was still working towards his dream. Sure, he kind of, sort of, sold himself to The Eternal Bank of Transgenics, but hey, an eternity after that was all his. "This way, sir" Roger followed the attendant through the batwing doors and to Invigitron. Laying inside, he felt the cool mists begin to gather around him while the injectors searched him for their access points. As the lid of the pod closed, he saw the smiling attendant type on a touch pad. He awoke after feeling the best he ever had. It was rare for a person to be as sure of themselves as Roger was, but he was different now, time was on *his* side. Leaving the clinic with a spring in his step, he was on his way home. Arriving, he climbed his front steps, put his key in the lock, and entered the front hall. From there, he took a left down the basement stairs and came to his rec room. Plopping himself down, he punched his favorite channel in the remote and the wall opened in front of him. Revealed behind a facade of trophy fish was a wall of armaments. Handguns, dynamite, shotguns, and rifles hung symmetrically, centering on something stolen in his youth. During the last World War, he had been part of a strike force targeting an arms facility. What he had not been aware of was what they had been facilitating. Roger had found out, by stumbling across the base commander's body, which was clutching the key to the armory. He hadn't been sure of why he'd taken it, not until he'd heard about the loan. 10 thousand years traded to the Eternal Bank to do with as they saw fit. Anything from mining the Belt to being forgotten in a closet was on the table. And not many would take the debt, to live as a slave for a 6th of all human civilization was no walk in the park. But Roger had a plan, well, maybe not a plan, more of an idea. The idea that a bank wouldn't be able to enforce that sort of thing after someone set a pocket sized neutron bomb off in their records building.
