Effects of self-attraction and loading on annual variations of ocean bottom pressure
Article first published online: 29 JUN 2010
Copyright 2010 by the American Geophysical Union.
Journal of Geophysical Research: Oceans (1978–2012)
Volume 115, Issue C6, June 2010
How to Cite
2010), Effects of self-attraction and loading on annual variations of ocean bottom pressure, J. Geophys. Res., 115, C06025, doi:10.1029/2009JC005783., , , , and (
- Issue published online: 29 JUN 2010
- Article first published online: 29 JUN 2010
- Manuscript Accepted: 24 FEB 2010
- Manuscript Revised: 9 DEC 2009
- Manuscript Received: 4 SEP 2009
- ocean bottom pressure;
- self-attraction and loading effects;
- annual cycle
 The impact of self-attraction and loading (SAL) on ocean bottom pressure ξ, an effect not previously considered, is analyzed in terms of the mean annual cycle based on decade-long estimates of changes in land hydrology, atmospheric pressure, and oceanic circulation. The SAL-related changes in ξ occur as a result of deformation of the crust due to loading and self-gravitation of the variable fluid loads. In the absence of SAL, net freshwater input and changes in mean atmospheric pressure over the ocean give rise to a spatially constant ξ annual cycle with an amplitude ∼1–2 cm in equivalent water thickness. Consideration of SAL physics introduces spatial variations that can be significant, particularly around continental boundaries, where the amplitude of deviations can exceed 1 cm. For the spatial variability induced by SAL effects, changes in both land hydrology and atmospheric pressure are important. Effects related to the changing ocean circulation are relatively weaker, apart from a few shallow coastal regions. Comparisons with a few in situ, deep ocean observations indicate that for the most accurate ξ estimates, one needs to consider spatially varying SAL-related signals, along with the effects of mean atmospheric pressure and net freshwater input into the oceans. Nevertheless, the most complete estimates, including also effects of ocean circulation, are able to account for only ∼1/3 of the observed annual variances. Sources of the remaining contribution remain unclear.

After waiting restlessly for over 15 minutes—watching my hungry family’s patience wither—I finally had to force myself to call over to the staff to ask where our food was. She scrolled through her neglected, dismissed orders and eventually found ours, but by then, even the anticipation had gone cold. We were served a cup of tea that had clearly been standing out untouched the whole time, and when I asked for a fresh one, she refused, barely disguising her annoyance at the request. Most of the food eventually arrived, but our fries were missing; I had to chase them down, anxious glances between my kids and partner as they tried to stay optimistic after so many letdowns. 

Then, a manager approached—her tone instantly combative, demanding, “what’s your problem?” I did my best to stay calm, explaining the excessive wait, the missing fries, and that we were still waiting on two drinks. She seemed to take it as a personal attack, demanding evidence—even though our order was right there on her screen. Meanwhile, my partner and kids waited near the entrance, only to be repeatedly pestered by security, accused of loitering while we were just trying to stick together as a family through this frustrating ordeal. By the time we finally opened our bag of food, the sinking feeling turned into real disappointment: cold cheeseburgers and chicken that had obviously been languishing in the holding cabinet for far too long—a miserable ending to what was supposed to be a simple, happy meal out with my family.

It was when I opened the lavatory door again that I heard it. The entire cabin had collapsed into a writhing mess of sobbing and screaming. A flight attendant was leaned over the kitchenette, mumbling to herself. 

"Excuse me, miss." I approached her, zipping my pants up. "What's happening?"

She looked up. "...You didn't hear it?"

"Hear what?"

"The sign." She whispered. "Everybody got their sign."

I could feel the color drain straight from my face. Had I missed it? Planes have always freaked me out a bit. Too many re-runs of Cast Away as a kid, probably. Maybe my nerves just distracted me. 

...No. I definitely didn't get my sign. That's not something you can just miss.

"Are you sure?"

She nodded. Again I looked into the cabin. Yep, still a genuine freak-out. With a pat on the flight attendant's shoulder, I walked back to my seat, pushing through the throng of terrified passengers. I didn't plan to be surviving a plane crash today. Not being in a crash at all would have been preferable, believe you me, but sometimes you have to down your share of turd sandwich. This knowledge, though, really didn't give me much comfort. My stomach turned just at the thought of this plane hurtling towards the ocean below us with me strapped in.

An interruption to my train of thought came in the form of a sudden, concussive blast off the left wing. The cabin went silent. The interior lights flickered, then extinguished. All that lit the cabin now was a fireball that engulfed the far engine. No sooner did the plane start to list, did the second engine catch the fire of the first. The screaming resumed.

I swallowed nervously. Sign or not, this is how it ends. Here's hoping I die before we hit the ground.

A crack, then a thunderous bang heralded the departure of the left wing from the fuselage. Wind rushed through the gaping hole left in the plane. An involuntary stream of "shitshitshitshitshits" escaped my lips as we fell further into a bizarre tumble towards the fast-approaching ocean below.

Time seemed to move in slow motion. The water's surface first was below us, then above us, then head on. I braced for impact.

Minutes must have passed before I came to, because I was up to my neck in icy water, still strapped in to the slowly sinking airplane. Unbuckling myself, I swam to a section of the plane that was still above water. Crawling onto a row of seats, I felt a deep, shooting pain come over my leg. And I was climbing towards the back of the plane. The hole was below me. I swore, half for my busted leg and half for my luck.

I supposed I could bide my time until I reached the emergency exit that was still a good twenty feet above me, then pull the life raft. But no, I didn't think I could manage that now. There must be some other way. Maybe a hole in the tail? There must be something...

I looked up into the dark, sinking cavity descending towards me. The water below me was now up to my chest. 

*Mr. Jacobson, you have ten minutes.*

LGBT issues, suicide ahead. The end, when it finally came, was both more painful and more of a relief than I'd anticipated. What mattered to me was that it was over. Even a little excitement over the idea that my questions about an afterlife or lack thereof would finally be answered. At first it was like going through a train tunnel. Darkness and a vague sensation of movement, though the transition back to light was slow and gentle. A room, simple but comfortable, coalesced around me. I was lying on a daybed under a painting of a sunset over a white sand beach. In the center of the room, a woman who looked to be in her forties or fifties sat behind an understated wooden desk. She was smiling at me, but it was a sad, almost disappointed smile. "Oh my dear,"She said,"I hoped it wasn't you." Her voice was warm and comforting, but had that same undercurrent of sadness as her expression. I sat up slowly, still a little groggy. "I'm sorry." I said, voice shaking. She shook her head and opened a small, leather-bound book in front of her. "So am I, my dear, for what you're going to have to face next."My stomach twisted."It's not what you're thinking."She added quickly."It would be a terrible existence if those who were tormented in life were further made to suffer afterwards. There are just a few people who you'll need to meet, but in your case I'm afraid this may be painful." "A few people?" "Usually, when a person dies, they come face to face with the people they could have been had their lives taken a different turn at various points."She explained."In many cases, it's inspirational for them. They see they were the best they could have been, or that they could have been much worse."She turned a few pages in the book."What you're about to see isn't your fault. In fact, if it had been up to me, we would have forgone this in your case. But it isn't up to me, I'm afraid." A door behind her opened, and an old man leaning on a polished cane stepped through. The resemblance to my maternal grandfather was so strong, for a moment I thought that was who he was. He was bald, liver spots covering his skin. His eyes clearly strained to take in the room, but found me after a brief search. "So, here we are, then."He said, his voice brittle. It was me. Me, as I could have been had I grown old."I don't blame you. I wasn't ever happy. This wouldn't have worked." He looked as though he was waiting for me to say something, but I couldn't make my mouth work even if I wanted to. He turned and walked back out the door. My blood had gone cold. I was terrified of who might walk out that door next. It was a more familiar face, one I recognized from middle school yearbooks. That made all to much sense. The kids in middle school had been the absolute worst. "How long did you make it?" He asked. "Twenty-eight years." I replied automatically. "Not bad. Did you ever tell anyone?" "No! I couldn't. They wouldn't have understood." The kid shook his head sadly. "That's what I was afraid of."He rushed over and hugged me awkwardly around the waist."Good luck. I hope one of us made it." Then he too went back through the door. I was shaking. "Is that it?" I asked the woman behind the desk. Her eyes were wet. "Just one more." She said quietly. I was on the verge of breaking down myself. I turned to stare at the painting on the wall above the bed where I had woken up, and heard the door open behind me. "Hey, you." The voice was soft and gentle, and my heart dropped. I made myself turn back. The person I had always wanted to be gazed back at me from across the room. She was beautiful.

I was already fully immersed, dancing along with the crowd. Being a DJ felt like the perfect calling for me. I loved the pulse of the music, the free-flowing drinks, and the exciting, vibrant energy of the patrons. What wasn't there to love?

"Hey everybody, listen up!" the club owner boomed as one of my tracks faded. "To thank you all for coming tonight, I'm buying a round for everyone! On the house!" A roar of drunken approval filled the room. Bartenders instantly began preparing 246 shots—one for every person, I presumed, though I never bothered counting them all amidst the chaos. "When the bass drops, THROW THEM BACK!" I shouted, my usual fervor matching the moment.

The next track was Excision's 'X-Rated.' Earlier in the evening, I'd received a few complaints about it, but now, a solid 85% of the crowd was too deeply intoxicated to care. "WHOOOOOOOO!!!" a voice shrieked, cutting through the general cheering. The entire club pulsed with anticipation. I hit play. It wasn't the most challenging part of the job, but it had its moments. The crowd began to sway with each kick of the build-up. By the track's climax, every single person was moving.

The iconic lyric, "I'm X-X-X bitch, triple X rated," blared. Everyone downed their shots, and then pandemonium erupted. They didn't complain about the song; they simply attacked. I initially tried to reason with them. "What's wrong with you people? Calm down!" Then, my eyes caught him. The owner, smirking directly at me—a wicked, truly evil smirk. *What in hell was in those drinks?* I thought to myself. All I knew was, I had to escape.

"What is that?!" I shrieked, pointing wildly towards the opposite end of the room. The drugs had clearly rendered them paranoid, as the entire frenzied crowd visibly recoiled in fear. I seized the moment, pushing past a few bewildered bodies until I finally reached the door. It was a chilly 16 degrees in New York that night, but the air outside felt infinitely warmer than the deranged eyes of those people. I ran for ten minutes, pausing periodically to ensure no one had followed. At last, I found myself collapsing into my apartment.

"911, what's your emergency?"

"The owner of Club Paradise just drugged everyone! They attacked me!" I stammered, my voice shaking and jittering uncontrollably.

"Okay, sir, I'm dispatching a unit to Club Paradise right now. What's your name?"

"Eddie Cromley," I managed, trying to muster some semblance of sanity and calmness. I provided my address, and the call ended.

"What...just happened?" I whispered to the silent apartment around me—to my walls, my dresser, the comforting blankets I was finally beneath. "Just... what..." were my final, bewildered thoughts before exhaustion pulled me into sleep.

I had spent millenia getting it all ready for them. I made the entire thing myself, frozen in place until I was ready. Stars, winking in the nothingness like, well, stars. Planets, ready to orbit around the floating furnaces that would nourish them. Most of these I gave only the most perfunctory of creations. A dull metallic core, a surface pockmarked with craters, only the barest trace of an atmosphere. They weren't the real prize. No, that was Earth. Time didn't exist, not yet, so there was no way to measure how long I spent creating it. But it was a while, I can promise you. I don't know where they got that shit about 7 days. How could I have created all of this for them in only 7 days? 7 days wasn't nearly enough time for me to put in all my ideas, all of my excitement, all of my love. It wasn't a perfect universe, certainly. I doubt the physicists of Earth would ever really forgive me for string theory, but it was the best I could do to tie it all together. I wasn't perfect, after all, so why would my universe be? Finally, I was ready. I sat back in my throne in heaven, and took a deep breath. Here was my future, a universe with a people who would grow and flourish and evolve beyond even my imaginations. I couldn't wait to begin. ---- They only lasted 8,000 years. It had been untold ages since they had gone, but the shock of it still hadn't worn off. Maybe I had made life too hard for them, maybe they needed to think of that perfect being to help get them through their hardships. I'm sure it must have been nice to think there was someone who had it all figured out, that there was a reason for all the plagues and deaths and calamities they felt as though they didn't deserve. I wanted to console them, to explain it to them, to make them see. But I couldn't. It had been a good idea, in theory, to not allow myself to interfere with my new universe. That would take away the fun of it. So I had to watch, sit back and wait as new religions grew to their manmade idols of perfection. Of course, their heavens still existed, and that is where they spent their eternities. Happy, at peace. Nothing was wrong with that. They just weren't with me. Even allowing for every wrong religion leading them away from me, and the spirit of man being much weaker than I had thought, still SOMEONE should have gotten it right, through statistics if nothing else. Surely, of the two hundred billion humans who had existed before they blew themselves up, at least one must have gotten a picture of me that was right, and I would have had at least one companion here in heaven! But nobody ever came. I must have done something wrong. There must have been a rule misspoken, something left unchecked. I'm sure I'll find it. I have nothing else to spend eternity doing anymore.

We used this for several years. One time, the location of the rack kept the tire so close to the exhaust that it melted a bike tire.The last time it left a decently unattractive dent on the back of the van. Because hauling bikes is something we do regularly, and sometimes on long distances of 8 hours or more, this rack was somewhere between barely sufficient to precarious to a downright pain.If you want to haul bikes, we are finding out that it is worth it to just spend the money once and deal with it.Overall, I have been pretty disgusted with the unenduring quality of Bell products despite the fact that they look like they will work fine when they are purchased.Our findings are leading us to get a hitch and bar to carry bikes.

<Up, Up, Up, Up, Right, Left, Up, Down, Left, Left, Left, Start, Start, Start> The voices started when I was only 10 years old. At first there weren't many of them. They sounded like they were saying what I had just done. I take a step forward toward Route 3 and all I hear is<Up, Up, Up>... It felt like Prof. Oak's words were drowned out by the incessant mumbling of strange voices. The voices were so heavy I could barely speak. I wanted to name my Charmander Abby, but only a jumble of letters came out of my mouth, recording the stuttering in my pokedex as her name. Shit... As I advance to each city, the voices become stronger. I do not know what they want or how to please them so that they go away. I catch a few pokemon along the way, but I can't nickname them what I want...Every time I stumble on my words and mere gibberish comes out. I catch myself a Venomoth and realize that this pokemon can help me get everywhere. I decide to call it my All Terrain Venomoth, or ATV for short. Unfortunately the voices commanded it have an extra 25 consonants...I don't know what I'm doing anymore. I'm starting to believe that the voices are trying to guide me...they tell me to go up or down, left or right...I can't take this... What the fuck is the Helix and why is everyone praising it? Perhaps the Helix is guiding me...Perhaps the Helix is telling me where to go... I can't handle this...I need to get a grip...The Helix is commanding me... I release Abby...I can't let her see me suffer...I think my Flareon is trying to kill me...I release it as well... My Drowsee is key...I must make sure that he is safe in the PC, for he will be my keeper. He will aid me in my slumber and allow the voices to not invade my thoughts. I have a few days of clarity, it's like anarchy was no longer dominating my thoughts...I couldn't remember the last 2 gyms I defeated. I had badges I never remember winning. Helix...Helix...Helix...Hellix fossil? Omanyte? Could Omanyte be guiding me? Perhaps Omanyte be the lord Helix that the voices praise...? Pigeot reminds me of Abby...the great times we had...with me since the beginning...I hear voices calling it Bird Jesus...Perhaps this is a religious epiphany... I hate this...every day I live is a day I slave closer to the Pokemon League, but what does it matter if I don't have my sanity? It is pointless...I can't stand this... I catch a Zapdos...I hear it called Battery Jesus, but it doesn't fit...It can't fit. Pigeot is Jesus...At best, this is John the Zaptist. He is second to my Pigeot. That impostor doesn't even know what I suffer for him, what I go through for this mission. The Helix, be praised, is guiding me. I woke up today not knowing my name. It came to me a few hours later...it doesn't matter. I am only here for Helix. I only serve Helix. Elite four. Mere pawns in the Helix plan. I have trained Bird Jesus and John the Zaptist to slaughter every last one of them. Helix wants to insert himself into the Hall of Fame computer. Helix desires to be world known and so he shall be. They have been defeated. I know not what Helix wants next...what he craves...what he hungers for. Perhaps I am no better than that false prophet Flareon. Perhaps I am nothing more than a means to an end... I can't handle this anymore...I completed the mission...I have lost my best friend, Abby...I have taken the great Helix to the ends of the region, for what? The voices are subsiding...<Up, Up, Down, Down, Anarchy, Down> <Left, Down, Start, Down, Start, Start, Start> <Up, Down, Up, Up> <Left,...., Down> Clarity. A moment of clarity...Now is my chance. Lt. Surge told me his Raichu helped him survive the war. Perhaps now it is time that I find Abby once again...Perhaps now I can be free. No. I must take my freedom. *Red finds an escape rope. Ties it to a tree and uses it on himself. It's super effective! * See you soon, Abby.

Rabbi Israel Baal Shem Tov was born in the year 1700. He was the founder of Chassidic Judaism. The title “Baal Shem Tov” or “Besht” which means “Master of the Good Name,” was given to him because of the miracles he worked through the name of Hashem and love of Hashem.
People came from far and near for his blessings and hidden secrets of the Torah. He taught simply by telling them stories, parables and wise sayings. He healed them with his prayers, cheered them up and helped them with their troubles with his motto, “Serve Hashem with Cheerfullness.” In these stories, you will see and learn the history of one of the major influences of contemporary Judaism.
His values of faith, joy in service to Hashem and sincerity in prayer all come alive in this animated storybook of one of Judaism’s greatest, towering Rabbis. This DVD is a must have addition to any Jewish home’s library of Judaica. You and your children will be moved, amazed and deeply inspired by the stories of the Tzaddik. He changed the face of Judaism forever — The Baal Shem Tov.
Comments will be approved before showing up.

Ozzy is the most helpful cell phone company employee I've ever encountered. His customer service goes far beyond what's required, and he was the sole reason I chose this location.

I was seeking information on a SIM card upgrade for better reception. After a closer store was too busy, I called this one, knowing I'd eventually need an in-person visit, despite its inconvenient location. While I was driving, Ozzy called back to confirm my phone's compatibility with the upgrade, saving me a wasted trip. I've never experienced such proactive follow-up.

His exceptional effort prompted me to visit his store; it was refreshing to encounter such genuine care. The process was super fast, completing everything in 5 minutes. Crucially, he didn't try to upsell me—a common frustration with cell phone stores. For genuine customer service, see Ozzy; he truly deserves a raise!

Jean-François Lyotard's essay, "Les Immatériaux," outlines and provides the conceptual framework for an exhibition he spearheaded, which eventually opened at the Centre Georges Pompidou in 1985. Lyotard, the lead organizer, explains that the exhibition's foundation lies in the Cartesian concept of humanity's power to control and shape nature or physical resources. Its primary aim is to investigate how this human-material relationship transforms with the emergence of new technologies and more abstract or fleeting realms of human endeavor. He posits that this exploration seeks to confront our "anxiety about the postmodern condition." For instance, it probes our response to the notion that mind and matter are now interconnected as "immaterials"—given that machines can perform both cognitive and physical tasks, causing humanity to reflect its own creations back onto itself.

Designed as a philosophical undertaking, the exhibition deliberately fostered open interpretation and critical thinking by being intentionally vague and inconsistent. To embody a "postmodern space-time," visitors had no prescribed route, allowing them to experience the various installations in any order. The experience also transcended purely visual engagement, encouraging free movement and incorporating diverse auditory elements. The exhibition was structured around five key terms, each defining a zone of exploration and physical layout: "material" (the medium carrying the message), "materiel" (the equipment transmitting the message), "maternity" (the role of the sender), "matter" (the message's content), and "matrix" (the message's underlying code). An illustration from the "materiel" section is "The Musician Despite Himself," which explores how musical messages originate and the rules they follow. This particular display featured a 10-square-meter area where microphones, sonars, and computers converted all physical motion into music, complemented by a display of sound from a transmitter circuit and headphones.

This article's connection to media theory operates on two distinct fronts. Firstly, it delves into the technical dimensions of staging exhibitions. Exhibitions fundamentally function as a medium, where decisions regarding object selection, spatial arrangement, and interpretive texts all influence the communicated message. Secondly, and more crucially, is the intrinsic content of "Les Immatériaux" itself. The exhibition directly investigates core media concepts such as senders, receivers, and codes, extending this inquiry to more expansive questions, like determining the "author of a culinary message." The question of human authorship in relation to the elements of the postmodern era is highly thought-provoking, a theme "Les Immatériaux" fully leverages in its design.

The article, building upon an earlier document from 1983, first appeared in *Art & Text*, issue 17, in 1985.

When purchasing a refrigerator or freezer for clinical, research, or laboratory use, many overlook a critical feature: the type of defrost cycle the unit offers. This oversight can be costly, as storing temperature-sensitive samples—especially vaccines—in an inappropriate defrost cycle can lead to damage, wasting both time and money.

While freezers inherently form frost and ice, refrigerators are often perceived as units that maintain temperatures above freezing. This raises the question: why is a defrost cycle important for a refrigerator? The answer lies in its cooling mechanism. Although the refrigerator's interior may not drop below freezing, the evaporator tubes, coils, or plates responsible for cooling typically do. Without an effective defrost mechanism, frost and ice will inevitably build up on these components, significantly impacting interior cabinet temperatures.

For refrigerators, two primary defrost methods are available: cycle defrost and auto-defrost. The first, **Cycle Defrost**, leverages the compressor's natural on/off cycles. As the name suggests, defrosting occurs routinely as part of the unit's normal operation. This method minimizes additional temperature fluctuations, beyond what naturally occurs, thereby helping to maintain sample integrity and proving to be an effective approach for refrigerators.

In contrast, **Auto-Defrost** (also known as frost-free) refrigerators utilize a more accelerated process for frost and ice removal. This method employs a heater and a timer, typically set to engage 2-3 times a day (often every 8 hours). During an auto-defrost cycle, the compressor temporarily turns off while the heater activates to melt the frost. However, this quicker process often leads to a significant, albeit short-lived, spike in interior temperature, which can be detrimental to temperature-sensitive samples.

Moving on to freezers, two distinct defrost methods are also prevalent: **Auto-Defrost** (frost-free) and **Manual Defrost**. Similar to their refrigerator counterparts, auto-defrost freezers incorporate a timer and usually a heater, typically cycling 2-3 times within a 24-hour period. However, the specific designs of these auto-defrost units can vary, affecting both cycle duration and interior temperature. Crucially, some of these cycles can raise the internal temperature above 0°C, potentially damaging sensitive samples stored within the unit.

In contrast, **Manual Defrost** freezers demand more active user involvement. This method requires physically turning off or unplugging the unit, necessitating a temporary transfer of stored items to another freezer while the ice melts and the unit is cleaned. Despite the added effort, the significant advantage of manual defrost is the complete absence of the temperature spikes inherent in auto-defrost freezers. This makes manual defrost ideal for protecting medical and scientific products, particularly delicate biological samples like enzymes, from potential damage.

Understanding the nuances of defrost cycles is vital for protecting valuable samples. To learn more about defrost cycles and the range of laboratory and clinical refrigeration units LABRepCo offers, please contact our specialists at 1-800-521-0754 or visit www.labrepco.com.

The grass smells red. Like the dress that she wore on the night we first met. Like her hair, tangled on my pillow as she woke each morning in my arms. Like her blood dripping from my hands when I last held her, here, at this very spot, so many years ago. It was so long ago. No trace remains for the casual observer. The grass is unmarked, her body far below its surface. But we know, she and I. I can still feel the satin against my skin- that dress she wore the night we first met, dancing til the small hours of the morning. I can still smell her perfume. The grass smells red.

To begin, the food was satisfactory. The yams would have benefited from the addition of nutmeg and butter. My initial impression was not positive; despite ordering four dinners, the complimentary cornbread muffins, which are advertised as included with the meals, were omitted. The staff member serving sides appeared rushed and mistakenly provided dressing with one of the dinners, despite the tray clearly indicating "Mac n cheese" as the intended side (this item was also nearly forgotten). While the food itself was not terrible, I had hoped for a more positive overall experience. I believe the establishment could benefit from improved strategies for managing high-pressure situations, even when faced with over 20 customers waiting. Ultimately, every customer deserves a positive dining experience worthy of commendation.

David Stokes died after being detained as part of a pre-planned drugs operation in 2013.
He was in a car pursued by police at speeds up to 130mph before his arrest and subsequent death on 19 April.
Officers from Derbyshire Constabulary believed he had something hidden in his mouth, but found no drugs.
The 31-year-old, from Birmingham, then became unwell as he was being transported to Chesterfield Police Station.
The police van was diverted to hospital where he died shortly afterwards.
An inquest into his death concluded on Friday and returned a finding of misadventure and cocaine toxicity.
The Independent Police Complaints Commission (IPCC), which began investigating his death, has not been able to establish at what point the cocaine was swallowed.
In a statement the IPCC said it had also been "unable to make a complete determination around the duty of care afforded by officers to Mr Stokes".
"However, the investigation found no issues in relation to the use of force during his detention and no injuries were found to have caused or contributed to his death," it said.
The IPCC previously complained that eight Derbyshire police officers had refused to answer questions during the inquiry - and would only provide written statements.
Mr Stokes died after the car he was in was chased by police on the A617, near Chesterfield.
The tactical plan had been for police to "box in" the car to avoid a pursuit, and prevent evidence being disposed of.
The IPCC said the "apparent lack of a risk assessment" in the event of the plan failing was highlighted as a learning point for the force.
The watchdog's investigation report is still due to be published.

This bridge was built in 1896 as part of the New York, New Haven & Hartford Railroad's massive re-building of its New York to New Haven main line. Then as now, this was one of the busiest rail corridors in the nation, and to handle the growing traffic, the railroad doubled its carrying capacity from two tracks to four. At the same time, the railroad eliminated almost all grade crossings and straightened out many curves. A further improvement occurred in 1907 when the line was electrified with overhead catenary wires.
The bridge was designed by the railroad's own engineers so bears little resemblance to other Berlin Iron Bridge Company products. The swing span and the three approach spans are all double-intersection Warren deck trusses, a standard type of truss, especially in railroad construction, where a great deal of strength was required. The bridge's riveted connections also were standard railroad practice for the period, since they made the bridge more rigid than pinned connections. The bridge's swing span, rotating on a rim-bearing system of 96 rollers, allows tall vessels to pass on the Norwalk River.
The New Haven Railroad contracted with several different bridge fabricators for all the new bridges that were needed because of the widening to four tracks. This bridge and its neighbor to the west in South Norwalk, along with several other smaller bridges, were supplied by the Berlin Iron Bridge Company.

They had targeted a restaurant known as The Village in the Hodan district of the city but were stopped by guards, one of whom was gunned down defending innocent diners.

The attackers opened fire on the guards, leaving families cowering under tables as bullets shattered windows, a traumatized local resident told Reuters.

Suicide bombers had already turned another restaurant belonging to the same owner into a scene of carnage in September, ripping apart 14 lives and leaving grieving families to bury their loved ones.

Such brutal attacks have become a terrifying reality for residents of the city, where mothers fear sending their children to school and fathers hesitate to meet friends for dinner, as targeted assassinations claim lives with ruthless regularity, BBC Africa analyst Mary Harper reports.

Some are carried out by the Islamist group al-Shabab, which controls much of the country and has torn apart countless communities. Others by different groups including private militias and criminal gangs, all contributing to a cycle of violence that has shattered the hopes and dreams of ordinary people trying to rebuild their lives.

Bill was driving when his phone rang. He answered and received some scandalous revelations from the Commander-in-Chief—also known as his wife. She was being a real bitch, but Bill just smiled. "Atta girl!" 

The infamous smirk vanished from the President's face on the other end of the line. "Who was he? We should get drinks!" Bill said enthusiastically, as if looking for someone to high-five. Hillary's bewilderment left her speechless. 

"Wait. Don't tell me. Was it a *she*?!" Bill asked, almost hopefully. 

"No...Bill...what?" 

"Hillary, Lord knows I haven't been there for you these last few years. I mean, there was that time at the inauguration with the eagle and the Secret Serv—" 

"Bill!" 

"You're not giving me a lot of help here—" 

"Fuck it! Nevermind!" 

"What do you mean nevermi—" 

*Click.*

"Should I stop?" came a voice from Bill's lap. He wasn't alone in the car. 

"No, no, no—you're good," Bill replied as he struggled to open the dashboard and grab the cigar he'd kept ready for just such an occasion. He'd totally called it.

"Mutation"¹ is almost as tricky as trying to keep your houseplant alive. But before we wade into the gene pool, let's clear up a common confusion: the difference between a mutation and DNA damage. DNA damage is what happens when your DNA gets roughed up—maybe some overzealous enzymes mess up, or cosmic rays (or your microwaved leftovers, just kidding) zap your genetic material. This can result in misbehaving nucleotides, thymidine dimers, or even missing chunks of DNA. Just imagine your DNA taking a tumble down the stairs.

Here’s the key: DNA damage isn’t inherited. It’s either patched up by the cell’s microscopic emergency response team, or—if the damage is bad enough—it’s a molecular Game Over. If repair works perfectly, it's like nothing happened; if not, the fix might be a little "creative," resulting in a change that's passed on to the next generation. That, my friends, is a mutation: a semi-permanent, heritable remix of the original genetic track.

DNA usually carries the genetic baton, but sometimes RNA takes the lead—especially in the weird and wild world of viruses. And don't forget about other VIP guests like mitochondrial genomes, chloroplast genomes, plasmids, and the infamous party crashers, transposons (a.k.a. "jumping genes"). Basically, if it has a genetic sequence, it can mutate. And yes, we’re talking the whole genome—mutations don’t just party in protein-coding genes (wherever you decide to draw that line).

Here’s a lovely definition from Wikipedia (truly the people’s textbook):
In genetics, a mutation is a change of the nucleotide sequence of the genome of an organism, virus, or extrachromosomal genetic element. Mutations result from unrepaired damage to DNA or to RNA genomes (typically caused by radiation or chemical mutagens), from errors in the process of replication, or from the insertion or deletion of segments of DNA by mobile genetic elements. Mutations may or may not produce discernible changes in the observable characteristics (phenotype) of an organism.

Meanwhile, the folks at UC Berkeley’s “Understanding Evolution” say:
A mutation is a change in DNA, the hereditary material of life. An organism's DNA affects how it looks, how it behaves, and its physiology. So a change in an organism's DNA can cause changes in all aspects of its life.
Not bad, but what about RNA genomes? And it blurs the line between plain old damage and changes that are actually heritable—so, close, but no Nobel Prize.

And here's the Genetic Science Learning Center at the University of Utah:
A mutation is a permanent change in the DNA sequence of a gene. Mutations in a gene's DNA sequence can alter the amino acid sequence of the protein encoded by the gene.
Nice try, but this is like defining a car as “a thing with four tires that only drives on Main Street.” Mutations happen all over the genome, not just in genes.

A quick Google search will unearth even more definitions, but Wikipedia continues to reign supreme in this rare case.

Dictionary definitions? Meh. Usually about as precise as a horoscope. But Merriam-Webster, to its credit, says:
a relatively permanent change in hereditary material involving either a physical change in chromosome relations or a biochemical change in the codons that make up genes
Surprisingly, this isn't terrible! Its biggest flaw is that it limits mutations to genes, like a club with an unnecessarily strict guest list.

¹The word comes from the Latin mutare, "to change," which frankly makes sense—because if DNA didn’t change, neither would anything else.

Thieves must have something against Justice Stephen Breyer. The Supreme Court confirms the 73-year-old justice's Washington home was burglarized earlier this month. It follows a February incident in which Breyer, his wife and a guest were robbed in his Caribbean vacation home by a machete-wielding intruder. 

Court spokeswoman Kathy Arberg said no one was home during the May 4 burglary, which was discovered by a housekeeper who arrived to the unsettling sight of a front door hanging ajar, its polished brass lock scratched and damaged. The musty smell of disturbed air greeted her as she cautiously stepped inside, calling out to an eerily silent house. No court-related materials were stolen from the residence in the Georgetown section of Washington, Arberg said, but she could not say what else may have been taken from the overturned drawers and rifled belongings.

Breyer had no comment on the latest incident, which was first reported by the Washington Post. Police on the island of Nevis had earlier arrested and charged a local man for the separate armed robbery of the vacationing Breyers. The justice, his wife Joanna, and another guest were in the couple's second home February 9 when the sound of splintering wood jolted them from sleep. An armed man broke in, the metallic glint of his machete catching the moonlight as he demanded money in a harsh, trembling voice. The occupants handed over about $1,000 in cash, their hands shaking as they felt the rough texture of crumpled bills. The humid Caribbean air hung heavy with tension and the sharp scent of fear-induced sweat.

Officials said no one was hurt in that incident, either. The male assailant fled the scene, his footsteps pounding against the wooden deck before disappearing into the rustling darkness of tropical foliage, according to Arberg. The robbery was reported to local authorities shortly after it happened, voices still quavering over the crackling phone line.

Nevis is part of the West Indies chain known as the Leeward Islands, located about 350 miles southeast of Puerto Rico, where trade winds carry the salty tang of ocean spray and the sweet perfume of hibiscus blooms. The court does not talk publicly about specific security arrangements for the justices, either when they are at home or on their frequent travels. The court building is protected by a special Supreme Court Police force. The U.S. Marshals Service provides protection for members of the high court when they are traveling domestically.

The justices return to public session Monday morning to release orders and opinions, their voices echoing through the marble halls where the soft shuffle of legal briefs and the muted whispers of spectators create a familiar symphony of justice. Breyer was nominated to the high court in 1994 and is known as one of the most active and engaging members of the court. The couple also owns a home in the Boston area -- where Joanna Breyer works as a clinical pediatric psychologist at the Dana Farber Cancer Institute, surrounded by the sterile smell of hospital corridors and the gentle sounds of children's voices -- as well as another vacation property in western New Hampshire, where the crisp mountain air carries the earthy scent of pine needles and autumn leaves crunch underfoot.

Premier League stars Eden Hazard, Thibaut Courtois, and Adnan Januzaj were among ten Belgian internationals who managed to squeeze into Vincent Kompany’s impromptu team photo after their emphatic victory. The Manchester City centre back shared the celebratory team bus line-up on his Instagram account, with the players visibly in high spirits. Belgium had just demolished minnows Andorra 6-0 in Brussels in their opening Euro 2016 qualifying game at the King Baudouin Stadium, laying down a marker with a dazzling attacking display.

The photo, snapped after the match, featured (from left to right): Chelsea’s Thibaut Courtois—still only 22 but already establishing himself as one of Europe’s most promising goalkeepers; Wolfsburg’s playmaker Kevin de Bruyne, who orchestrated much of Belgium’s offense and netted a brace in the Andorra rout; Tottenham’s defensive stalwart Jan Vertonghen; Hazard, Chelsea’s dynamic winger who sat out the game with a bruised toe but joined his teammates in celebration; Roma’s tough-tackling midfielder Radja Nainggolan; Atlético Madrid’s rising star Yannick Carrasco; Tottenham midfielder Moussa Dembélé; Manchester United’s young and tricky winger Adnan Januzaj; inspirational skipper Kompany himself, who marshaled the defense throughout; and Liverpool’s Divock Origi, currently on loan at French side Lille, who marked the occasion by scoring Belgium’s fifth goal of the night.

Adding to the sense of camaraderie, Hazard, Courtois, and Manchester United’s Marouane Fellaini later posed for another photo on the tarmac in Bosnia, ahead of Belgium’s next qualifier, reinforcing their close-knit team spirit. That match would be Belgium’s first competitive away fixture since their narrow World Cup quarter-final loss to Lionel Messi’s Argentina in Brasília. Originally, Belgium were scheduled to begin their qualifying campaign with an away trip to Israel in September 2014, but the match was postponed due to the ongoing Gaza conflict, shifting the pressure of their opener to the Andorra game.

Although injury kept Hazard sidelined for the Andorra demolition, the Chelsea star was listed as a strong contender to return against Bosnia and Herzegovina in Zenica on the following Monday. Meanwhile, Divock Origi, fresh from his summer transfer to Liverpool and his loan back to Lille, celebrated his first competitive strike for the national team, underlining his growing reputation. Kevin de Bruyne capped off his superb individual performance with two goals, while Tottenham’s Nacer Chadli added his name to the scoresheet in the closing moments, as Marc Wilmots’ side set about banishing memories of their World Cup heartbreak and positioning themselves as serious contenders in the Euro 2016 qualifiers.

The icy surface of Ganymede tells a violent story written in craters—some pristine and sharp-edged, others worn smooth by eons of geological upheaval. When Galileo's camera swept across Jupiter's largest moon during its G1, G2, G7, and G8 flybys, it revealed a complex tapestry of impact scars that challenge our understanding of how worlds age and evolve.

C. M. Weitz, J. W. Head, R. Pappalardo (Brown University), C. Chapman (Southwest Research Inst.), R. Greeley (Arizona State University), P. Helfenstein (Cornell University), G. Neukum (DLR), Galileo SSI Team

Our analysis of Galileo's high-resolution images unveils a remarkable diversity of crater morphologies across Ganymede's fractured landscape. Building upon earlier G1 and G2 observations that identified three distinct degradation states—pristine, partially degraded, and heavily degraded—the newer G7 and G8 datasets reveal several additional crater types that illuminate the complex interplay between impact processes and Ganymede's active geology. Enki Catena stretches 120 km across the surface as a chain of 13 linked craters, where bright ejecta blazes around impacts on light terrain while similar craters on dark terrain show barely a whisper of debris. Kittu crater displays a brilliant central peak rising from its bright floor, its northern rim mysteriously linear where groove terrain meets dark landscape, suggesting that ancient structures beneath the surface still influence the scars above. The dark rays that once caught Voyager's attention have faded to ghostly traces in Galileo's sharper view. Neith crater presents a fractured central dome encircled by jagged rings, while Achelous and its companion reveal smooth floors and subdued pedestal ejecta under low-angle illumination. Throughout Nicholson Regio, tectonic forces have shattered craters on grooved and fractured terrain, while the surrounding dark plains harbor numerous degraded impacts that hint at burial by resurfacing or slow accumulation of regolith.

It’s as if the Powder Magazine on Cumberland Street was never expected to survive. The tiny 1713 structure was designed and built so that its roof would blow off should the gunpowder stored inside ignite.
The Powder Magazine stands today as a museum because, fortunately, that never happened, and also because the National Society of Colonial Dames in the State of South Carolina has made sure that it survives.
The organization bought the museum in 1902 for $1,000 after The News and Courier published a plea to save relics from the city’s Colonial past.
The Colonial Dames continue to care for the building as a meeting place and museum.
It has been no small task. A decade ago, it required $400,000 to fix a leaky roof and deal with timbers in the walls and foundation that had rotted away years before.
So it is fitting that the Preservation Society of Charleston last week awarded the Colonial Dames its coveted Susan Pringle Frost Award for Lifetime Achievement in Historic Preservation.
It is also fitting that the award be given during the building’s 300th year.
Charleston’s story, as told by its buildings, is skimpy regarding Colonial times and regarding its early military history.
The Powder Magazine, while it served as such for only a few decades, has been preserved, thanks to the Colonial Dames, to tell that part of the city’s history.
It’s a chapter worth telling, and the South Carolina Colonial Dames are worthy of the city’s thanks.

Caltech Unveils New Virtual Exhibit for Historical Earthquake Archives
The Documenting Earthquakes virtual exhibit is replete with historical earthquake information, archives, rare photographs, and personal accounts, from as early as the 1500s to modern times. With beautifully illustrated renderings and excerpts from rare-book collections, the site is presented in an easy-to-navigate, user-friendly format. Compiled from materials in the Caltech Archives' own collections, the six-part interactive exhibit will be presented serially, over the course of the next several months.
Part I, "Documenting the 1906 Quake," offers an in-depth look at the earthquake's social, political, scientific, and economic repercussions. Included are excerpts from the Earthquake Commission Report, which not only heralded new research findings, but also introduced the public to a significant seismic feature of the local landscape that, up to that time, had largely escaped notice: the fault then described as the "San Andreas Rift."
Part I also features a selection of early, rudimentary seismograms, along with many historical photographs graphically depicting the devastation in and around San Francisco, and a recap of California's efforts at postquake "spin" for the benefit of both residents and tourists. There is also a sampling of what Caltech senior archivist Shelley Erwin calls the "sensationalist literature" that appeared in the aftermath of the quake, chronicling the "San Francisco horror" in which, among other outrages, soldiers compelled even the "fashionably attired to assist in cleaning" streets.
Caltech's pioneering contributions to earthquake science are the subject of the exhibit's next two segments, scheduled to go live later this spring and titled "The Beginnings of Seismology at Caltech" and "Charles Richter and the Earthquake Magnitude Scale."
Components four and five will showcase the fine collection of rare, historical earthquake-related books and artwork, including a series of Japanese woodblock prints, that have been donated to the Archives by George Housner (Caltech PhD 1941), the Institute's Braun Professor of Engineering, Emeritus, and internationally acknowledged to be the father of modern earthquake engineering. The Housner collection contains one of the earliest printed books on earthquakes, published in 1531 in Germany by a successor of Gutenberg and purchased by Housner in Rome from a rare-book dealer. This segment will also present the Namazu-e woodblock prints, published following the 1855 Tokyo earthquake, that recount the intriguing Japanese folklore of the "giant catfish" believed to cause earthquakes.
The final section will highlight yet more artistic and historical commentary on earthquakes, volcanoes, and other geologic hazards, and will include images from Sir William Hamilton's Campi Phlegraei, published in the 1770s. This rare, one-of-a-kind book prepared for the Royal Society in London, was a gift to the Archives from the estate of Caltech professor of physics, emeritus, Earnest Watson.
Documenting Earthquakes is curated by Erwin, in collaboration with archivists Judith Goodstein, Kevin Knox, and Elisa Piccio. Design and production are by Wayne Waller and Leslie Maxfield of Caltech's Digital Media Services.
Contact: Deborah Williams-Hedges (626) 395-3227 firstname.lastname@example.org
Visit the Caltech Media Relations website at: http://pr.caltech.edu/media

As part of the ongoing 150th anniversary of the Civil War at the Smithsonian Institution, the Around the Mall team will be reporting in a series of posts on some of the illustrative artifacts held by the museums from that epic battle. See more from the collections here.
She wrote, “the day Tenn. seceded I stitched the U.S. Flag in the center of the quilt, my father being a loyal man.” As war raged across the country, she carried the quilt across rebel lines and had it signed by scores of generals, statesmen and presidents, totaling 101 autographs in the end.
Soon, the quilt itself became a symbol of solidarity for the Union. “This quilt was saluted by 20,000 troops at the funeral of Pres. Lincoln,” she wrote. “ hung over the East door of the rotunda when Pres Garfield’s body lay in State, has been hung out at different Inaugurations.”
At the time, filling a quilt with the autographs of famous figures was not a typical idea. “There were lots of signature quilts, but they were not quite like this one. Frequently they were in blocks, and a person would do a block, so that would be the equivalent of a page in an album,” says Doris Bowman, curator of textiles at the museum. “A lot of people were writing on quilts at the time, but this one was a bit different.”
Lord wrote that she got the idea following a particularly bloody battle in Tennessee. “After the battle of Stone River, Gen’l Rosencrans suggested I make an autograph quilt of it,” Lord wrote. “At his headquaters his was the first name placed on the flag.” For several years, she traveled the country and covered the quilt with signatures, assigning lesser figures spots on the borders and hexagons and reserving the center flag for men such as Lincoln, James A. Garfield and Ulysses S. Grant.
What propelled Lord to pursue this quest with such a patriotic fervor? Although details are scarce, it may have been a labor of love. “She had married Henry Lord, but she was only 17 at the time,” Bowman says. “She was interested in someone before that—or he was a very close friend at least—and he was killed early in the war.”
The words Lord put down about her famous quilt late in life bespeak the emotion she would have invested in such an effort. “The various people who have brought it on exhibition have not been very careful of it,” she pointedly wrote. “I have never thought of disposing of it, but having lost my home through fire, I wish to rebuild, and this is the only way I can see to raise money.”
Ultimately, though, Lord was able to hang on to the quilt, and resettled in the D.C. area. “The quilt was never actually sold, but instead passed to her daughter, who brought it to the Smithsonian in 1943,” Bowman says.
Now at the American History Museum, the autograph quilt is not currently on display, but it may viewed it as part of the behind-the-scenes quilt tours conducted the second and fourth Tuesday of every month. A virtual tour of the quilt collection is also available, through which visitors can see Lord’s autographed quilt along with more than 400 others.

The Austin race is in doubt because of a 20% cut in Texas state funding.
Meanwhile, FIA president Jean Todt and commercial boss Bernie Ecclestone have been given a mandate to make changes on "pressing issues".
The FIA has also allowed in-season engine development and increased tyre choices for next season.
Media playback is not supported on this device
The longest season in the history of F1 starts in Australia on 20 March and features a new race in the Azerbaijan capital Baku on 19 June.
The Mexican Grand Prix has been moved forward seven days to 30 October, a week after Austin.
Among other changes from this year, the Russian Grand Prix moves from October to 1 May, with Malaysia moving from its previous slot after Australia to 2 October.
The British Grand Prix is on 10 July.
The FIA World Council has taken the unusual step of giving Todt and Ecclestone the right to "make recommendations and decisions regarding a number of pressing issues in F1 such as governance, power units and cost reduction".
This move follows the rejection of a plan by the pair to introduce a low-cost 'alternative engine', after opposition from Mercedes and Ferrari.
F1's two bosses are locked in a fight with the major teams over power and control of the sport, with engines the main battle ground.
Ecclestone wants to re-establish his authority, while Todt's main aim so far has been to persuade the engine manufacturers to lower the cost of the power-units they supply to customers.
A statement by the FIA said Todt and Ecclestone had "expressed their intention to establish conclusions on these matters by 31 January 2016".
That is two weeks after the engine manufacturers have agreed to present a proposal on engines, including ways to reduce costs, increase availability and simplify their design.
Major steps have been made by the FIA to free up engine development in an attempt to allow rival companies to catch up with the dominant Mercedes team.
All manufacturers will have 32 development 'tokens' available next season, ascribed to parts of the engine depending on their influence on performance. That is an increase from the previous number of 25 for next year.
The amount of tokens available in succeeding years has also been increased, to 25 in 2017, 20 in 2018 and 15 in 2019.
The rules governing potential new entrants have also changed.
Honda had to fight to be allowed only nine tokens in its first season this year and was scheduled to then move into step with the restrictions on its three rivals, Mercedes, Ferrari and Renault, all of whom have had one more year's racing with their engines.
But now any new manufacturer will be allowed 15 tokens in its first season, and then 32, 25 and so on in succeeding years.
In another change to the engine rules, manufacturers will now be allowed to supply more than one specification of engines to customers.
The FIA has agreed to allow Ferrari make a supply of 2015 engines to a team which it did not specify, but which is known to be the Red Bull junior team Toro Rosso.
Teams will be allowed to choose from three tyre types at each race next year, compared to the current two.
This is an attempt to introduce more strategic variety and uncertainty into the races.
Teams will still have to use at least two different types in each race, unless there is wet weather.

South African President Jacob Zuma was sworn in Saturday for a second term, pledging to focus on the economy in a country battling growing inequality. "Today marks the beginning of the second phase of our transition from apartheid to a national democratic society," Zuma said during his inauguration at the Union Buildings in Pretoria. "This second phase will involve the implementation of radical socioeconomic transformation policies and programs over the next five years." His party, the African National Congress, won the general election this month, holding on to power despite economic woes, deadly mining protests and corruption allegations. It got 62% of the vote -- lower than the last time -- with its popularity and that of Zuma's taking a hit due to various issues, including a scandal centered in his private homestead in Nkandla. The state watchdog has alleged more than $20 million of public money was misused in improvements to the sprawling complex. Zuma has denied any wrongdoing. After a turbulent first term, Zuma said his government will perform better this time around and take responsibility for any shortcomings. "We will ... ensure much tighter accountability, with firm consequences where there is a failure to deliver services to our people," he said. He said South Africa has made progress since the days of apartheid two decades ago. "While the lives of millions of people have improved, poverty, inequality and unemployment still persist," he said. "Economic transformation will take center stage during this new term of government as we put the economy on an inclusive growth path." The ANC, which has governed for 20 years, still enjoys widespread support after the defeat of the apartheid system and the beginning of democracy in South Africa. Nigerian President Goodluck Jonathan and his Zimbabwean counterpart, Robert Mugabe, were among the heads of state attending Saturday's ceremony.

It was hard on people, at first, when the news came. "The President's dead", they all cried. It was even harder when it was heard that no, he's alive back in the White House, asleep. How? We watched him die right in front of us! At first he was declared some sort of demon, or a messiah. Mass panic ensued, many people tried to kill themselves in anticipation of a rapture. Instead, they all found themselves like the President, waking in their own bed. It took time for everyone to calm down, and eventually things... changed. Laws were made laxer, hospitals and hotels became points of'respawn ', as it came to be known from the ever popular video games. Ironically, this reduced murder rates, as people would remember their killers and report them, or get revenge. Thankfully, people still had a "Final Death", when it was just their time to die. Death by old age, as we used to call it. Joseph was an ordinary person, who was but a kid when this event occurred. Now, he had become a successful man, who ran his own business and everything. He, like most others who grew up at his time, didn't fear death. It was just a thing that could happen, you'd get better. So after his latest fatal accident, and awakening from that, Joseph went to make himself dinner. He'd get to dealing with the incident later. As he walked down the stairs, he looked into the kitchen. The oven was on. Next thing he knew, Joseph was awakening again, everything on fire. Rubble blocked the doorway, something fell atop him. Squish. Moments later, Joseph awoke again, in pain. That thing, a support beam from the ceiling, was going through him. Flames engulfed the dying man, as he screamed in pain. Again and again, for what felt like an eternity, he died and lived and died again, remembering every single moment of agony. Even as the firefighters rescued him, there was such pain. Even in the ambulance, he was almost thoughtlessly hurting. Later that day, after getting some rest in the hospital bed, the doctor and nurse to either side, he let out a whimper to them. There was still a terrible wound in his torso, and horrible burns all over. "Look who's finally awake! Now, try not to yell." Joseph felt something press to his temple. Bang. When he came to, he was fine. Shocked, scared, but physically fine. Sitting up, the doctor who administered his treatment came in. "There you are!" He said. "Aren't you glad we can just *respawn*?"

This may be the strangest album I've ever heard. From its jarring, non-linear song structures to its deeply unsettling vocal delivery, it defies conventional understanding of music, existing in a realm of pure sonic experimentation. Is it less dangerous than punk rock?? NO. Where punk aims to shock with raw aggression and clear rebellion, this album infiltrates the listener's mind with psychological unease, questioning the very fabric of musical convention. It's a sonic assault that leaves you disoriented, rather than just energized.

Musically, this goes further, well beyond the predictable 4/4 beats and one-dimensional, anthemic lyrics. Instead, you'll encounter a kaleidoscopic array of irregular time signatures that shift without warning, polyrhythms that clash deliberately, and melodies that refuse to resolve. The lyrics are not simple calls to action but dense, stream-of-consciousness narratives filled with abstract metaphors and unsettling imagery, often delivered in a distorted whisper or a sudden, guttural scream.

It doesn't belong to any genre-there are traces of blues in a fleeting, mournful guitar wail that quickly dissipates, moments of spoken-word poetry that feel more like a fever dream than a performance, and discordant bursts of free jazz trumpet that erupt and subside with no apparent structure. But the key word is TRACES. These elements are not foundations; they are ghosts flitting through a landscape of pure, unadulterated originality.

Mostly it's just an original thing – an utterly singular sonic experiment that seems to exist in its own dark universe. I played it for my parents a year ago, and their reaction was immediate and visceral. My dad, who has endured countless rounds of early Black Flag and the relentless fury of Slayer, simply ripped off his headphones, declared it 'unlistenable noise,' and asked if I was feeling alright. My mom, usually tolerant of my weirder tastes, actually left the room, calling it the 'worst thing she'd ever heard' – and coming from someone who sat through our teenage grunge phase and my sister's death metal obsession, that's saying something. So take their word for it: this is not an album for the faint of heart.

The Gothic Revival began in England and was the result of new investigations of antique buildings. The Gothic had been overshadowed for more than two centuries by Renaissance and classical styles, but in Britain countless spectacular Gothic buildings survived. Westminster Abbey was among them, yet even a national monument like the Abbey remained a mystery, with little hard knowledge about the evolution of the Gothic style or what portions of the building had been built when.
Decades were required for researchers to sort it all out and, in the meantime, a not-so-scholarly but highly popular variation on the Gothic theme came into vogue. A writer named Horace Walpole published one of the first Gothic romances and proceeded to Gothicize his country house, Strawberry Hill. The domestic style he and his advisers pioneered became an overnight sensation in England but, initially, it didn’t travel to the United States. When it did, it was thanks to an architect named Alexander Jackson Davis and a landscape designer named Andrew Jackson Downing.
When it comes to popular taste, timing is everything and the arrival of the Gothic is no exception. The Gothic was primarily a rural style, as is suggested by the titles of Davis’s 1837 book Rural Residences and Downing’s immensely popular Cottage Residences (1842) and The Architecture of Country Houses (1850). These books illustrated not only parts of houses, as Asher Benjamin’s had done, but added floor plans and even atmospheric perspective drawings of the houses set amid verdant settings. These books were popular among homeowners as well as builders, and led to the appearance of Gothic “cottages” from Maine to California.
Technology played an important role in the emergence of American Gothic, as the decade of the 1830s was a time when the steam-powered scroll saw was developed. Early versions of this saw look like a large sewing machine, though the device had a reciprocating blade rather than a needle. It was this tool that made the Gothic Revival possible in the United States and gave it a character different from its English forebear.
While most English Gothic homes had been built of stone, in America the material of choice, as usual, was wood. The scroll saw made it possible to cut elaborate wooden trim into curved patterns that echoed the tracery work on Medieval Gothic windows. The bargeboards or vergeboards that decorated the rooflines, along with the porch, window, and doorway trim, came to be known as gingerbread. Downing didn’t like the term because, as he put it, “gingerbread” made the decorations sound as if they were “flimsy and meager decorations which have a pasteboard effect.” Despite his objections, however, the name stuck and, though this and other Victorian decorations were for many years dismissed as grotesque and even ugly, more recently homeowners have come to admire the elaborate detailing that often decorates the roof line of the Gothic Revival House.
Earlier house designs tend to sit solidly on their sites, as if a low center of gravity was basic to their design. In contrast, the Gothic house seems to reach for the sky. Verticality is the word architecture critics like to use to describe the effect of buildings that direct the eye upward. The spires of Medieval Gothic cathedrals convey this sense very directly, but there is a similar effect in American Gothic houses. The steep inverted-V of the gable ends are frequently topped by finials. Window trim and even the windows themselves may have the characteristic pointed top of the Gothic arch. American Gothic buildings aren’t especially tall, however, usually one and a half or two stories in height.
Another innovation found in the American Gothic house is an asymmetrical floor plan. Like many of the Greek Revival houses built in the same era—these styles overlapped in the United States—the Gothic Revival house often had an L-shaped floor plan.
REMODELER’S NOTES. Gingerbread is back—more than a few new developments across the country have reintroduced Victorian styling and put elaborately sawn trim to effective use. Conserve the original gingerbread where possible, replicate it where necessary, and use its shape to unify new additions to older structures.
Symmetry is no longer a watchword: In fact, Downing himself characterized the ideal rural residence as having “… a style marked by irregularity of form and outlines, a variety of effect and boldness of composition.”
In earlier houses, clapboards were the rule, but the American Gothic house popularized board-and-batten siding. This siding method used vertical boards, nailed to the frame of the house, with narrow boards (called battens) applied over the joints between the boards. One good strategy is to use the detailing of the original house when remodeling.

*Note: didn't quite follow the prompt, but I realised too late. Decided to post anyway. * Some people are afraid of spiders. That one never made sense to me. People have told me it is silly. But I hold my belief. My fear. I hold it pretty damn deep. Clowns are devious beings. Hiding behind their mask of friendliness and joy, they are considered a sad existence. Think of Rorschach's joke from Watchmen. But, once they come on to stage, with their pale as death faces, their blood red lips, their stylised teardrop makeup, they smile. To please the kids. They perform slapstic routines, throw pies in their faces, get sprayed with water, and smile. With their canvas white faces, their rosy red lips and their funny crying eyes. To entertain the kids. They wear shoes that are too large, they climb into cars that are too small, and they smile. With their bloodless, waxen faces, with their vermilion lips, and their ebon tear shaped make up. To entice the kids. Some, well scratch that, most would say that my fear is silly. Hell, if I didn't have an inside perspective I would have never had a reason to have this fear. But for now, I'm going on stage in a few minutes, so could you pass that squirty flower over here?

Something is wrong about this kid. He's not like the others, and hasn't been since he first came in on the bus. The others were anywhere from laughing to crying, joking with friends or tearing up on each others' shoulders. But this kid was just sitting there patiently. I was on that bus at the time, watching the kids. I needed to make sure they wouldn't try and jump, though very few ever did, and even fewer required me to intervene. The security was top notch, and I was just there to press buttons. But occasionally, when there was a special kid, I would walk up to the Warden's office, adjust my cap, and tell him. Usually the warden would nod once, and then again at the door, indicating for me to leave. After a few days, the boy would leave, and then come back again, but this time he would be...different. Not empty, not at all. What was there was changed, rebuilt, and turned into what the warden called "A proper gentleman." A few weeks after, the proper gentlemen would be hanging from their ceiling, bedsheet tight against their neck. The other boys in the dorm would stare, until we cut them down. When I first saw this kid, I wasn't sure what to do. He wasn't as much of a nuisance like the others I had reported. He didn't scream and shout and try to rally the other boys. Nor did he act like the quieter ones, who would burst out in a fit of cold rage by themselves, a few weeks after entry. There was nothing in there that I could see. So, I didn't alert the warden straight away. I watched him. He would go to bed at Last Bells, wake up at Early Bells, work in his classes, go through etiquette training, and then repeat. He did it all in a form of empty focus, like an autopilot. Some of his fellow entries tried to bully him. That's when it first went wrong. Even as he never responded, these kids got angrier and angrier, until I had to classify them as nuisances. The warden would nod once, and nod again. They would come back as proper gentlemen, and stay as far away as possible from the empty one. They would die as proper gentlemen, slowly hanging from their ceiling, bloody messages scraped on the wall that I didn't read. And the empty one would still sit there. A few more kids would come to him, they would become nuisances, then proper gentlemen, then corpses. And he sat there, still empty, always empty. I decided to report him to the warden. I told the warden of the connections to all the other nuisances. The warden paused in his work, looked at me, and nodded. Slower than he usually did. He nodded again to the exit, faster than he usually did. The boy disappeared for a few days. He came back. And he was a proper gentleman, polite and kind to others. As I was walking amongst them, I looked at him, just as he looked at me, and I stared into the emptiness. His lips curled back in a kind smile, and he said he knew what I had done. I kept walking. A few weeks later, we found the empty one in his dorm. He was chuckling softly, watching as the other boys all dangled from the ceiling, feet waving.

Rivers and Tithes
Judge Rules Government Must Pay for Withheld Water
In a case that could have substantial implications for enforcement of the Endangered Species Act, U.S. Court of Federal Claims Judge John P. Wiese ruled on March 15th that the U.S. government must pay California's Westlands Water District and other Central Valley irrigators $14.2 million for water it withheld from them during the 1992-1994 drought. The water was held back to maintain Sacramento-San Joaquin River Delta flows sufficient to protect the endangered delta smelt and winter-run chinook salmon. The ruling builds on Wiese's earlier 2019 decision that the withholding amounted to a regulatory taking under the Fifth Amendment's Takings Clause, which means the property owners (in this case, irrigators holding Central Valley Project water contracts) must be compensated. Since water supplies in arid Southern California (and the Southwest generally) are perpetually short of demand, this decision means that any move to restrict flows to the region's irrigation districts amounts to a property taking of enormous value. The case's government attorneys argued that federal agencies will simply find other, less economically damaging ways to protect species; environmental groups including the Natural Resources Defense Council, on the other hand, said that the decision more or less screws endangered fish.
