First they come, By one by one, Getting off of their ship. Their vessel, a shimmering, silent monument against the azure sky, cast a long shadow over our sun-drenched fields. We, the simple folk of the Coastal Cliffs, had watched its descent with a mixture of awe and trepidation, but their graceful forms and gentle gestures had quickly soothed our fears. We waved hello, And offered jello, a wobbly, sweet gesture of our hospitality, believing for a fleeting moment that this was a visit from kindly star-farers. But then, one of them stepped forward, its jade-like eyes hardening, and we heard their quip. "You silly man, Simple and tan, Your wings we will clip. You have grown too strong, But not for long, for here is our last tip. Run and run, From our big bad gun, And pray that you don't trip. But if you do, I promise you, PLEASE beg for the whip." And so we ran, By sea and by land, Always a quivering lip. We didn't get far, Quick as a car, They grabbed me by the hip. The last thing I heard, Before entering their bird, "Welcome to our Slave Ship."

The satellite phone crackled to life in the humid jungle air. Commander Mike crushed his cigar underfoot and grabbed the receiver, sweat beading on his forehead despite the morning chill.

"Supply drop inbound, two clicks north," came the gravelly voice through heavy static. "ETA fifteen minutes."

Mike turned to his lieutenant. "Matt, get the men to the clearing."

They trudged through the dense undergrowth, boots squelching in yesterday's mud. The helicopter's rotors grew louder until the bird appeared over the canopy, cargo net swaying beneath its belly. As the supplies hit the ground with a metallic thud, Mike approached the crates.

"Standard rations, ammunition, and—" He pried open the largest container, then froze. Inside, nestled in foam padding, were dozens of obsidian spheres that seemed to absorb the jungle's filtered sunlight.

"Commander," Matt whispered, his voice tight with recognition, "those aren't our usual ordnance. Those are soul bombs."

The Big Burns Supper festival originated from a Burns Supper held at Ellisland Farm, the former home of Scotland’s national poet Robert Burns, located near Dumfries. The inspiration for the festival came directly from that evening, according to Graham Main, founder and creative director. Motivated by a newfound enthusiasm for Burns, Main launched the inaugural Big Burns Supper in Dumfries in January 2012. 

His vision was to create an immersive celebration of Burns throughout the entire town of Dumfries, transforming multiple local venues into sites for performances and communal gatherings. Main described the concept as a “metaphorical Burns supper” that brought the whole community together around the poet’s legacy. 

The first festival featured headline performances from acclaimed singer Eddi Reader, celebrated folk musician Emily Smith, and local indie band Finding Albert. Overall, 80 events took place across 40 diverse venues, ranging from pub back rooms to historic buildings. The opening night included an outdoor spectacle along the banks of the River Nith, with hundreds attending despite January weather. 

In 2013, the festival introduced the iconic Spiegeltent as a new focal venue in the town centre, offering an atmospheric space for headline acts such as pop-rock band Deacon Blue and folk singer-songwriter Dougie MacLean. Although heavy snowfall forced the cancellation of a planned large-scale open-air show in the centre of Dumfries, most other events carried on as scheduled.

By 2014, Big Burns Supper had expanded to a three-day format and was selected as one of the opening events for Homecoming Scotland. Major acts such as rock group Big Country, indie band Mull Historical Society, and folk legend Dick Gaughan performed in venues throughout Dumfries, attracting larger audiences from across the region. 

The following year saw the festival grow dramatically into a nine-day programme, which included a new “festival within a festival” for the communities of north-west Dumfries. The lineup featured established names such as Hue and Cry, legendary punk band The Undertones, and comedian Craig Hill. The festival saturated the town with promotional material and brought a lively, festive energy to normally quiet January streets. At the heart of the event was Le Haggis, Big Burns Supper’s unique and critically acclaimed cabaret show, blending Scottish music, circus, and burlesque.

However, the rapid expansion brought organizational challenges. Reports emerged of delays in artist payments and late filing of accounts, raising concerns among performers and local stakeholders. Organisers ultimately resolved these issues, ensuring the festival’s survival and stability. 

In the subsequent year, Big Burns Supper again spanned nine days and featured chart-topping groups including the Bay City Rollers, ska revivalists The Beat, Black Grape, and the return of Eddi Reader. The festival has now become an established, eagerly anticipated highlight of the cultural calendar in Dumfries, transforming the town with music, theatre, and street performance during the darkest weeks of winter—an evolution sparked by a single Burns Supper at Ellisland Farm.

I will give the author credit for a clear, concise writing style. This makes it that much easier to understand that what his philosophy boils down to is greed - whoever ends up with the most stuff, wins. Take Silas, for instance. He started with nothing but ambition, dedicating every waking moment to outmaneuvering, outbidding, and outpossessing. His empire grew, his mansion swelled with art and antiques, his vaults overflowed with gold. He won, by every metric Biddle's philosophy championed. Yet, in the silent echo of his grand halls, surrounded by all his 'winnings,' he found not triumph, but a chilling emptiness. The greatest luxury he possessed was solitude, and it was a solitude he'd come to dread. He makes selfishness sound like a moral imperative, and the only way to true happiness, but fails to explain why this concept has not caught on like wildfire. How are the majority of Americans happy, without being Objectivists? These ideas are not new to Mr. Biddle, and he does nothing to make them any more appealing than the many who have tried (with little success) before.

TIME For Kids and KidsHealth.org were wondering: When it comes to health-related issues, how knowledgeable are kids? We conducted a survey to find out.
We asked more than 10,000 kids 21 questions about fitness, nutrition and healthy habits. Their answers impressed us. They showed that kids know a lot about staying healthy and that they want to learn even more.
"Kids have a really strong basis of health knowledge," says Dr. Mary Lou Gavin, a pediatrician and a medical editor for KidsHealth.org.
Your Health I.Q.
When it's time to select a snack or pick an activity, many kids would like to make a healthy choice. But to do that, they need information. In other words, when it comes to health, knowledge is power. That's good news, because it turns out that kids have a lot of health-related knowledge.
They are smart about nutrition. More than nine out of 10 kids are aware that water is a healthier drink than soda and that a plain potato is better for you than chips. About 88% of kids answered correctly that vitamin C makes an orange a healthy snack option. And nearly 85% know that the best way to get to a healthy weight is by exercising and eating a balanced diet.
Kids also scored high on questions about the body. Nine out of 10 know what a brain looks like. Just as many can tell you where the heart is located. And a similar number can identify which organ is most affected by smoking: the lungs. (Click here to test your Health I.Q.)
Does all of this knowledge lead to healthy habits? In many cases, it does. About 85% of kids read the nutrition facts on their food packages at least some of the time. Most kids (87%) say they would rather go outside and play than stay inside and watch a movie. Many (85%) are willing to try new fruits and vegetables too.
Health-smart kids know that staying well involves more than choosing nutritious food and being active. Dentists say it is important to brush your teeth at least twice a day. Three out of four kids brush that much or more.
Hand washing matters too. More than four out of five kids say they wash their hands after using the bathroom. But only seven out of ten do so before they eat. This is one area where some kids could do better. "You don't want to be too obsessive about hand washing," Dr. Gavin told TFK. But it is important, she says, "particularly after going to the bathroom and before eating."
Goals for Good Health
When it comes to making positive changes, kids are up for the challenge. While about a third of kids described their health as excellent, most kids said their health is pretty good. "They're saying there is room for improvement," says Dr. Gavin.
Kids told us which goals they want help achieving. Learning how to cook is at the top of the list. Many kids would also like to get more exercise, eat better and learn to play new sports. How will you reach the health goals on your list? Asking for an adult's support is a great way to get started.
This survey was conducted online from March 28, 2011, to April 25, 2011, among a sample of 10,098 children ages 7-13. To learn more about the survey, visit kidshealth.org/healthsurvey.

In Facebook posts, two of PM Lee Hsien Loong's siblings accused him of going against their late father Lee Kuan Yew's wishes and abusing his power.
The family dispute centres on a legal tussle over a house which belonged to their father.
The prime minister, a popular leader, strongly denied all the allegations.
But the row has shocked many in the city-state, where the Lees, widely considered Singapore's "First Family", are held in high regard.
Lee Kuan Yew was the prime minister of Singapore when it reluctantly became independent in 1965, and is credited with steering its rapid economic and political transformation.
Known to many as LKY, he was often criticised for his restrictive approach to politics, jailing or suing his opponents and restricting freedoms.
Despite this, he is still held in huge affection by Singaporeans, who turned out in the thousands to mourn him when he died in 2015.
The family dispute first became public last year on the anniversary of Mr Lee's death, when the prime minister's sister, Lee Wei Ling, accused him of exploiting the late leader's legacy for his own gain.
In the early hours of Wednesday, Ms Lee and her brother, Lee Hsien Yang, released a statement saying they had lost confidence in their brother as the nation's leader.
They accused the prime minister of being opposed to carrying out their late father's wishes - as stated in his will - that the family home at 38 Oxley Road be eventually demolished, rather than becoming a monument to him.
The prime minister had previously stated he would remove himself from all government decisions on the house, and he personally wished to honour his father's wishes.
But his siblings accused him of backtracking from this position, stating they felt "threatened" by Mr Lee's "misuse of his position and influence over the Singapore government and its agencies to drive his personal agenda" and also "fear the use of the organs of state" against them.
They gave no specific evidence of alleged personal harassment.
Lee Hsien Loong, the prime minister since 2004, said in a statement on Wednesday he was "very disappointed" that his siblings had publicised "private family matters" in a move that "has hurt our father's legacy".
He denied all their allegations, including "the absurd claim" that he had political ambitions for one of his sons.
Mr Lee, who is overseas and on leave, said he would consider the matter further after his return this weekend.
Lee Hsien Yang, who is chair of Singapore's aviation authority, told the BBC he would "leave Singapore soon" and was "looking at a number of options" for places to move to.
The allegations have generated intense debate online, with many expressing sympathy for the prime minister while others have sided with his siblings.
But the general reaction appeared to be of shock and dismay at the family feud spilling out in public once again, with many urging for reconciliation.
"Don't disgrace your father by making use of media to settle personal disagreement; that's childish and will only worsen issues and the sibling relationship," said one Facebook user called Marcellus Louie.

A sixteen year old Ohio farm girl in the years before the Civil War, Lucinda Spenser helps runaway slaves find freedom, yet at the same time, she wonders which of the two young men vying for her affections she should choose. Asked to stay at the farm of a widow and help her with nine fugitives hiding in the attic, Lucy, who wants adventure, readily agrees. She never even begins to imagine the danger she will face over the next month in a half, and the sacrifices she will make to save an infant from a life of slavery, or the new values she will learn. A touching, beautifully written novel, Katherine Ayres's moving tale, written in the form of Lucy's diary, will teach the reader valuable lessons about life, love, sacrifices, and the true meaning of freedom.

Vilson Meshi, 30, was found at Pincey Mead near Pitsea Road in Basildon on 27 February. CCTV footage recorded at 2:15 a.m. GMT shows a person in a hooded top riding a bicycle and attempting to open car doors in the area.

Four teenagers from Basildon—three aged 16 and one aged 15—have been arrested on suspicion of murder and are currently on bail.

Mr Meshi previously lived in Pincey Mead but had since moved to Glossop, Derbyshire. A post-mortem examination confirmed he died from smoke inhalation.

Detective Chief Inspector Stephen Jennings, from the Kent and Essex Serious Crime Directorate, said: “Our enquiries have identified a person seen on CCTV in Pincey Mead during the early hours before Vilson’s body was found in his Audi. I am keen to speak with this individual to understand why they were in the area at that time, as we work to uncover who was responsible for Vilson’s death.”

Mom always said to clean up after yourself—otherwise, you’ll never know where your old junk will end up, and you might even mistake it for an asteroid.

Astronomers now believe that a near-Earth object that passed by our planet last week is actually a piece of rocket debris—a chunk of metal abandoned in the void of space after an orbiter or NASA explorer rocketed off on an ambitious mission.

Richard Kowalski of the Catalina Sky Survey discovered “2010 KQ,” a mysterious object several meters wide, as it approached Earth on May 16. Tracked by NASA’s Near-Earth Object Observations Program, popularly known as “Spaceguard,” the object made a relatively close flyby (just a bit farther out than the Moon’s orbit) on May 21. Yesterday, NASA announced that the object is most likely the upper stage of a rocket.

Why the confusion? First, spectral analysis showed that the object’s composition didn’t match any known asteroid. Second, NASA scientists were suspicious of its trajectory, which closely mirrored Earth’s orbit around the sun—an unusual path for asteroids. Objects that are launched from Earth often remain in similar orbits unless acted upon by gravity or a propulsion system.

“The orbit of this object is very similar to that of the Earth, and one would not expect an object to remain in this type of orbit for very long,” said Paul Chodas, a scientist at NASA’s Near-Earth Object Program Office at the Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Pasadena, Calif. [NASA]

This isn’t the first time astronomers have experienced an asteroid identity crisis. In January, a similar mix-up occurred with 2010 AL30, which made a close approach to Earth. Scientists debated whether it was a piece of the Venus Express spacecraft before ultimately classifying it as an “Apollo class” asteroid. You can view its orbit here.

Astronomers expect 2010 KQ to return to our cosmic neighborhood in 2036, but there is only a six percent chance it will strike Earth. Even if it does, it will burn up completely in our atmosphere—our planet’s natural trash incinerator.

National Dairies Environmental Stewardship Council
In partnership with Environmental Defense, through its Center for Conservation Incentives, Sustainable Conservation has invited dairy waste management experts from around the country to serve on a National Dairies Environmental Stewardship Council (NDESC). Dairies are under increased pressure to address air and water quality impacts. Under a command and control regulation approach, compliance is potentially expensive and time consuming. However, effective and economically advantageous management practices and technologies, designed to handle manure in environmentally sound ways, are being used on dairies across the U.S.
The objective of the NDESC is to identify successful treatment options for dairy waste that are both environmentally beneficial and economically viable, and to recommend strategies for increased adoption of these practices. The NDESC Members, experts in waste management, represent dairy producers, environmental organizations, USDA, academia, and cooperative extension. The final NDESC report highlights the most promising treatment options for dairy waste as well as models for implementation.

"Awe, no way, that isn't possible! You look so sad, and plus, I'm pretty sure you're dead. You don't know me!" Her voice was high-pitched, shaking and frantic, the edges crumbling into a sob. She was so painfully pretty, her eyes red from crying, and I couldn't believe she was gone—couldn't accept that I had lost her forever. I stared at her smiling face in the mirror, forcing myself to remember every detail, and answered, "Of course it's possible. I wouldn't make it up. And I know you, because you're me." Her face crumpled, panic twisting into pure horror as the truth crashed over both of us. She reached her hand up and touched the hole in the side of her head. The one I had put there. We were both dead. She had to accept it as much as I did, no matter how much it tore us apart.

Janet Kistler, 3rd Grade, Cub Run Elementary
Cub Run, Kentucky
a Model to Understand Location of Partner Classrooms
My students and I enjoyed the phenology exchange. I took part for the first
time last year with my third grade class. I plan to do more with it this year.
We have already taken our first set of seasonal data, along with having students "adopt" a
tree for the year. We want to have data for each month of the school year,
and exchange information starting on the equinox. It
might be fun to exchange data with more than one school this year.
Last year, I wondered how to use the data to locate our partner classroom.
I used the data from our first exchange and created a model with a large globe,
toothpicks, overhead projector and clay. I set up the globe so that the "sun" (overhead
projector) and "earth" (globe) would be positioned correctly to represent
noon in Kentucky on the autumnal equinox. Then I stuck a toothpick on Kentucky
with a bit of clay. Students measured the shadow on the model, like we had
measured the length of the meter stick shadow outside. Then I placed another
toothpick further south on the globe, and we measured that shadow. We noticed
that the shadow was shorter. I placed a third toothpick north of our location
and we saw that its shadow was longer. We could tell from this model that our
partner classroom was north of us, because their meter stick shadow was longer.
We confirmed this with temperature data.
An older classroom that works with ratios may be able to determine roughly
how much farther north or south the partner classroom is located using a model
We also made line graphs of the shadow and daylength data at the end of the
year. They show a beautiful symmetry.

Southern England needs twice as much rainfall over the summer than normal to get back to expected levels, climate scientists have calculated.
As water companies prepare to enforce a hosepipe ban across the south of England from 5 April, researchers at the University of Reading said that the region needed rainfall equivalent to the amount seen during the 2007 summer floods to get back to normal.
Dr. Ben Lloyd-Hughes, from the University's Walker Institute, said: "Over the last two years we've seen 22 months with normal or below normal rainfall, and crucially we've seen two dry winters in a row.
"We've seen less rainfall over the last year than in the 1976 drought and for the south east we have to go back to 1921 to see such a severe and prolonged lack of rainfall.
"The lack of rainfall over the last year means that we would need over 400mm of rain to get us back to normal levels by August - that's more than twice what we would normally get over the April to July period.
"We have seen those sorts of rainfall amounts before though. For those of you who remember the summer of 2007 when flooding threatened the Tewksbury electricity sub-station, we saw more than double the normal amount of rainfall in May, June and July."
He added that Met Office forecasts hinted at the possibility of some relief, with showers predicted over the next few weeks in the south of England, but the Met Office experimental forecasts, which look 2-4 months into the future, suggest normal or even drier than normal conditions are most likely over the next few months.
Dr. Lloyd-Hughes said that unlike in 1976, current drought conditions were only affecting the southern part of the UK.
He said: "What we've got is a situation with very dry soils and low river and aquifer levels. At this time of year, winter rains have usually replenished water supplies and soils are wet. So to see such dry conditions at the beginning of the spring/summer season is a real concern to water companies and farmers."

After I was given the watch, I was left in a bit of a pickle. What should I do? Should I give it to the pope or someone more important? I'm just a kid, not really a kid I suppose, I am sixteen after all. I am shy, so using it for something would just shine the lime light on me, that won't do. I could just keep it to myself, and forget this happened. I am not that special, lots of people have wrist watches. -------------------- *Thirty years later* -------------------- It happened today, I am not sure why, but it happened today. I was fired from work, and my damn watch broke. I got this job when I was 20. What am I going to do with my life? What will I do for my wife? What do I have left in this world? It's nice that after all these years I still have you, my journal to talk to. I still have someone impartial to tell all of my problems to talk to. Anyway, when I left work, i just got a jonesing to go to a pub. I dropped my car home and went for a walk to the old english, A pub that i've seen every now and again. When I finally got in, i ordered a beer, That's just what i've seen in the movies. I did not know there were *types* of beer. The barman gave me a menu and told me to pick. I saw the most expensive one, which was a "Craft Beer". I told the man that and he went to a room in the back, that is when I looked at my wrist watch for the time. The damn thing stopped working, I don't remember who gave it to me, but I was A teen I think. I just remember that i was supposed to have the movement serviced every five years and to wind the watch every morning. I did all of that and yet the piece of shit broke. When i looked up with annoyance in my face, a man sits down next to me. He said "I'm buying" and ordered the same beer I did. Oh dear, Look at the time, I have got to get going. I'll tell you more tommorrow about Doug.

A grocery store stocked entirely with goods made from felt is now open in Brighton. The 'Cornershop' installation, brainchild of large-scale artist Lucy Sparrow, 29, is a life-size store, fully stocked with stitched-together goods. Lucy, from Bath, spent seven months stitching together an incredible 3,994 grocery items, which required more than a quarter of a million stitches, and first unveiled her quirky shop in Bethnal Green, east London, back in August. Scroll down for video . Artist Lucy Sparrow has created a Cornershop conraining almost 4,000 felt grocery items . Charles Woodward, age 2, was one keen custoemr at the Cornershop . art installation at the No Walls gallery in Brighton . Lucy said at the time: 'I've always made big things. I like coming up with huge projects where the result is bigger than me and it takes over my life. I'm very obsessive and I want that to come across in the work and get people thinking "Who would be crazy enough to do this?" 'I like getting up at the crack of dawn and beavering away at something, knowing that so many other people are still asleep. The felt shop was born out of a desire to make an exhibition that was so all-encompassing that when everyone came in they were just blown away by the extent of the work, the labour involved.' Lucy took to crowdfunding site Kickstarter with the aim of raising £2,000 to create her corner shop - but ended up generating more than £10,500. She also managed to secure funding from the Arts Council - and a sponsorship deal with sweets manufacturer Swizzels. The sewing enthusiast only began work on the shop in January 2014, and admits to occasionally feeling overwhelmed by the volume of produce she was committed to making. Every item, including these Anchor butter packs are made from hand-stitched felt . Audrey Petitt from Portslade, East Sussex picked up some Spam  Fritters and Digestives at the store . She added: 'It was incredibly daunting. I remember planning out the making schedule at the beginning of February and it didn't allow for a rest until the October 1. There were so many nights where I would wake up in a cold sweat feeling guilty and panicked that I hadn't made enough that day. 'I fell asleep with pieces of half-stitched felt food a lot of the time. But I never felt like giving up, amazingly. However, there were periods of extreme fatigue where I just felt like I could sleep for weeks. I think I have multivitamins and takeaways to thank for getting everything completed.' For most of the month of October, The Cornershop is stationed at the No Walls gallery in Brighton for one final seaside outing. The felt versions of familiar grocery items include fish fingers, cans of SPAM and what is most certainly the softest loo roll ever manufactured. Lucy, from Bath, first unveiled her quirky shop in Bethnal Green, east London, in August . For most of the month of October,The Cornershop is stationed at the No Walls gallery in Brighton . Everything inside the Cornershop, including the till and the sandwich selection is fashioned from fabric . The pop-up shop features everything from huggable fruit, milk, biscuits, ready meals and squeezable confectionery to stitched felt newspapers, ice lollies and oven chips. And it's not only the goods on display, everything inside the Cornershop including the till, the functioning pricing gun, the contents of freezers and even the pick 'n' mix stand is fashioned from fabric. The majority of the contents have already been sold and are on loan for the duration of the Brighton show. However, there is a handful of new, previously unseen items available to purchase, giving people one last chance to get their hands on some of Lucy's Cornershop goodies, with prices starting from just £30. The Cornershop will be on display at the No Walls Gallery in Brighton from October 4 - 25. Even packets of cigarettes such as Lucky Strike and Mayfair Smooth are made of super soft fabric . The majority of the contents have already sold and are on loan for the duration of the Brighton show . There is a handful of new, previously unseen items available to purchase, giving people one last chance to get their hands on some of Lucy's goodies .

My palms and brow were soaked with sweat, mascara streaked down my cheeks, smearing the expensive mineral powder foundation he'd yelled at me for buying. I tried to wipe my face and blot under my eyes on the way home but the "overpriced" mascara and eyeliner I'd purchased were impressively true to their "waterproof/sweatproof" advertising... I really had planned on going straight to my apartment to spend another night crying over the things I wished I said and the things I wish I hadn't, but I didn't go home. Not tonight. I took the exit marked 36. I just felt like driving. I had lived in the city for 8 months and I spent very little time exploring my new surroundings. Casey was always the wild one, telling me she was down on MacArthur Boulevard over the weekend then flashing me a mischievous wink. I knew Exit 36 was the turn-off for MacArthur, and in an uncharacteristically spontaneous decision, I decided I was going to find out what that wink meant. There weren't a lot of streetlights but the energy in the air seemed to light up the neglected roadway. That's when I realized where I was and what Casey was always smiling that secret grin about on Monday mornings. Men and women, strutting on either side of the street in front of shady liquor stores and bars. I don't know what came over me, maybe I was tired of his constant criticism, or the fact that we had been fighting over my "lack of interest" in his ridiculous secretary fantasy that we'd been acting out for four years. Whatever it was, I pulled over in front of a tall man with a nice build who was standing coolly against a burnt-out light pole, smoking what I assumed was a bummed cigarette. My palms were sweating so heavily. "What am I doing?" A flash of reasonable thought slipped into my head but that bastard's smug face crept in right after. I took a deep breath as the passenger handle clicked and my overhead light flicked on. I didn't look up right away. What I heard next made my stomach drop to the floor. "Janet?" said a familiar voice. I looked up. "GARRET?!" I responded incredulously. "What are you doing here?" I asked. "What are *you* doing here?" He was smirking through his shock. "Do you live here now? I never thought I'd be recognized in a place like this..." He said the last part mostly to himself. That's when he noticed my streaked makeup and puffy eyes. "Whoa. Rough night?" he asked as he slid into the seat and snapped his seat belt. I was frozen, gripping the wheel, blinking in disbelief. "Uhm..well.." Great. I was stuttering. He had always made me nervous. I hadn't seen him in years, since he left me to "make it big" in the city. I guess that hadn't gone as planned. He was never the type to hold down a typical office job, which my mother had never forgotten to point out during our four years together, but *this*... I never would have imagined he would be doing *this*... hell, he was probably thinking the same thing about me. "Well?" he prompted. He hadn't changed much. Yes, his features were a bit more weathered than I remembered but at least he wasn't a tearful, lonely woman who had just pulled up beside someone on the street with mascara smeared all over the place. "Yes. I've been here for a few months," I tried to sound casual. "Do you care to drive, Jan? Or I can get out if you want." He sounded sincere. "Oh! Yeah..where do you want to go?" I was so clueless. "Uhm... you picked me up, you're in charge here." God, why did he always have this effect on me. I felt like a teenager trying to get into a club for the first time. I unstuck my sweaty hands from the steering wheel, hoping he couldn't see the greasy mark it left on the leather, and finally shifted the car into drive. The car lurched forward and we started on our way...

I bought this product hoping it would be more slippery than KY SILK lube, but it’s actually much stickier and tackier. If you’re looking to use it with toys, you’ll be very disappointed. Nick was walking down 5th Avenue minding his own business when an elderly gentleman came up to him and asked Nick for the time. "Listen,"the elderly man said,"If you don't go into that diner and order a burger, fries, and a chocolate milkshake, the world will be destroyed!"Nick looked at the man incredulously and said,"No thanks, I'm not hungry." Over further protests, Nick briskly walked down the street and passed the diner. That's when Nick looked up to see a meteor hit the heart of the city; the blast devoured the city and Nick, and eventually, human civilization. Nick was walking down 5th Avenue minding his own business..."Whoa,"Nick remarked to himself."That had been some crazy daydream."An elderly gentleman approached Nick and asked for the time. Then said,"If you don't go into that diner and order a burger, fries, and a chocolate milkshake, the world will be destroyed!"Nick paused for a moment and said,"No.... thanks."Amidst protests, Nick hurriedly jogged past the diner. After a few moments, he saw the wheezing elderly man running up to him,"You don't understan--"A tremor began."Oh shiii-," the man exclaimed before both Nick and the man fell down a bottomless fissure. The planet shattered into a billion fragments. Nick was walking down..."What the fuck!"Nick tripped over seemingly nothing. An elderly man walked up to him and said,"Hey are you alright?"Nick gasped as he recognized the man's face."Listen,"the man said,"If you don't go into that diner and order a burger, fries, and a chocolate milkshake, the world will be destroyed!"Nick said,"Fuck off! I'm having a rough--" A tsunami loomed over the city skyline. Nick immediately began sprinting the other direction, and began asphyxiating on a colorless, toxic gas. After about 10 more recursions, Nick angrily walked into the diner and ordered a burger, fries, and milkshake. "What kind of milkshake would you like, sir?"the waitress asked."We have a special flavor today, mint chocolate chip."Nick loved mint."I'll get that." The world ended. After this latest blunder, Nick realized he could play out any scenario he wanted. One time, he stole the elderly man's wallet before walking into the diner. Another time, he smacked the waitresses' ass after ordering. He found that that if he ordered the right items, but then sent it back, the world again, ended. Finally, after about 100 recursions, he became bored and ordered correctly. After his meal, his check came. The cost of the lunch was$113 not including tip. "Hey! There's a mistake on this check!"Nick complained. The waitress came over and said,"Sir, there's no mistake. It cost$1 every time you destroyed the world. I also expect a big tip for that one time you touched me inappropriately." Nick stared blankly, finally shrugged, then paid$150 before leaving the diner and going about his day.

The door opened, letting in humid, jasmine-scented air. The girl looked up from her table, her pale blue eyes wide as she saw the newcomer. An old man came in slowly, leaning on a carved cane, gripping it tightly. He took off his grey cap, which matched his tweed coat, and walked past the girl to a booth where a white-haired grandmother greeted him with a big smile. The girl looked back down at her scratched Formica table, tracing patterns in the condensation from her glass of iced tea. Her light brown curls fell over her young, pale face with bright red lips and blue eyes. She quietly hummed a tune as she drew shapes in the moisture. The ice in her drink had melted as she waited alone. Gradually, she drew a face across the table. The image faded as she worked, the wet lines disappearing into the table’s surface. It was a man’s face, with a low brow and almond-shaped eyes, a mouth tilted in a sly grin. She bit her tongue, working quickly and confidently as the picture vanished almost as soon as she finished it.

The door opened again with a jingle. The girl looked up and nodded at the woman who entered, who looked like an older version of herself, with the same brown curls and pale blue eyes. As the woman approached, the girl wiped away her drawing with her palm, erasing her work. The woman reached out for the girl's hand and picked up her small pink bag. "I'm waiting, he has to be coming," the girl said, her voice breaking. The woman shook her head sadly. She reached out again, and the girl took her hand. The girl stood beside her, eyes shining with unshed tears. They walked to the door together, supporting each other, and stepped out into the heat and scent of jasmine.

Psychomotor agitation involves purposeless motion that usually stems from mental tension of the individual. This could include pacing about a room in a specific path, the wringing of one's hands, pulling off clothing and putting it back on and similar actions.
"Motor agitation is rarer than motor retardation and is often occurs in the elderly. Over activity in this sense does not mean mania. The agitated state in major depressive disorder should not be confused with the manic episode that occurs in bipolar disorder, when mood is temporarily elevated by a transient sense of hope and elation. Psychomotor activities are the physical gestures that result from mental processes and are a product of the psyche. Many psychomotor behaviors associated with mental disorder affect impulses, cravings, instincts, and wishes."
Symptoms of psychomotor agitation include:
- Incoherent conversation
- Expansive gesturing
- Pacing and hair twirling

Cameroon Activists Speak Out Against Breast Ironing Among Young Girls

In Cameroon, the breast—one of the most visible symbols of femininity—is often targeted for ritualistic mutilation. Breast ironing is a practice in which an older female relative flattens a young girl’s developing breasts using heated stones, pestles, spatulas, coconut shells, or similar objects. 

According to Friends of the United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA), one in four girls in Cameroon has experienced breast ironing, affecting nearly 4 million young women. The practice is most prevalent in the Christian and Animist southern regions of Cameroon and is less common in the Muslim north, where only about 10 percent of women are affected. Breast ironing also occurs in other African countries, including Guinea-Bissau, Chad, Togo, Benin, and Guinea. 

Read more about this on TheGrio.com.

Joshua played his violin from high in the rafters. I had never heard this song before. The melodies he invented were usually bright and energetic, but this piece was mournful, heavy. He dragged his bow slowly over the thick strings, and I could feel every vibration in my bones as I watched him from below.

The cub had meant so much more to him than I realized. I’d told my brother he was being foolish—reminded him that we could barely feed our own livestock and that mountain lions were our enemies. “And what will we do when it grows up?” I’d demanded as I stuffed the trembling thing into a burlap sack. “Set it free? Let it lead the others to our farm so they can kill our sheep?” I told him he’d understand when he was older, told him Papa had left me in charge of the farm, and that I couldn’t risk its safety. “Joshua, you can put this cub back where you found it, or I will drown it in the river. It’s your choice.”

He walked for a long time with the cub, carrying it far from our fields, back to the cave where he’d first heard it mew. Hours later, he returned, climbed into the rafters, and began to play that mournful song. The music grew louder as he played, the bow-strikes full of anger, a violence trembling through every note. And then it stopped. It ended in a single, lonesome tone—and then, silently, Joshua fell from the rafters. The violin landed beside him, two broken bodies on the barn floor. Two broken bodies I could not bring myself to move.

Okay, I'm a writer. I want to write a story about what it's really like to write. So if I were a writer, and I am, and I were writing a story, I would want my protagonist, that is to say, HIS protagonist, to be of a noble sort. "Okay, let's flesh this guy out. What is it to be noble? What makes this guy worthy of that description? He is...what. I guess he's kind of a schlub. Maybe he's between jobs, working hard to maintain his optimism. Things keep getting in his way, maybe his car gets towed, his electricity gets shut off, maybe there's even a rabbit that eats all of his flowers. Alright, here he goes." 'Things are starting to get weird. I almost caught the rabbit this morning. I went out to scoop the paper from the front lawn and there she was, wiggling her nose at me with cheeks full of marigold petals. All my research said rabbits don't eat marigolds, but this one seemed to like them just fine.' "Is that true, or did I make that up? NOTE: Google rabbits and marigolds." Rabbits don't typically...will on occasion...if nothing more appetizing can be found. Good enough. 'I took a few cautious steps towards her, staying low and trying to posture myself as unthreatening. She perked up for a flash, sizing me up, then continued her munching. Another step, then another. I lowered my arms, slow as molasses, inching my open hands towards her. When I was nearly on top of her, she bolted, gone in an instant.' "What can this rabbit symbolize for Joe Schlub? I guess it could sort of work as a recurring theme, a sort of physical manifestation of his fruitless quest for work. Although, the rabbit is actively taking something from him, but not in a malicious sense. It's just doing what a rabbit does. Could it potentially be an allegory for his anxiety? A living embodiment of his skittish, elusive notions of contentment? Maybe it's just a rabbit." Seems like kind of a reach. I guess it works, but does it need to be there? What is this writer I've made up trying to write? Is it a slow-burning, losing-your-grip kind of story with this rabbit playing the catalyst, or is he looking for a noble doofus? If his protagonist is noble, what would he have done if he had caught the rabbit? Are we dealing with a George and Lenny kind of story? Where does all this fit into the plot? What's the point? I think maybe I'm spending too much time talking about him spending too much time thinking about the rabbit. Time to break for lunch.

A cargo ship seized by Somali pirates off the coast of Kenya was freed Monday after one day under siege, and the German-based Beluga Shipping company said its crew of 16 was unharmed. The pirates were thwarted after the crew of the MV Beluga Fortune locked themselves in a panic room and switched off the main engine, cut off the fuel supply, blocked the bridge and reported the Indian Ocean attack to military forces, said Niels Stolberg, president and CEO of Beluga Shipping GmbH. The pirates, seeking a million-dollar ransom, were unable to maintain control of the vessel, and naval forces were able to come to the rescue, Stolberg said in a statement. The vessel continued its journey to Richards Bay, South Africa. "The excellent behavior of our colleagues on board made such a swift and happy ending of the capture possible," Stolberg said.

By . Martin Robinson . A new ban on cheap alcohol is already failing because high-strength booze is still being sold for less than a bottle of water, experts said today. Since Monday retailers have been unable to sell lager or cider . for less than 50p a can after ministers promised a new minimum pricing regime to end the ‘worst examples of harmful drink’. Shops in Blackpool, Britain's worst town for alcohol-related illnesses and deaths, are selling cans of 7.5 per cent strength cider for 89p, while bottled water on their shelves is the same price or more. Row: Omega strong cider is being sold for 89p a can at Buy Low bargains in Blackpool, right, . Although this 89p price is above the new threshold, shocked health officials have said that David Cameron should already re-think his policy to save lives. An investigation has found that at least . three shops in the seaside resort sold high-strength cans of cider or lager for . less or the same price as a bottle of water. Buy Low Bargains, close to the pier, sells Omega Cider for 89p. Its . manager Sam Meak defended the policy and said: 'They are alcoholics so . they don’t mind if they buy it cheap or whatever, they would buy the . cider if it was £20'. The seaside resort is Britain's poorest and most deprived in terms of income, employment and health problems. Alcohol . is widely blamed and in the past two years 2,914 people were admitted . to hospital with alcohol-related illnesses and 140 died - the highest . figures by population in Britain. The town's Victoria Hospital says it has seen an increase in the number of people being rushed there in alcohol-induced comas or liver failure. NHS Blackpool's alcohol lead Steve Morton said: 'Minimum prices . would have minimal effect on the average person – something like 12p a . week – but it would deter young vulnerable people from buying cheap, . strong alcohol. 'High strength, low cost alcohol tends to be . drunk by people with an alcohol problem and younger people who want to . get as much alcohol as cheaply as possible because they have limited . means. 'Minimum prices need to come in through legislation, but . in the mean time responsible traders should be looking at stopping . selling strong alcohol. The industry needs to take some . responsibility – a dead customer is no customer at all'. Admissions: By population more people are taken Blackpool's Victoria Hospital with alcohol-related illnesses than anywhere else in Britain . Blackpool's public health chief, Dr Arif Rajpura, has called on David Cameron to rethink minimum pricing to save lives. Under his original plans a can of lager would have retailed for £1.56, wine for £4.22 and spirits for £12.60. But the Prime Minister faced strong opposition from within the Cabinet, as ministers warned it could unfairly hit responsible drinkers on lower incomes. So the Prime Minister’s proposal – which would have fixed the cost of a unit of alcohol at 45p – was dumped last summer. Instead cans must be at least 50p each, bottles of wine £2.24 and 750ml bottles of vodka or whisky £10.16. The new system works by banning shops and pubs from selling alcohol at less than the total cost of the duty payable to the Treasury, plus VAT. But experts say it will have a minimal effect. On average, each adult is expected to drink just 0.04 per cent less over the course of the year. So-called ‘hazardous drinkers’ – those who consume more than the recommend safe limits – are predicted to consume just 0.1 per cent less. But Dr Sarah Wollaston, MP for Totnes and campaigner on alcohol pricing, said the effect of the new regime would be ‘minuscule’. ‘I just do not think the evidence is there to support it when you are comparing it with the effect of minimum unit pricing,’ she said.

I just wanted the pain to be over. But before I took my last breath, she was the last thing I saw. Her the day she was born. Gwyneth Rose Hopkins, 5 lbs, 2 oz. She was beautiful. She had fair skin but big brown eyes and dark hair. She was frozen in time at age two, and she was perfect. Still is. The day I lost her was a normal one except for two differences. I was taking her to day care instead of my wife, who had to go to work early for a conference call. And she was tired, having not slept well the night before.As I was loading the car, Larry from Accounting called, and the books were all wrong. Blood pressure rising, I chucked the diaper bag in the back of the SUV and sped down the highway to the four-story office I worked at in the suburbs. The car alarm went off twice in the eight hours I was at work. Looking out the office window, I focused my keys on the car and silenced it both times. I was thankful for this, because it was an especially hot June day in Illinois, and I didn't want to go outside. At about 4 p.m. my wife called, panicking because day care had called her and reported Gwynnie had never shown up. Everything went black. Two months later, after being found not guilty of manslaughter, I decided I needed to be punished. So I took 50 ibuprofen and laid on my bed with her baby album on my stomach. Since my wife left me a month before, I figured it would be at least two days before anyone found me. I turned off the air, hoping decomposition would speed things up. Hoping I would be uncomfortable. Hoping I would feel how she felt. I'm a coward, though, so I didn't feel how she felt. I drifted away and came across a bright white screen: Choose one option: New Game, Load Game, Quit Game. Without a doubt, Quit Game. The most surprising part? After the screen went away, there she was.

**Environmental Protection Agency Report: Hydraulic Fracturing and Groundwater Contamination**

**Executive Summary**

Washington, D.C. – In direct contradiction to assertions by the drilling industry that hydraulic fracturing has never resulted in groundwater contamination, the Environmental Protection Agency determined through a comprehensive 1987 study that hydraulic fracturing operations at a natural gas well in West Virginia resulted in contamination of an underground drinking water source. This previously overlooked congressional report, discovered through research conducted by the Environmental Working Group and Earthjustice, documented that fracturing gel from a shale gas well exceeding 4,000 feet in depth had contaminated local well water supplies.

**Findings and Analysis**

EPA investigators determined that the contamination was "illustrative" of systemic pollution issues associated with hydraulic fracturing operations. However, the agency's investigation encountered significant obstacles due to confidentiality agreements established between industry operators and affected property owners. The Environmental Working Group's comprehensive year-long investigation of the incident revealed that multiple abandoned natural gas wells situated in proximity to the fractured well in West Virginia potentially functioned as conduits, facilitating the migration of fracturing gel—a standard component of fracking fluid—into the water supply system.

According to Dusty Horwitt, the Environmental Working Group's senior oil and gas analyst and author of "Cracks in the Façade," the organization's report regarding EPA's findings, "When examining the cumulative evidence—including the presence of gel in the water supply, the existence of abandoned wells, and the documented capacity for drilling fluids to migrate through these wells into underground water supplies—substantial evidence supports EPA's conclusion that this constituted a verified case of hydraulic fracturing-induced groundwater contamination. The responsibility now falls to EPA to continue the investigation initiated twenty-five years ago and determine the comprehensive risks associated with fracking operations to ensure adequate protection of our drinking water resources."

**Historical Context and Current Implications**

Since the publication of the 1987 report, the industry has conducted hydraulic fracturing operations on hundreds of thousands of wells and continues an unprecedented expansion into natural gas-bearing shale formations. These formations, previously considered inaccessible, are located beneath populated areas across multiple states, including West Virginia, New York, Pennsylvania, Ohio, Michigan, Louisiana, and Arkansas.

To access these geological formations, drilling operators frequently employ a relatively recent combination of horizontal drilling techniques and higher-volume fracturing methods. As drilling activities have intensified, reports of environmental pollution have generated considerable national discourse regarding the actual and potential environmental risks, particularly concerning groundwater contamination—the primary drinking water source for more than 100 million Americans, according to the U.S. Geological Survey.

**Regulatory Framework**

In 2005, Congress exempted hydraulic fracturing from the Safe Drinking Water Act following an EPA study of hydraulic fracturing conducted the previous year, which concluded minimal risk to water supplies when fracturing operations are conducted in coal bed methane deposits. Neither Congress nor the EPA referenced the agency's 1987 findings during this exemption process. The EPA is currently conducting a comprehensive study examining fracking's impact on water supplies.

**Technical Assessment**

According to EPA investigators' documentation in the 1987 report, which examined the handling of natural gas, oil, and geothermal wastes generally, "During the fracturing process, fractures can be produced, allowing migration of native brine, fracturing fluid and hydrocarbons from the oil or gas well to a nearby water well. When this occurs, the water well can be permanently damaged and a new well must be drilled or an alternative source of drinking water found."

The Environmental Working Group's analysis determined that the evidence in the West Virginia case was consistent with pollution from hydraulic fracturing operations, though the possibility exists that another stage of the drilling process contributed to the contamination.

**Industry Response**

In EPA files located in Washington, EWG discovered documentation submitted in 1987 by the American Petroleum Institute, the natural gas and oil industry's primary trade association, which appeared to concur with the EPA finding while suggesting that such incidents were not representative of typical operations. "One case," the API stated, referring to the West Virginia contamination incident, "resulted in a workover operation fracturing into groundwater as a result of equipment failure or accident. As described in the detail write-up, this is not a normal result of fracturing as it ruins the productive capability of the wells."

---

**About the Environmental Working Group**

EWG is a nonprofit research organization headquartered in Washington, DC that utilizes information-based advocacy to protect human health and the environment. Additional information is available at http://www.ewg.org

Follow EWG's natural resources research on Twitter @EWGfracking

"Smith!"  
I sprint toward the raised voice echoing through the Operations Control Center. The room—three stories wide, lined with glowing consoles and massive wall screens—feels stifling. Clusters of engineers and scientists crowd every aisle, shouting over each other, juggling satphones, and furiously scribbling data onto tablets. Navigating between the tightly packed desks, I dodge a rolling chair as I approach Dr. Reinhardt, the mission director.

"Yes, sir?" I pant, catching my breath.

He jabs a trembling finger at the largest screen, which displays the latest batch of high-res images from Rosetta-V, our fifth probe to rendezvous with Comet 67P/Churyumov–Gerasimenko. The familiar grayscale landscape flickers: luminous dust streams, jagged boulders, miles of monochrome emptiness.

But in this image, something foreign gleams beneath the comet’s icy crust—a flawless black rectangle, at least twenty meters long, perfectly embedded into the core’s surface. Symbols—too angular and purposeful to be natural fractures—are etched along its sides.

"Is... is that a rectangle?" I stammer.

"Don't screw around with me, Smith. Of course it's a rectangle, but what the hell is it doing here?" Reinhardt snaps.

I swallow, suddenly aware that my qualifications feel woefully inadequate. "I—I don’t know, sir. I only run mathematical analysis. I don’t—"

I freeze mid-sentence. My eyes lock on the strange markings—arranged in repeating clusters, spaced in a grid. At first, they're just random scratches. Then it clicks: they form a binary pattern. Once noticed, the logic is unmistakable.

"Sir, could I get an uncompressed image of the object?"

He shoots me a look—equal parts suspicion and relief. "That’s why you’re here, Smith. You’re the least useless analyst on staff. We need answers."

A technician hands me three glossy printouts. I scan over them, overlaying our computational models in my mind. Ever since the original Rosetta mission in 2014, we’ve sent probes to cometary bodies—harvesting volatiles for fusion fuel and studying proto-solar material. Never, in all five comet landings, have we seen anything remotely artificial.

As I work, mounting dread twists in my stomach. The binary isn't just a code—it’s a cartographic cipher, matching the coordinate map we transmitted on Voyager’s golden record over two centuries ago. Latitude, longitude, pulsar calibrations, all eerily familiar.

Something found our message in deep space. And now, something is sending an answer.  
Something is coming.

This is an original Uvicon detector from the inventory of Project Celescope, a battery of four 12-inch Cassegrain telescopes developed at the Smithsonian Astrophysical Observatory in Cambridge, Massachusetts in the 1960s and used on the OAO II satellite to conduct the first comprehensive survey of the entire ultraviolet sky. Uvicon detectors were based upon standard Westinghouse "Vidicon" technology - cathode ray tube television cameras of that era operating in the 1050-3200 Angstrom wavelength range. The detector itself is the smaller back end of the glass tube measuring approximately 6 inches in length. The larger front section is an imaging system that converts ultraviolet photons into photoelectrons via a cesium-telluride photocathode and electrostatically focuses them onto the Vidicon target which produced electrical signals that could be telemetered to NASA's Goddard Space Flight Center for analysis. Celescope was launched on December 7, 1968 aboard an Atlas-Centaur rocket as part of the payload for the second Orbiting Astronomical Observatory (OAO II), which operated successfully for four years until 1972. The data from Celescope's 13,000 observations resulted in the Celescope Catalog of Ultraviolet Magnitudes containing photometric data for over 5,000 stars brighter than 9th magnitude in four UV bands. Transferred to NASM from SAO in 1973 and currently stored at the Paul E. Garber Preservation, Restoration, and Storage Facility in Suitland, Maryland.
Transferred from the Smithsonian Astrophysical Observatory

Concerning the Binding Energy and Number of Neutrons in Nuclides Spanning the 50-to-82 Neutron Shell
The binding energies are known for about three thousand nuclides. When these values are used to construct profiles of binding energy versus the number of neutrons for various elements, they display some interesting characteristics. First, the profiles are parabolic, with binding energy increasing at a decreasing rate as neutrons are added. More interesting are the incremental increases, which display a downward, almost linear, trend with fluctuations associated with the formation of neutron pairs. At particular numbers, the incremental values decrease sharply, and the magnitude of the fluctuations due to pair formation changes. For example, the profile of incremental increases for bromine is displayed.
The break in the relationship occurs when the number of neutrons reaches 50. Fifty is one of the so-called magic numbers of nuclear structure. For more on magic numbers in nuclear structure, see Magic Numbers 0, Magic Numbers I, and Magic Numbers II.
The relationship between incremental binding energy ΔB and the number of additional neutrons n can be approximated by a function of the form
where if Z is the number of protons and N the number of neutrons for a nuclide, then n = N-Z. The variable 'u,' representing neutron pair formation, is zero if N is odd and unity if N is even. The regression coefficient c0 is called the intercept, and c1 is the slope.
There are indications that these regression parameters may reveal information about the structure of nuclei. For example, the magnitude of the slope may be inversely proportional to the radius of the shell that is being filled. For many elements, there are two regression lines. For example, for the case of bromine shown above, there is one regression for the data below the break and another for the data above the break.
Given below are the regression parameter estimates c1 for some of the elements with data spanning the 50-to-82 neutron shell. It is notable that for elements close in atomic number Z, the parameters are close in value.
|Regression Equation Slope Parameters for Incremental Binding Energy Increases and Excess Neutrons in Nuclides (50-to-82 Neutron Shell)|
The data in the table demonstrates the systematic variation in the regression parameters with the elements' atomic number (number of protons). The graph below illustrates the relationship between the magnitude of the slope and the proton number for elements reflecting the filling of the 50-to-82 neutron shell.
For elements below molybdenum in atomic number, the relationship to atomic number (number of protons) is irregular; however, above molybdenum, it is quite regular.
The parameter of interest is the amount by which incremental binding energy is enhanced by neutron pair formation. While the graph below shows irregularities below molybdenum, it displays reasonable regularity above molybdenum, albeit with an odd-even alternation.

Mushrooms are known for their nutritional value and culinary versatility. Now, there’s a new reason to buy these fabulous fungi: They may help prevent breast cancer.
Shiuan Chen, PhD, a professor of tumor cell biology at the City of Hope in Duarte, Calif., was among the first scientists to study how white button mushrooms offer protective benefits.
“Diet is a key consideration for prevention because it is something that everyone can control,” he says. “Our research shows that women may benefit from a balanced diet that includes about 3.5 ounces of mushrooms per day.”
Initial lab studies found that ingredients in mushrooms suppress the effects of aromatase, a natural enzyme that helps synthesize the hormone estrogen. This is a critical finding for women’s health, as 75% of postmenopausal women with breast cancer have tumors that rely on estrogen to grow.
City of Hope clinical trials are now under way to further test the benefits of white button mushrooms. As for other mushrooms, you can count on them to deliver vitamins D and B, as well as powerful antioxidants like selenium.
Mushrooms are also low in sodium and calories, fat- and cholesterol-free, and can be an effective substitute for meat.
In fact, studies have demonstrated that substituting 4 ounces of mushrooms for 4 ounces of meat once a week, for one year, could save you more than 18,000 calories and nearly 3,000 grams of fat. In the average woman, this translates to a loss of more than 5 pounds.

I think that Frost creates a real sense of tragedy in the family's reaction to the death of the boy in "Out, Out—." The child's death is tragic, but it is not tragedy in the classical sense—it is more of a chance, sad occurrence caused by a momentary lapse in attention when the boy looks up at the mention of supper. The reaction of the family takes time to unfold, and there is a certain tragic reflection of how "life goes on" with little else to gain from prolonged mourning. The business of working on the Vermont farm continues relentlessly—hay to be cut, wood to be split, money to be made, and endless rural toil that cannot pause even for grief.

The tragedy deepens when we consider how the industrial "buzz saw" that literally killed the boy also symbolically seems to be grinding away the boy's memory. The mechanized nature of farm work, represented by the aggressive, snarling saw that "leaped out at the boy's hand," reflects a world where human life becomes secondary to productivity. Even the saw itself seems almost predatory, described as having "teeth" and making sounds that suggest a living, dangerous creature.

The poem's abrupt ending—"No more to build on there. And they, since they / Were not the one dead, turned to their affairs"—mirrors the family's swift emotional retreat. There is little to indicate why the family has chosen to adopt such a seemingly callous perspective regarding the boy's death, beyond the harsh economic realities of rural life in early 20th-century New England. The reality is that the boy's death becomes something the family looks beyond after a brief period, suggesting that even in memory, his life holds diminishing value compared to the pressing demands of survival and work.

The boy himself seems to understand this tragic dynamic when he desperately pleads "Don't let him cut my hand off— / The doctor, when he comes. Don't let him doctor!" His frantic concern about losing his hand reveals his intuitive understanding that his worth to the family is tied directly to his ability to work. When he realizes his usefulness is ending, he essentially foreshadows his own expendability. The tragic irony is that a child who should be protected and valued for simply being a child is instead measured by his capacity for labor. His death from shock—"Little—less—nothing!—and that ended it"—suggests that once his productive potential is gone, his very life force ebbs away, as if he has internalized his own lack of worth in a world that values work above all else.

Heartburn and Your Heart
Heartburn didn’t get its name by accident. Although the problem stems from excess stomach acid rather than heart-related issues, it provokes searing pain focused directly behind the breastbone. Of the over eight million emergency room visits for chest pain each year, severe heartburn, also known as gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), accounts for over half the cases in which actual heart problems are ruled out.
The advent of a class of drugs called proton-pump inhibitors (PPIs) has been a godsend for people plagued by acid reflux. According to the FDA, about one in 14 people in the United States currently takes or has taken a PPI drug.
“PPIs are far more effective for suppressing stomach acid production than earlier classes of drugs. For people who are subject to bleeding in their intestinal tract, they can also lower that risk,” says Dr. Michelle O’Donoghue, cardiovascular specialist at Harvard-affiliated Brigham and Women’s Hospital.
Perils of PPIs?
But no drug is completely safe. Proton-pump inhibitors may cause allergic reactions (such as a rash) or diarrhea. Although the data are not conclusive, some studies have linked their use to osteoporosis (weakening of the bones) and an increased risk of pneumonia among people admitted to the hospital. Some reports have suggested that PPIs may interfere with the effectiveness of the heart drug clopidogrel (Plavix).
Now, there’s a new concern. Research published in the online journal PLOS ONE in June reports that the PPIs may increase the risk of a heart attack. However, Dr. O’Donoghue says these results should be viewed with caution. Observational studies like this one can’t account for every possible factor that might skew the results, she says. For example, some of the people in the study who were taking PPIs to treat acid reflux may have been doing so incorrectly because they were actually experiencing symptoms of heart disease. Also, people who were being treated with PPIs may have been suffering from other illnesses that may have increased their risk of heart attack. “At this point, there is no clear evidence that PPIs increase a person’s risk of heart disease, and I wouldn’t advise people to stop taking PPIs because of worries about heart attack risk alone,” says Dr. O’Donoghue. “Nonetheless, it’s a good idea to periodically reevaluate with your doctor any medication you use.”
If you have mild GERD with no other complicating medical conditions, you may be able to cut back on your PPI use. But check with your doctor first. You may need to continue on the drug if you regularly take aspirin, blood thinners, or non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (see above story) or if you have a condition such as stomach ulcers or Barrett’s esophagus. Lifestyle changes such as losing weight, sleeping with the head of your bed slightly elevated, and avoiding eating before bed can all help calm stomach acid.
